revision.text:
'''Minamoto no Yoriie''' ([[1182]]-[[1204]]) was the second [[shogun]] of the [[Kamakura shogunate]]. Eldest son of the founder of the Kamakura shogunate [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], his mother was [[Hojo Masako]].
After his father's death in [[1199]], Yoriie became head of the [[Minamoto]] clan and was appointed ''Seii Taishogun'' in [[1202]]. By this time however, the power of the shogun and [[shogunate]] had already fallen into the hands of his grandfather [[Hojo Tokimasa]]. Yoriie in turn plotted to subjugate the [[Hojo]] but failed and was put under house arrest and eventually assasinated in 1204.
Minamoto no Yoriie was succeeded by his younger brother [[Minamoto no Sanetomo]] as third shogun of the Kamakura shogunate.

After his father's death in [[1199]], Yoriie became head of the [[Minamoto]] clan and was appointed ''Seii Taishogun'' in [[1202]]. By this time however, the power of the shogun and [[shogunate]] had already fallen into the hands of his grandfather [[Hojo Tokimasa]]. Yoriie in turn plotted to subjugate the [[Hojo]] but failed and was put under house arrest and eventually assasinated in 1204.
Minamoto no Yoriie was succeeded by his younger brother [[Minamoto no Sanetomo]] as third shogun of the Kamakura shogunate.

Eldest son of the founder of the Kamakura shogunate [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], his mother was [[Hōjō Masako]].
After his father's death in [[1199]], Yoriie became head of the [[Minamoto]] clan and was appointed [[Shogun|Seii Taishogun]] in [[1202]]. By this time however, the power of the shogun and [[shogunate]] had already fallen into the hands of his grandfather [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. Yoriie in turn plotted to subjugate the [[Hōjō clan]] but failed and was put under house arrest and eventually [[assassin]]ated in 1204.
Minamoto no Yoriie was succeeded by his younger brother [[Minamoto no Sanetomo]] as third shogun of the Kamakura shogunate.
{{start box}} {{succession box | title=[[Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura Shogun]] | before=[[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] | after=[[Minamoto no Sanetomo]] | years=1202-1203}} {{end box}}

By this time however, the power of the shogun and [[shogunate]] had already fallen into the hands of his grandfather [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. Yoriie in turn plotted to subjugate the [[Hōjō clan]] but failed and was put under house arrest and eventually [[assassin]]ated in 1204.
Minamoto no Yoriie was succeeded by his younger brother [[Minamoto no Sanetomo]] as third shogun of the Kamakura shogunate.

Eldest son of the founder of the Kamakura shogunate [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], his mother was [[Hōjō Masako]]. After his father's death in [[1199]], Yoriie became head of the [[Minamoto]] clan and was appointed [[Shogun|Seii Taishogun]] in [[1202]]. By this time however, the power of the shogun and [[shogunate]] had already fallen into the hands of his grandfather [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. Yoriie in turn plotted to subjugate the [[Hōjō clan]] but failed and was put under house arrest and eventually [[assassin]]ated in [[July 17]], [[1204]] . Minamoto no Yoriie was succeeded by his younger brother [[Minamoto no Sanetomo]] as third shogun of the Kamakura shogunate. ==Era of Yoriie's ''bakufu''== The years in which Yoriie was shogan are encompassed within only one [[Japanese era names| era name]] or ''[[nengō]]''. * ''[[Kennin]]'' (1201-1204) ==References== ===Notes=== {{reflist}} ===Further reading=== * [[Isaac Titsingh |Titsingh]], Isaac, ed. After his father's death in 1199, Yoriie became head of the [[Minamoto]] clan and was appointed [[Shogun|Seii Taishogun]] in 1202. By this time however, the power of the shogun and [[shogunate]] had already fallen into the hands of his grandfather [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. Yoriie in turn plotted to subjugate the [[Hōjō clan]] but failed and was put under house arrest and eventually [[assassin]]ated on [[July 17]], [[1204]] . Minamoto no Yoriie was succeeded by his younger brother [[Minamoto no Sanetomo]] as third shogun of the Kamakura shogunate. ==Era of Yoriie's ''bakufu''== The years in which Yoriie was shogan are encompassed within only one [[Japanese era names| era name]] or ''[[nengō]]''. * ''[[Kennin]]'' (1201-1204) ==References== ===Notes=== {{reflist}} ===Further reading=== * [[Isaac Titsingh |Titsingh]], Isaac, ed. [[Image:Minamoto no Yoriies grave 2007-02-25.jpg|thumb|right|250px|Minamoto no Yoriie's grave in Shuzenji, [[Izu, Shizuoka|Izu City]]]]'''Minamoto no Yoriie''' 源頼家 ([[September 11]], [[1182]] – [[August 14]], [[1204]]) was the second [[shogun]] (1202 – 1203) of the [[Kamakura shogunate]] of [[Japan]]. Eldest son of the founder of the Kamakura shogunate [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], his mother was [[Hōjō Masako]]. Raised at the residence of [[Hiki Yoshikazu]] like his son [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]], he had as wet nurses among others the wives of Hiki himself and of [[Kajiwara Kagetoki]]. After his father's death in 1199, Yoriie became head of the [[Minamoto]] clan and was appointed [[Shogun|Seii Taishogun]] in 1202. By this time however, the power of the shogun and the [[shogunate]] had already fallen into the hands of his grandfather [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. Yoriie in turn plotted to subjugate the [[Hōjō clan]] but failed and was put under house arrest and eventually [[assassin]]ated on [[July 17]], [[1204]]. Minamoto no Yoriie was succeeded by his younger brother [[Minamoto no Sanetomo]] as third shogun of the Kamakura shogunate. The years in which Yoriie was shogun are encompassed within only one [[Japanese era names| era name]] or ''[[nengō]]'': ''[[Kennin]]'' (1201-1204) ==References== * {{cite book | last = Yasuda | first = Motohisa (editor) | coauthors = | title = Kamakura, Muromachi Jinmei Jiten | publisher = Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha | date = | location = Tokyo | language = | isbn = 978-4-404-01757-4 | author = | oclc = 24654085 }} ===Further reading=== * [[Isaac Titsingh |Titsingh]], Isaac, ed. Eldest son of the founder of the Kamakura shogunate [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], his mother was [[Hōjō Masako]]. Raised at the residence of [[Hiki Yoshikazu]] like his son [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]], he had as wet nurses among others the wives of Hiki himself and of [[Kajiwara Kagetoki]]. After his father's death in 1199, Yoriie became head of the [[Minamoto]] clan and was appointed [[Shogun|Seii Taishogun]] in 1202. By this time however, the power of the shogun and the [[shogunate]] had already fallen into the hands of his grandfather [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. Yoriie in turn plotted to subjugate the [[Hōjō clan]] but failed, was put under house arrest, forced to abdicate and eventually [[assassin]]ated on [[July 17]], [[1204]] in {{nihongo|[[Izu, Shizuoka|Shuzenji]]|修善寺}} in Izu. Born at [[Hiki Yoshikazu]]'s residence in Kamakura, he had as wet nurses, among others, the wives of Hiki himself and of [[Kajiwara Kagetoki]]. After his father's death in 1199, Yoriie became head of the [[Minamoto]] clan and was appointed [[Shogun|Seii Taishogun]] in 1202. Yoriie in turn plotted to subjugate the [[Hōjō clan]] but failed, was put under house arrest, forced to abdicate and eventually [[assassin]]ated by the Hōjō in {{nihongo|[[Izu, Shizuoka|Shuzenji]]|修善寺}} in Izu. Minamoto no Yoriie was succeeded by his younger brother [[Minamoto no Sanetomo]] as third shogun of the Kamakura shogunate. [[Image:Minamoto no Yoriies grave 2007-02-25.jpg|thumb|right|250px|Minamoto no Yoriie's grave in Shuzenji, [[Izu, Shizuoka|Izu City]]]]{{nihongo|'''Minamoto no Yoriie'''|源頼家}} ([[September 11]], [[1182]] – [[August 14]], [[1204]]) was the second [[shogun]] (1202 – 1203) of the [[Kamakura shogunate]] of [[Japan]]. Eldest son of the founder of the Kamakura shogunate [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], his mother was [[Hōjō Masako]]. Born at [[Hiki Yoshikazu]]'s residence in Kamakura, he had as wet nurses, among others, the wives of Hiki himself and of [[Kajiwara Kagetoki]]. After his father's death in 1199, Yoriie became head of the [[Minamoto]] clan and was appointed [[Shogun|Seii Taishogun]] in 1202. By this time however, the power of the shogun and the [[shogunate]] had already fallen into the hands of his grandfather [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. Yoriie in turn plotted to subjugate the [[Hōjō clan]] but failed, was put under house arrest, forced to abdicate and eventually [[assassin]]ated by the Hōjō in {{nihongo|[[Izu, Shizuoka|Shuzenji]]|修善寺}} in Izu. Minamoto no Yoriie was succeeded by his younger brother [[Minamoto no Sanetomo]] as third shogun of the Kamakura shogunate. [[Image:Minamoto no Yoriies grave 2007-02-25.jpg|thumb|right|250px|Minamoto no Yoriie's grave in Shuzenji, [[Izu, Shizuoka|Izu City]]]]{{nihongo|'''Minamoto no Yoriie'''|源頼家}} ([[September 11]], [[1182]] – [[August 14]], [[1204]]) was the second [[shogun]] (1202 – 1203) of the [[Kamakura shogunate]] of [[Japan]]. Eldest son of the founder of the Kamakura shogunate [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], his mother was [[Hōjō Masako]]. Born at [[Hiki Yoshikazu]]'s residence in Kamakura, he had as wet nurses, among others, the wives of Hiki himself and of [[Kajiwara Kagetoki]]. After his father's death in 1199, Yoriie became head of the [[Minamoto]] clan and was appointed [[Shogun|Seii Taishogun]] in 1202. By this time however, the power of the shogun and the [[shogunate]] had already fallen into the hands of his grandfather [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. His mother explicitly prohibited him any political activity. Yoriie in turn plotted to subjugate the [[Hōjō clan]] but failed, was put under house arrest, forced to abdicate and eventually [[assassin]]ated by the Hōjō in {{nihongo|[[Izu, Shizuoka|Shuzenji]]|修善寺}} in Izu. Minamoto no Yoriie was succeeded by his younger brother [[Minamoto no Sanetomo]] as third shogun of the Kamakura shogunate. His sons died of a violent death. The eldest, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]], died as a child, victim of political intrigue. The last to die was [[Kugyō (Minamoto no Yoshinari)|Kugyō]], who was forced to became a Buddhist priest and in 1219 at age 20 assassinated his uncle Sanetomo. Kugyō was himself immediately executed for his crime, thus bringing the [[Seiwa Genji]] dynasty to a sudden end. The years in which Yoriie was shogun are encompassed within only one [[Japanese era names|era name]] or ''[[nengō]]'': ''[[Kennin]]'' (1201-1204) ==References== * {{cite book | last = Yasuda | first = Motohisa (editor) | coauthors = | title = Kamakura, Muromachi Jinmei Jiten | publisher = Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha | date = | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-404-01757-4 | author = | oclc = 24654085 }} ===Further reading=== * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh]], Isaac, ed. (1834). [Siyun-sai Rin-siyo, 1652], ''[[Nipon o daï itsi ran]]; ou, Annales des empereurs du Japon, tr. par M. Isaac Titsingh avec l'aide de plusieurs interprètes attachés au comptoir hollandais de Nangasaki; ouvrage re., complété et cor. sur l'original japonais-chinois, accompagné de notes et précédé d'un Aperçu d'histoire mythologique du Japon, par M. J. [[Julius Klaproth|Klaproth]].'' Paris: Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. [http://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran --''Two digitized examples of this rare book have now been made available online: (1) from the library of the University of Michigan, digitized January 30, 2007; and (2) from the library of Stanford University, digitized June 23, 2006.'' [[Image:Minamoto no Yoriies grave 2007-02-25.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Minamoto no Yoriie's grave in Shuzenji, [[Izu, Shizuoka|Izu City]]]] [[File:Minamoto no Yoriie.jpg|thumb|200px|Minamoto no Yoriie]] {{nihongo|'''Minamoto no Yoriie'''|源頼家}} (September 11, 1182 – August 14, 1204) was the second [[shogun]] (1202 – 1203) of the [[Kamakura shogunate]] of [[Japan]]. Eldest son of the founder of the Kamakura shogunate [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], his mother was [[Hōjō Masako]]. Born at [[Hiki Yoshikazu]]'s residence in Kamakura, he had as wet nurses, among others, the wives of Hiki himself and of [[Kajiwara Kagetoki]]. After his father's death in 1199, Yoriie became head of the [[Minamoto]] clan and was appointed [[Shogun|Seii Taishogun]] in 1202. By this time however, the power of the shogun and the [[shogunate]] had already fallen into the hands of his grandfather [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. His mother explicitly prohibited him any political activity. Yoriie in turn plotted to subjugate the [[Hōjō clan]] but failed, was put under house arrest, forced to abdicate and eventually [[assassination|assassinated]] by the Hōjō in {{nihongo|[[Izu, Shizuoka|Shuzenji]]|修善寺}} in Izu. Minamoto no Yoriie was succeeded by his younger brother [[Minamoto no Sanetomo]] as third shogun of the Kamakura shogunate. His sons died of a violent death. The eldest, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]], died as a child, victim of political intrigue. The last to die was [[Kugyō (Minamoto no Yoshinari)|Kugyō]], who was forced to became a Buddhist priest and in 1219 at age 20 assassinated his uncle Sanetomo. Kugyō was himself immediately executed for his crime, thus bringing the [[Seiwa Genji]] dynasty to a sudden end. The years in which Yoriie was shogun are encompassed within only one [[Japanese era names|era name]] or ''[[nengō]]'': ''[[Kennin]]'' (1201-1204) ==References== * {{cite book | last = Yasuda | first = Motohisa (editor) | coauthors = | title = Kamakura, Muromachi Jinmei Jiten | publisher = Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha | date = | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-404-01757-4 | author = | oclc = 24654085 }} ===Further reading=== * {{fr icon}} [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh]], Isaac, ed. (1834). [Siyun-sai Rin-siyo/[[Hayashi Gahō]], 1652], ''[[Nipon o daï itsi ran]]; ou, [http://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran Annales des empereurs du Japon.]'' Paris: [[Royal Asiatic Society|Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland]]. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/300555357?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 300555357] {{start box}} {{succession box | title=[[Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura Shogun]]:
Minamoto no Yoriie | before=[[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] | after=[[Minamoto no Sanetomo]] | years=1202-1203}} {{end box}}

[[Image:Minamoto no Yoriies grave 2007-02-25.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Minamoto no Yoriie's grave in Shuzenji, [[Izu, Shizuoka|Izu City]]]] [[File:Minamoto no Yoriie.jpg|thumb|200px|Minamoto no Yoriie]] {{nihongo|'''Minamoto no Yoriie'''|源頼家}} (September 11, 1182 – August 14, 1204) was the second [[shogun]] (1202–1203) of the [[Kamakura shogunate]] of [[Japan]]. Eldest son of the founder of the Kamakura shogunate [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], his mother was [[Hōjō Masako]]. Born at [[Hiki Yoshikazu]]'s residence in Kamakura, he had as wet nurses, among others, the wives of Hiki himself and of [[Kajiwara Kagetoki]]. After his father's death in 1199, Yoriie became head of the [[Minamoto]] clan and was appointed [[Shogun|Seii Taishogun]] in 1202. By this time however, the power of the shogun and the [[shogunate]] had already fallen into the hands of his grandfather [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. His mother explicitly prohibited him any political activity. Yoriie in turn plotted to subjugate the [[Hōjō clan]] but failed, was put under house arrest, forced to abdicate and eventually [[assassination|assassinated]] by the Hōjō in {{nihongo|[[Izu, Shizuoka|Shuzenji]]|修善寺}} in Izu. Minamoto no Yoriie was succeeded by his younger brother [[Minamoto no Sanetomo]] as third shogun of the Kamakura shogunate. His sons died of a violent death. The eldest, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]], died as a child, victim of political intrigue. The last to die was [[Kugyō (Minamoto no Yoshinari)|Kugyō]], who was forced to became a Buddhist priest and in 1219 at age 20 assassinated his uncle Sanetomo. Kugyō was himself immediately executed for his crime, thus bringing the [[Seiwa Genji]] dynasty to a sudden end. The years in which Yoriie was shogun are encompassed within only one [[Japanese era names|era name]] or ''[[nengō]]'': ''[[Kennin]]'' (1201–1204) ==References== * {{cite book | last = Yasuda | first = Motohisa (editor) | coauthors = | title = Kamakura, Muromachi Jinmei Jiten | publisher = Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha | date = | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-404-01757-4 | author = | oclc = 24654085 }} ===Further reading=== * {{fr icon}} [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh]], Isaac, ed. (1834). [Siyun-sai Rin-siyo/[[Hayashi Gahō]], 1652], ''[[Nipon o daï itsi ran]]; ou, [http://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran Annales des empereurs du Japon.]'' Paris: [[Royal Asiatic Society|Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland]]. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/300555357?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 300555357] {{start box}} {{succession box | title=[[Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura Shogun]]:
Minamoto no Yoriie | before=[[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] | after=[[Minamoto no Sanetomo]] | years=1202–1203}} {{end box}}

[[Image:Minamoto no Yoriies grave 2007-02-25.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Minamoto no Yoriie's grave in Shuzenji, [[Izu, Shizuoka|Izu]]]]{{nihongo|'''Minamoto no Yoriie'''|源頼家}} ([[September 11]], [[1182]] – [[August 14]], [[1204]]) was the second [[shogun]] (1202–1203) of [[Japan]]'s [[Kamakura shogunate]], and the first son of first shogun [[Minamoto no Yoritomo|Yoritomo]]. His short rule was purely nominal, and he died in Shuzenji, a small town in today's [[Izu province]], assassinated by his uncle [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. The years in which Yoriie was shogun are encompassed within only one [[Japanese era names|era name]] or ''[[nengō]]'': ''[[Kennin]]'' (1201–1204) == Life == [[File:Minamoto no Yoriie.jpg|thumb|left||Minamoto no Yoriie]]Born from Tokimasa's daughter [[Hōjō Masako]] at [[Hiki Yoshikazu]]'s residence in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], Yoriie had as wet nurses the wives of powerful men like Hiki himself, [[Kajiwara Kagetoki]], and Yoshikazu's younger sister.Yasuda (1990:592-593 Before he was born, his father Yoritomo had [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] and his men carry stones to build the [[Tsurugaoka_Hachiman-gū#Wakamiya_.C5.8Cji|Dankazura]] on [[Wakamiya Ōji]] to pray for the child's safe delivery. When Yoriie later himself had a heir, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]], the child was also born at the Hiki mansionThe mansion no longer exists and its location ({{coord|35|19|1.31|N|139|33|20.95|E|region:JP_type:landmark_scale:1500|display=inline}}) is occupied by Myōhon-ji, a temple dedicated to the memory of the Hiki clan, exterminated by the [[Hōjō clan]]. from Hiki's daughter Wakasa no Tsubone, a fact which further consolidating an already strong emotional bondKamiya Vol. 1 (2008:44-45) From this relationship Hiki gained considerable influence when Yoriie became shogun, raising the hostility of [[Hōjō Tokimasa]], who was instead close to Yoriie's younger brother Senman (future third shogun [[Minamoto no Sanetomo|Sanetomo]]), and was in his turn trying to leverage that relationship for political advantage. Yoriie showed when still very young great interest in military arts like fencing, and horse-riding.Papinot (2972:385_386) After his father's death in 1199, the 17-year-old became head of the [[Minamoto]] clan and was appointed [[Shogun|''seii taishogun'']] in 1202. He was however criticized for his abandonment of his father's policies, and his mother forbade him to do any political activity.. On June 30, 1333 (([[Shōji]] 1, 12th day of the 4th month) his remaining powers were formally taken from him and assumed by a council of 13 elders headed by his grandfather [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. Yoriie in turn plotted with the Hiki to subjugate the Hōjō clan but failed, was put under house arrest, forced to abdicate and eventually assassinated on July 17, 1204 in Izu. . Yoriie was succeeded by his younger brother Sanetomo, the last of the [[Seiwa Genji]] line to rule, at least nominally, over Kamakura. == Hiki Yoshikazu's rebellion (比企能員の乱 Hiki Yoshikazu no Ran)== {{main|Hiki Yoshikazu}} Hiki's daughter was married to [[Minamoto no Yoriie]], the second shogun of the [[Kamakura shogunate]]. Seriously ill, Yoriie proposed to name both his younger brother [[Minamoto no Sanetomo|Sanetomo]], and his young son (Hiki's grandson) [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] to succeed him; the two would split power, governing separate parts of the country.According to Japanese Wikipedia's "源一幡" (Minamoto no Ichiman) article, the [[Gukanshō]] and the [[Azuma Kagami]] give different versions of the events. This is the Azuma Kagami's version. According to the Gukanshō, when in 1203 Yoriie became seriously ill, the Hōjō clan supported his younger brother Senman (future third shogun Minamoto no Sanetomo) as a successor, while the the Hiki supported son Ichiman. To avoid power falling into the hands of the Hiki, the Hōjō decided to get rid of the Hiki and their protege. This is the version of events described in the article [[Minamoto no Ichiman]].
According to the same Wikipedia article, the Azuma Kagami claims Ichiman was killed by the Hiki, and not by the Hōjō. It seemed natural that Hiki would then be the regent, even if unofficially, of young Ichiman. Thus, he suggested to Yoriie, who would be assassinated shortly afterwards by a separate faction (the [[Hōjō clan]]), that they arrange to have Sanetomo killed. [[Hōjō Masako]], Yoriie's mother and wife of the first shogun Yoritomo, overheard this conversation. Hōjō warriors assassinated Hiki Yoshikazu, and then attacked the palace of young Ichiman, setting a fire and killing both the young heir and a great number of members of the extended Hiki family. When in 1203 Yoriie became seriously ill, the [[Hōjō clan]] supported his brother Senman (future third shogun [[Minamoto no Sanetomo]]) as a future successor, while the the Hiki supported son IchimanAccording to Japanese Wikipedia's "源一幡" (Minamoto no Ichiman) article (see link below), the [[Gukanshō]] and the [[Azuma Kagami]] disagree on the reasons for the clash between clans. This is the Gukanshō's version of events. According to the Azuma Kagami, Yoriie wanted to split the shogunate between his brother and his son, then Hiki proposed him the murder of Sanetomo, but the conversation was overheard by Hōjō Masako. This is also the version of events described in the article [[Hiki Yoshikazu]].
According to the same Japanese Wikipedia article, the Azuma Kagami claims Ichiman was killed by the Hiki, and not by the Hōjō.. To avoid power falling into the hands of the Hiki, the Hōjō decided to get rid of the Hiki and their protege.Kamakura Citizen's Net On a pretext, [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] invited Hiki Yoshikazu to his home and assassinated him. A battle between the clans ensued, the Hiki were defeated by a coalition of the Hōjō, [[Wada clan|Wada]], [[Miura clan|Miura]] and [[Hatakeyama clan]]s and exterminated. == The three sons of Yoriie== Yoriie had three sons, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]], [[Kugyō (Minamoto no Yoshinari)|Kugyō]], and Senju-maru, all of whom died of a violent death, victims of the power struggle that followed Yoritomo's sudden death. Ichiman（1198-1203) was the eldest. His mother Wakasa no Tsubone was Hiki Yoshikazu's daughter, and the child was brought up by the Hiki clan and he died in the fire that destroyed the Hiki residence . Second son Yoshinari, the only one of the three to reach adulthood, was forced to become a bonze and in 1219 murdered his uncle Sanetomo on the stone stairs at Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū in the shogunal capital of Kamakura, an act for which he was himself slain on the same dayKusumoto (2002: 70-73). Third son {{nihongo|Senju-maru|千寿丸|(1201-1214)}} was 12 when Izumi Chikahira rebelled against the Hōjō to make the child shogun. After Chikahira's defeat, the child was forced to become a bonze like his older brother Yoshinari. A year later [[Wada Yoshimori]] also rebelled but, like Chikahira, was defeated and Senju-maru died with the others of the Wada clan. ==Notes== ==References== {{Reflist}} ==Bibliography== * [http://www.kcn-net.org/e_kama_history/omachi/omachi_1.htm Ōmachi], by the Kamakura Citizen's Net, accessed on September 30, 2008 * {{cite book | last = Kusumoto | first = Katsuji | coauthors = | title = Kamakura Naruhodo Jiten | publisher = Jitsugyō no Nihonsha | date = July 2002 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | id = ISBN 978-4-408-00779-3 }} * Papinot, E. (1910). "Historical and Geographical Dictionary of Japan." 1972 Printing. Charles E. Tuttle Company, Tokyo, ISBN 0-8048-0996-8. * {{cite book | last = Yasuda | first = Motohisa (editor) | coauthors = | title = Kamakura, Muromachi Jinmei Jiten | publisher = Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha | date = 1990 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-404-01757-4 | author = | oclc = 24654085 }} * {{cite book | last = Kamiya | first = Michinori | coauthors = | title = Fukaku Aruku - Kamakura Shiseki Sansaku Vol. 1 & 2 | publisher = Kamakura Shunshūsha | date = 2008 | location = Kamakura | language = Japanese | isbn = 4774003409 | oclc = 169992721 }} ===Further reading=== * {{fr icon}} [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh]], Isaac, ed. (1834). [Siyun-sai Rin-siyo/[[Hayashi Gahō]], 1652], ''[[Nipon o daï itsi ran]]; ou, [http://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran Annales des empereurs du Japon.]'' Paris: [[Royal Asiatic Society|Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland]]. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/300555357?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 300555357] {{start box}} {{succession box | title=[[Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura Shogun]]:
Minamoto no Yoriie | before=[[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] | after=[[Minamoto no Sanetomo]] | years=1202–1203}} {{end box}}

(1834). [Siyun-sai Rin-siyo/[[Hayashi Gahō]], 1652], ''[[Nipon o daï itsi ran]]; ou, [http://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran Annales des empereurs du Japon.]'' Paris: [[Royal Asiatic Society|Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland]]. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/300555357?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 300555357] {{start box}} {{succession box | title=[[Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura Shogun]]:
Minamoto no Yoriie | before=[[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] | after=[[Minamoto no Sanetomo]] | years=1202–1203}} {{end box}}
[[Category:1182 births]] [[Category:1204 deaths]] [[Category:Kamakura shoguns]] [[Category:Minamoto clan|Yoriie]] [[Category:People of Heian period Japan]] [[Category:People of Kamakura period Japan]] {{Japan-hist-stub}} {{Shogun-stub}} [[ca:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[de:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[es:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[eu:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[fr:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[ko:미나모토 요리이에]] [[it:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[nl:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[ja:源頼家]] [[pl:Yori'ie Minamoto]] [[ru:Минамото-но Ёрииэ]] [[sv:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[zh:源賴家]]

[[Image:Minamoto no Yoriies grave 2007-02-25.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Minamoto no Yoriie's grave in Shuzenji, [[Izu, Shizuoka|Izu]]]]{{nihongo|'''Minamoto no Yoriie'''|源頼家}} ([[September 11]], [[1182]] – [[August 14]], [[1204]]) was the second [[shogun]] (1202–1203) of [[Japan]]'s [[Kamakura shogunate]], and the first son of first shogun [[Minamoto no Yoritomo|Yoritomo]]. His short rule was purely nominal, and he died in Shuzenji, a small town in today's [[Izu province]], assassinated by his uncle [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. The years in which Yoriie was shogun are encompassed within only one [[Japanese era names|era name]] or ''[[nengō]]'': ''[[Kennin]]'' (1201–1204) == Life == [[File:Minamoto no Yoriie.jpg|thumb|left||Minamoto no Yoriie]]Born from Tokimasa's daughter [[Hōjō Masako]] at [[Hiki Yoshikazu]]'s residence in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], Yoriie had as wet nurses the wives of powerful men like Hiki himself, [[Kajiwara Kagetoki]], and Yoshikazu's younger sister.Yasuda (1990:592-593 Before he was born, his father Yoritomo had [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] and his men carry stones to build the [[Tsurugaoka_Hachiman-gū#Wakamiya_.C5.8Cji|Dankazura]] on [[Wakamiya Ōji]] to pray for the child's safe delivery. When Yoriie later himself had a heir, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]], the child was also born at the Hiki mansionThe mansion no longer exists and its location ({{coord|35|19|1.31|N|139|33|20.95|E|region:JP_type:landmark_scale:1500|display=inline}}) is occupied by Myōhon-ji, a temple dedicated to the memory of the Hiki clan, exterminated by the [[Hōjō clan]]. from Hiki's daughter Wakasa no Tsubone, a fact which further consolidating an already strong emotional bondKamiya Vol. 1 (2008:44-45) From this relationship Hiki gained considerable influence when Yoriie became shogun, raising the hostility of [[Hōjō Tokimasa]], who was instead close to Yoriie's younger brother Senman (future third shogun [[Minamoto no Sanetomo|Sanetomo]]), and was in his turn trying to leverage that relationship for political advantage. Yoriie showed when still very young great interest in military arts like fencing, and horse-riding.Papinot (2972:385_386) After his father's death in 1199, the 17-year-old became head of the [[Minamoto]] clan and was appointed [[Shogun|''seii taishogun'']] in 1202. He was however criticized for his abandonment of his father's policies, and his mother forbade him to do any political activity.. On June 30, 1333 (([[Shōji]] 1, 12th day of the 4th month) his remaining powers were formally taken from him and assumed by a council of 13 elders headed by his grandfather [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. Yoriie in turn plotted with the Hiki to subjugate the Hōjō clan but failed, was put under house arrest, forced to abdicate and eventually assassinated on July 17, 1204 in Izu. . Yoriie was succeeded by his younger brother Sanetomo, the last of the [[Seiwa Genji]] line to rule, at least nominally, over Kamakura. == Hiki Yoshikazu's rebellion (比企能員の乱 Hiki Yoshikazu no Ran)== {{main|Hiki Yoshikazu}} Hiki's daughter was married to [[Minamoto no Yoriie]], the second shogun of the [[Kamakura shogunate]]. Seriously ill, Yoriie proposed to name both his younger brother [[Minamoto no Sanetomo|Sanetomo]], and his young son (Hiki's grandson) [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] to succeed him; the two would split power, governing separate parts of the country.According to Japanese Wikipedia's "源一幡" (Minamoto no Ichiman) article, the [[Gukanshō]] and the [[Azuma Kagami]] give different versions of the events. This is the Azuma Kagami's version. According to the Gukanshō, when in 1203 Yoriie became seriously ill, the Hōjō clan supported his younger brother Senman (future third shogun Minamoto no Sanetomo) as a successor, while the the Hiki supported son Ichiman. To avoid power falling into the hands of the Hiki, the Hōjō decided to get rid of the Hiki and their protege. This is the version of events described in the article [[Minamoto no Ichiman]].
According to the same Wikipedia article, the Azuma Kagami claims Ichiman was killed by the Hiki, and not by the Hōjō. It seemed natural that Hiki would then be the regent, even if unofficially, of young Ichiman. Thus, he suggested to Yoriie, who would be assassinated shortly afterwards by a separate faction (the [[Hōjō clan]]), that they arrange to have Sanetomo killed. [[Hōjō Masako]], Yoriie's mother and wife of the first shogun Yoritomo, overheard this conversation. Hōjō warriors assassinated Hiki Yoshikazu, and then attacked the palace of young Ichiman, setting a fire and killing both the young heir and a great number of members of the extended Hiki family. When in 1203 Yoriie became seriously ill, the [[Hōjō clan]] supported his brother Senman (future third shogun [[Minamoto no Sanetomo]]) as a future successor, while the the Hiki supported son IchimanAccording to Japanese Wikipedia's "源一幡" (Minamoto no Ichiman) article (see link below), the [[Gukanshō]] and the [[Azuma Kagami]] disagree on the reasons for the clash between clans. This is the Gukanshō's version of events. According to the Azuma Kagami, Yoriie wanted to split the shogunate between his brother and his son, then Hiki proposed him the murder of Sanetomo, but the conversation was overheard by Hōjō Masako. This is also the version of events described in the article [[Hiki Yoshikazu]].
According to the same Japanese Wikipedia article, the Azuma Kagami claims Ichiman was killed by the Hiki, and not by the Hōjō.. To avoid power falling into the hands of the Hiki, the Hōjō decided to get rid of the Hiki and their protege.Kamakura Citizen's Net On a pretext, [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] invited Hiki Yoshikazu to his home and assassinated him. A battle between the clans ensued, the Hiki were defeated by a coalition of the Hōjō, [[Wada clan|Wada]], [[Miura clan|Miura]] and [[Hatakeyama clan]]s and exterminated. == Three sons of Yoriie== Yoriie had three sons, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]], [[Kugyō (Minamoto no Yoshinari)|Kugyō]], and Senju-maru, all of whom died of a violent death, victims of the power struggle that followed Yoritomo's sudden death. Ichiman（1198-1203) was the eldest. His mother Wakasa no Tsubone was Hiki Yoshikazu's daughter, and the child was brought up by the Hiki clan and he died in the fire that destroyed the Hiki residence . Second son Yoshinari, the only one of the three to reach adulthood, was forced to become a bonze and in 1219 murdered his uncle Sanetomo on the stone stairs at Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū in the shogunal capital of Kamakura, an act for which he was himself slain on the same dayKusumoto (2002: 70-73). Third son {{nihongo|Senju-maru|千寿丸|(1201-1214)}} was 12 when Izumi Chikahira rebelled against the Hōjō to make the child shogun. After Chikahira's defeat, the child was forced to become a bonze like his older brother Yoshinari. A year later [[Wada Yoshimori]] also rebelled but, like Chikahira, was defeated and Senju-maru died with the others of the Wada clan. ==Notes== ==References== {{Reflist}} ==Bibliography== * [http://www.kcn-net.org/e_kama_history/omachi/omachi_1.htm Ōmachi], by the Kamakura Citizen's Net, accessed on September 30, 2008 * {{cite book | last = Kusumoto | first = Katsuji | coauthors = | title = Kamakura Naruhodo Jiten | publisher = Jitsugyō no Nihonsha | date = July 2002 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | id = ISBN 978-4-408-00779-3 }} * Papinot, E. (1910). "Historical and Geographical Dictionary of Japan." 1972 Printing. Charles E. Tuttle Company, Tokyo, ISBN 0-8048-0996-8. * {{cite book | last = Yasuda | first = Motohisa (editor) | coauthors = | title = Kamakura, Muromachi Jinmei Jiten | publisher = Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha | date = 1990 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-404-01757-4 | author = | oclc = 24654085 }} * {{cite book | last = Kamiya | first = Michinori | coauthors = | title = Fukaku Aruku - Kamakura Shiseki Sansaku Vol. 1 & 2 | publisher = Kamakura Shunshūsha | date = 2008 | location = Kamakura | language = Japanese | isbn = 4774003409 | oclc = 169992721 }} ===Further reading=== * {{fr icon}} [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh]], Isaac, ed. (1834). [Siyun-sai Rin-siyo/[[Hayashi Gahō]], 1652], ''[[Nipon o daï itsi ran]]; ou, [http://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran Annales des empereurs du Japon.]'' Paris: [[Royal Asiatic Society|Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland]]. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/300555357?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 300555357] {{start box}} {{succession box | title=[[Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura Shogun]]:

[[Category:1182 births]] [[Category:1204 deaths]] [[Category:Kamakura shoguns]] [[Category:Minamoto clan|Yoriie]] [[Category:People of Heian period Japan]] [[Category:People of Kamakura period Japan]] [[ca:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[de:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[es:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[eu:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[fr:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[ko:미나모토 요리이에]] [[it:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[nl:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[ja:源頼家]] [[pl:Yori'ie Minamoto]] [[ru:Минамото-но Ёрииэ]] [[sv:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[zh:源賴家]]

[[Image:Minamoto no Yoriies grave 2007-02-25.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Minamoto no Yoriie's grave in Shuzenji, [[Izu, Shizuoka|Izu]]]]{{nihongo|'''Minamoto no Yoriie'''|源頼家}} ([[September 11]], [[1182]] – [[August 14]], [[1204]]) was the second [[shogun]] (1202–1203) of [[Japan]]'s [[Kamakura shogunate]], and the first son of first shogun [[Minamoto no Yoritomo|Yoritomo]]. His short rule was purely nominal, and he died in Shuzenji, a small town in today's [[Izu province]], assassinated by his uncle [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. The years in which Yoriie was shogun are encompassed within only one [[Japanese era names|era name]] or ''[[nengō]]'': ''[[Kennin]]'' (1201–1204) == Life == [[File:Minamoto no Yoriie.jpg|thumb|left||Minamoto no Yoriie]]Born from Tokimasa's daughter [[Hōjō Masako]] at [[Hiki Yoshikazu]]'s residence in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], Yoriie had as wet nurses the wives of powerful men like Hiki himself, [[Kajiwara Kagetoki]], and Yoshikazu's younger sister.Yasuda (1990:592-593 Before he was born, his father Yoritomo had [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] and his men carry stones to build the [[Tsurugaoka_Hachiman-gū#Wakamiya_.C5.8Cji|Dankazura]] on [[Wakamiya Ōji]] to pray for the child's safe delivery. When Yoriie later himself had a heir, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]], the child was also born at the Hiki mansionThe mansion no longer exists and its location ({{coord|35|19|1.31|N|139|33|20.95|E|region:JP_type:landmark_scale:1500|display=inline}}) is occupied by Myōhon-ji, a temple dedicated to the memory of the Hiki clan, exterminated by the [[Hōjō clan]]. from Hiki's daughter Wakasa no Tsubone, a fact which further consolidating an already strong emotional bondKamiya Vol. 1 (2008:44-45) From this relationship Hiki gained considerable influence when Yoriie became shogun, raising the hostility of [[Hōjō Tokimasa]], who was instead close to Yoriie's younger brother Senman (future third shogun [[Minamoto no Sanetomo|Sanetomo]]), and was in his turn trying to leverage that relationship for political advantage. Yoriie showed when still very young great interest in military arts like fencing, and horse-riding.Papinot (2972:385_386) After his father's death in 1199, the 17-year-old became head of the [[Minamoto]] clan and was appointed [[Shogun|''seii taishogun'']] in 1202. He was however criticized for his abandonment of his father's policies, and his mother forbade him to do any political activity.. On June 30, 1333 (([[Shōji]] 1, 12th day of the 4th month) his remaining powers were formally taken from him and assumed by a council of 13 elders headed by his grandfather [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. Yoriie in turn plotted with the Hiki to subjugate the Hōjō clan but failed, was put under house arrest, forced to abdicate and eventually assassinated on July 17, 1204 in Izu. . Yoriie was succeeded by his younger brother Sanetomo, the last of the [[Seiwa Genji]] line to rule, at least nominally, over Kamakura. == Hiki Yoshikazu's rebellion== {{main|Hiki Yoshikazu}} Seriously ill, Yoriie proposed to name both his younger brother [[Minamoto no Sanetomo|Sanetomo]], and his young son (Hiki's grandson) [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] to succeed him; the two would split power, governing separate parts of the country.According to Japanese Wikipedia's "源一幡" (Minamoto no Ichiman) article, the [[Gukanshō]] and the [[Azuma Kagami]] give different versions of the events. This is the Azuma Kagami's version. According to the Gukanshō, when in 1203 Yoriie became seriously ill, the Hōjō clan supported his younger brother Senman (future third shogun Minamoto no Sanetomo) as a successor, while the the Hiki supported son Ichiman. To avoid power falling into the hands of the Hiki, the Hōjō decided to get rid of the Hiki and their protege. This is the version of events described in the article [[Minamoto no Ichiman]].
According to the same Wikipedia article, the Azuma Kagami claims Ichiman was killed by the Hiki, and not by the Hōjō. It seemed natural that Hiki would then be the regent, even if unofficially, of young Ichiman. He suggested to Yoriie, who would be assassinated shortly afterwards by a separate faction (the [[Hōjō clan]]), that they arrange to have Sanetomo killed. [[Hōjō Masako]], Yoriie's mother and wife of the first shogun Yoritomo, allegedly overheard the conversation. On a pretext, [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] invited Hiki Yoshikazu to his home and assassinated him. A battle between the clans ensued, the Hiki were defeated by a coalition of the Hōjō, [[Wada clan|Wada]], [[Miura clan|Miura]] and [[Hatakeyama clan]]s and exterminated.Kamakura Citizen's Net == Three sons of Yoriie== Yoriie had three sons, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]], [[Kugyō (Minamoto no Yoshinari)|Kugyō]], and Senju-maru, all of whom died of a violent death, victims of the power struggle that followed Yoritomo's sudden death. Ichiman（1198-1203) was the eldest. His mother Wakasa no Tsubone was Hiki Yoshikazu's daughter, and the child was brought up by the Hiki clan and he died in the fire that destroyed the Hiki residence . Second son Yoshinari, the only one of the three to reach adulthood, was forced to become a bonze and in 1219 murdered his uncle Sanetomo on the stone stairs at Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū in the shogunal capital of Kamakura, an act for which he was himself slain on the same dayKusumoto (2002: 70-73). Third son {{nihongo|Senju-maru|千寿丸|(1201-1214)}} was 12 when Izumi Chikahira rebelled against the Hōjō to make the child shogun. After Chikahira's defeat, the child was forced to become a bonze like his older brother Yoshinari. A year later [[Wada Yoshimori]] also rebelled but, like Chikahira, was defeated and Senju-maru died with the others of the Wada clan. ==Notes== ==References== {{Reflist}} ==Bibliography== * [http://www.kcn-net.org/e_kama_history/omachi/omachi_1.htm Ōmachi], by the Kamakura Citizen's Net, accessed on September 30, 2008 * {{cite book | last = Kusumoto | first = Katsuji | coauthors = | title = Kamakura Naruhodo Jiten | publisher = Jitsugyō no Nihonsha | date = July 2002 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | id = ISBN 978-4-408-00779-3 }} * Papinot, E. (1910). "Historical and Geographical Dictionary of Japan." 1972 Printing. Charles E. Tuttle Company, Tokyo, ISBN 0-8048-0996-8. * {{cite book | last = Yasuda | first = Motohisa (editor) | coauthors = | title = Kamakura, Muromachi Jinmei Jiten | publisher = Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha | date = 1990 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-404-01757-4 | author = | oclc = 24654085 }} * {{cite book | last = Kamiya | first = Michinori | coauthors = | title = Fukaku Aruku - Kamakura Shiseki Sansaku Vol. 1 & 2 | publisher = Kamakura Shunshūsha | date = 2008 | location = Kamakura | language = Japanese | isbn = 4774003409 | oclc = 169992721 }} ===Further reading=== * {{fr icon}} [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh]], Isaac, ed. (1834). [Siyun-sai Rin-siyo/[[Hayashi Gahō]], 1652], ''[[Nipon o daï itsi ran]]; ou, [http://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran Annales des empereurs du Japon.]'' Paris: [[Royal Asiatic Society|Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland]]. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/300555357?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 300555357]

[[Image:Minamoto no Yoriies grave 2007-02-25.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Minamoto no Yoriie's grave in Shuzenji, [[Izu, Shizuoka|Izu]]]]{{nihongo|'''Minamoto no Yoriie'''|源頼家}} ([[September 11]], [[1182]] – [[August 14]], [[1204]]) was the second [[shogun]] (1202–1203) of [[Japan]]'s [[Kamakura shogunate]], and the first son of first shogun [[Minamoto no Yoritomo|Yoritomo]]. His short rule was purely nominal, and he died in Shuzenji, a small town in today's [[Izu province]], assassinated by his uncle [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. The years in which Yoriie was shogun are encompassed within only one [[Japanese era names|era name]] or ''[[nengō]]'': ''[[Kennin]]'' (1201–1204) == Life == [[File:Minamoto no Yoriie.jpg|thumb|left||Minamoto no Yoriie]]Born from Tokimasa's daughter [[Hōjō Masako]] at [[Hiki Yoshikazu]]'s residence in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], Yoriie had as wet nurses the wives of powerful men like Hiki himself, [[Kajiwara Kagetoki]], and Yoshikazu's younger sister.Yasuda (1990:592-593 Before he was born, his father Yoritomo had [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] and his men carry stones to build the [[Tsurugaoka_Hachiman-gū#Wakamiya_.C5.8Cji|Dankazura]] on [[Wakamiya Ōji]] to pray for the child's safe delivery. When Yoriie later himself had a heir, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]], the child was also born at the Hiki mansionThe mansion no longer exists and its location ({{coord|35|19|1.31|N|139|33|20.95|E|region:JP_type:landmark_scale:1500|display=inline}}) is occupied by Myōhon-ji, a temple dedicated to the memory of the Hiki clan, exterminated by the [[Hōjō clan]]. from Hiki's daughter Wakasa no Tsubone, a fact which further consolidating an already strong emotional bondKamiya Vol. 1 (2008:44-45) From this relationship Hiki gained considerable influence when Yoriie became shogun, raising the hostility of [[Hōjō Tokimasa]], who was instead close to Yoriie's younger brother Senman (future third shogun [[Minamoto no Sanetomo|Sanetomo]]), and was in his turn trying to leverage that relationship for political advantage. Yoriie showed when still very young great interest in military arts like fencing, and horse-riding.Papinot (2972:385_386) After his father's death in 1199, the 17-year-old became head of the [[Minamoto]] clan and was appointed [[Shogun|''seii taishogun'']] in 1202. He was however criticized for his abandonment of his father's policies, and his mother forbade him to do any political activity.. On June 30, 1333 (([[Shōji]] 1, 12th day of the 4th month) his remaining powers were formally taken from him and assumed by a council of 13 elders headed by his grandfather [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. Yoriie in turn plotted with the Hiki to subjugate the Hōjō clan but failed, was put under house arrest, forced to abdicate and eventually assassinated on July 17, 1204 in Izu. . Yoriie was succeeded by his younger brother Sanetomo, the last of the [[Seiwa Genji]] line to rule, at least nominally, over Kamakura. == Hiki Yoshikazu's rebellion (比企能員の乱 Hiki Yoshikazu no Ran)== {{main|Hiki Yoshikazu}} Hiki's daughter was married to [[Minamoto no Yoriie]], the second shogun of the [[Kamakura shogunate]]. Seriously ill, Yoriie proposed to name both his younger brother [[Minamoto no Sanetomo|Sanetomo]], and his young son (Hiki's grandson) [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] to succeed him; the two would split power, governing separate parts of the country.According to Japanese Wikipedia's "源一幡" (Minamoto no Ichiman) article, the [[Gukanshō]] and the [[Azuma Kagami]] give different versions of the events. This is the Azuma Kagami's version. According to the Gukanshō, when in 1203 Yoriie became seriously ill, the Hōjō clan supported his younger brother Senman (future third shogun Minamoto no Sanetomo) as a successor, while the the Hiki supported son Ichiman. To avoid power falling into the hands of the Hiki, the Hōjō decided to get rid of the Hiki and their protege. This is the version of events described in the article [[Minamoto no Ichiman]].
According to the same Wikipedia article, the Azuma Kagami claims Ichiman was killed by the Hiki, and not by the Hōjō. It seemed natural that Hiki would then be the regent, even if unofficially, of young Ichiman. Thus, he suggested to Yoriie, who would be assassinated shortly afterwards by a separate faction (the [[Hōjō clan]]), that they arrange to have Sanetomo killed. [[Hōjō Masako]], Yoriie's mother and wife of the first shogun Yoritomo, overheard this conversation. Hōjō warriors assassinated Hiki Yoshikazu, and then attacked the palace of young Ichiman, setting a fire and killing both the young heir and a great number of members of the extended Hiki family. When in 1203 Yoriie became seriously ill, the [[Hōjō clan]] supported his brother Senman (future third shogun [[Minamoto no Sanetomo]]) as a future successor, while the the Hiki supported son IchimanAccording to Japanese Wikipedia's "源一幡" (Minamoto no Ichiman) article (see link below), the [[Gukanshō]] and the [[Azuma Kagami]] disagree on the reasons for the clash between clans. This is the Gukanshō's version of events. According to the Azuma Kagami, Yoriie wanted to split the shogunate between his brother and his son, then Hiki proposed him the murder of Sanetomo, but the conversation was overheard by Hōjō Masako. This is also the version of events described in the article [[Hiki Yoshikazu]].
According to the same Japanese Wikipedia article, the Azuma Kagami claims Ichiman was killed by the Hiki, and not by the Hōjō.. To avoid power falling into the hands of the Hiki, the Hōjō decided to get rid of the Hiki and their protege.Kamakura Citizen's Net On a pretext, [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] invited Hiki Yoshikazu to his home and assassinated him. A battle between the clans ensued, the Hiki were defeated by a coalition of the Hōjō, [[Wada clan|Wada]], [[Miura clan|Miura]] and [[Hatakeyama clan]]s and exterminated. == Three sons of Yoriie== Yoriie had three sons, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]], [[Kugyō (Minamoto no Yoshinari)|Kugyō]], and Senju-maru, all of whom died of a violent death, victims of the power struggle that followed Yoritomo's sudden death. Ichiman（1198-1203) was the eldest. His mother Wakasa no Tsubone was Hiki Yoshikazu's daughter, and the child was brought up by the Hiki clan and he died in the fire that destroyed the Hiki residence . Second son Yoshinari, the only one of the three to reach adulthood, was forced to become a bonze and in 1219 murdered his uncle Sanetomo on the stone stairs at Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū in the shogunal capital of Kamakura, an act for which he was himself slain on the same dayKusumoto (2002: 70-73). Third son {{nihongo|Senju-maru|千寿丸|(1201-1214)}} was 12 when Izumi Chikahira rebelled against the Hōjō to make the child shogun. After Chikahira's defeat, the child was forced to become a bonze like his older brother Yoshinari. A year later [[Wada Yoshimori]] also rebelled but, like Chikahira, was defeated and Senju-maru died with the others of the Wada clan. ==Notes== ==References== {{Reflist}} ==Bibliography== * [http://www.kcn-net.org/e_kama_history/omachi/omachi_1.htm Ōmachi], by the Kamakura Citizen's Net, accessed on September 30, 2008 * {{cite book | last = Kusumoto | first = Katsuji | coauthors = | title = Kamakura Naruhodo Jiten | publisher = Jitsugyō no Nihonsha | date = July 2002 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | id = ISBN 978-4-408-00779-3 }} * Papinot, E. (1910). "Historical and Geographical Dictionary of Japan." 1972 Printing. Charles E. Tuttle Company, Tokyo, ISBN 0-8048-0996-8. * {{cite book | last = Yasuda | first = Motohisa (editor) | coauthors = | title = Kamakura, Muromachi Jinmei Jiten | publisher = Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha | date = 1990 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-404-01757-4 | author = | oclc = 24654085 }} * {{cite book | last = Kamiya | first = Michinori | coauthors = | title = Fukaku Aruku - Kamakura Shiseki Sansaku Vol. 1 & 2 | publisher = Kamakura Shunshūsha | date = 2008 | location = Kamakura | language = Japanese | isbn = 4774003409 | oclc = 169992721 }} ===Further reading=== * {{fr icon}} [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh]], Isaac, ed. (1834). [Siyun-sai Rin-siyo/[[Hayashi Gahō]], 1652], ''[[Nipon o daï itsi ran]]; ou, [http://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran Annales des empereurs du Japon.]'' Paris: [[Royal Asiatic Society|Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland]]. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/300555357?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 300555357] {{start box}} {{succession box | title=[[Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura Shogun]]:
Minamoto no Yoriie | before=[[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] | after=[[Minamoto no Sanetomo]] | years=1202–1203}} {{end box}}

revision.comment: /* Hiki Yoshikazu's rebellion (比企能員の乱 Hiki Yoshikazu no Ran) */ Typo
revision.text:
[[Image:Minamoto no Yoriies grave 2007-02-25.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Minamoto no Yoriie's grave in Shuzenji, [[Izu, Shizuoka|Izu]]]]{{nihongo|'''Minamoto no Yoriie'''|源頼家}} ([[September 11]], [[1182]] – [[August 14]], [[1204]]) was the second [[shogun]] (1202–1203) of [[Japan]]'s [[Kamakura shogunate]], and the first son of first shogun [[Minamoto no Yoritomo|Yoritomo]]. His short rule was purely nominal, and he died in Shuzenji, a small town in today's [[Izu province]], assassinated by his uncle [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. The years in which Yoriie was shogun are encompassed within only one [[Japanese era names|era name]] or ''[[nengō]]'': ''[[Kennin]]'' (1201–1204) == Life == [[File:Minamoto no Yoriie.jpg|thumb|left||Minamoto no Yoriie]]Born from Tokimasa's daughter [[Hōjō Masako]] at [[Hiki Yoshikazu]]'s residence in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], Yoriie had as wet nurses the wives of powerful men like Hiki himself, [[Kajiwara Kagetoki]], and Yoshikazu's younger sister.Yasuda (1990:592-593 Before he was born, his father Yoritomo had [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] and his men carry stones to build the [[Tsurugaoka_Hachiman-gū#Wakamiya_.C5.8Cji|Dankazura]] on [[Wakamiya Ōji]] to pray for the child's safe delivery. When Yoriie later himself had a heir, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]], the child was also born at the Hiki mansionThe mansion no longer exists and its location ({{coord|35|19|1.31|N|139|33|20.95|E|region:JP_type:landmark_scale:1500|display=inline}}) is occupied by Myōhon-ji, a temple dedicated to the memory of the Hiki clan, exterminated by the [[Hōjō clan]]. from Hiki's daughter Wakasa no Tsubone, a fact which further consolidating an already strong emotional bondKamiya Vol. 1 (2008:44-45) From this relationship Hiki gained considerable influence when Yoriie became shogun, raising the hostility of [[Hōjō Tokimasa]], who was instead close to Yoriie's younger brother Senman (future third shogun [[Minamoto no Sanetomo|Sanetomo]]), and was in his turn trying to leverage that relationship for political advantage. Yoriie showed when still very young great interest in military arts like fencing, and horse-riding.Papinot (2972:385_386) After his father's death in 1199, the 17-year-old became head of the [[Minamoto]] clan and was appointed [[Shogun|''seii taishogun'']] in 1202. He was however criticized for his abandonment of his father's policies, and his mother forbade him to do any political activity.. On June 30, 1333 (([[Shōji]] 1, 12th day of the 4th month) his remaining powers were formally taken from him and assumed by a council of 13 elders headed by his grandfather [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. Yoriie in turn plotted with the Hiki to subjugate the Hōjō clan but failed, was put under house arrest, forced to abdicate and eventually assassinated on July 17, 1204 in Izu. . Yoriie was succeeded by his younger brother Sanetomo, the last of the [[Seiwa Genji]] line to rule, at least nominally, over Kamakura. == Hiki Yoshikazu's rebellion (比企能員の乱 Hiki Yoshikazu no Ran)== {{main|Hiki Yoshikazu}} Hiki's daughter was married to [[Minamoto no Yoriie]], the second shogun of the [[Kamakura shogunate]]. Seriously ill, Yoriie proposed to name both his younger brother [[Minamoto no Sanetomo|Sanetomo]], and his young son (Hiki's grandson) [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] to succeed him; the two would split power, governing separate parts of the country.According to Japanese Wikipedia's "源一幡" (Minamoto no Ichiman) article, the [[Gukanshō]] and the [[Azuma Kagami]] give different versions of the events. This is the Azuma Kagami's version. According to the Gukanshō, when in 1203 Yoriie became seriously ill, the Hōjō clan supported his younger brother Senman (future third shogun Minamoto no Sanetomo) as a successor, while the the Hiki supported son Ichiman. To avoid power falling into the hands of the Hiki, the Hōjō decided to get rid of the Hiki and their protege. This is the version of events described in the article [[Minamoto no Ichiman]].
According to the same Wikipedia article, the Azuma Kagami claims Ichiman was killed by the Hiki, and not by the Hōjō. It seemed natural that Hiki would then be the regent, even if unofficially, of young Ichiman. Thus, he suggested to Yoriie, who would be assassinated shortly afterwards by a separate faction (the [[Hōjō clan]]), that they arrange to have Sanetomo killed. [[Hōjō Masako]], Yoriie's mother and wife of the first shogun Yoritomo, overheard this conversation. Hōjō warriors assassinated Hiki Yoshikazu, and then attacked the palace of young Ichiman, setting a fire and killing both the young heir and a great number of members of the extended Hiki family. When in 1203 Yoriie became seriously ill, the [[Hōjō clan]] supported his brother Senman (future third shogun [[Minamoto no Sanetomo]]) as a future successor, while the the Hiki supported son IchimanAccording to Japanese Wikipedia's "源一幡" (Minamoto no Ichiman) article (see link below), the [[Gukanshō]] and the [[Azuma Kagami]] disagree on the reasons for the clash between clans. This is the Gukanshō's version of events. According to the Azuma Kagami, Yoriie wanted to split the shogunate between his brother and his son, then Hiki proposed him the murder of Sanetomo, but the conversation was overheard by Hōjō Masako. This is also the version of events described in the article [[Hiki Yoshikazu]]. To avoid power falling into the hands of the Hiki, the Hōjō decided to get rid of the Hiki and their protege.Kamakura Citizen's Net On a pretext, [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] invited Hiki Yoshikazu to his home and assassinated him. A battle between the clans ensued, the Hiki were defeated by a coalition of the Hōjō, [[Wada clan|Wada]], [[Miura clan|Miura]] and [[Hatakeyama clan]]s and exterminated. == Three sons of Yoriie== Yoriie had three sons, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]], [[Kugyō (Minamoto no Yoshinari)|Kugyō]], and Senju-maru, all of whom died of a violent death, victims of the power struggle that followed Yoritomo's sudden death. Ichiman（1198-1203) was the eldest. His mother Wakasa no Tsubone was Hiki Yoshikazu's daughter, and the child was brought up by the Hiki clan and he died in the fire that destroyed the Hiki residence . Second son Yoshinari, the only one of the three to reach adulthood, was forced to become a bonze and in 1219 murdered his uncle Sanetomo on the stone stairs at Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū in the shogunal capital of Kamakura, an act for which he was himself slain on the same dayKusumoto (2002: 70-73). Third son {{nihongo|Senju-maru|千寿丸|(1201-1214)}} was 12 when Izumi Chikahira rebelled against the Hōjō to make the child shogun. After Chikahira's defeat, the child was forced to become a bonze like his older brother Yoshinari. A year later [[Wada Yoshimori]] also rebelled but, like Chikahira, was defeated and Senju-maru died with the others of the Wada clan. ==Notes== ==References== {{Reflist}} ==Bibliography== * [http://www.kcn-net.org/e_kama_history/omachi/omachi_1.htm Ōmachi], by the Kamakura Citizen's Net, accessed on September 30, 2008 * {{cite book | last = Kusumoto | first = Katsuji | coauthors = | title = Kamakura Naruhodo Jiten | publisher = Jitsugyō no Nihonsha | date = July 2002 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | id = ISBN 978-4-408-00779-3 }} * Papinot, E. (1910). "Historical and Geographical Dictionary of Japan." 1972 Printing. Charles E. Tuttle Company, Tokyo, ISBN 0-8048-0996-8. * {{cite book | last = Yasuda | first = Motohisa (editor) | coauthors = | title = Kamakura, Muromachi Jinmei Jiten | publisher = Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha | date = 1990 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-404-01757-4 | author = | oclc = 24654085 }} * {{cite book | last = Kamiya | first = Michinori | coauthors = | title = Fukaku Aruku - Kamakura Shiseki Sansaku Vol. 1 & 2 | publisher = Kamakura Shunshūsha | date = 2008 | location = Kamakura | language = Japanese | isbn = 4774003409 | oclc = 169992721 }} ===Further reading=== * {{fr icon}} [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh]], Isaac, ed. (1834). [Siyun-sai Rin-siyo/[[Hayashi Gahō]], 1652], ''[[Nipon o daï itsi ran]]; ou, [http://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran Annales des empereurs du Japon.]'' Paris: [[Royal Asiatic Society|Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland]]. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/300555357?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 300555357] {{start box}} {{succession box | title=[[Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura Shogun]]:
Minamoto no Yoriie | before=[[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] | after=[[Minamoto no Sanetomo]] | years=1202–1203}} {{end box}}

{{start box}} {{succession box | title=[[Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura Shogun]]:
Minamoto no Yoriie | before=[[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] | after=[[Minamoto no Sanetomo]] | years=1202–1203}} {{end box}}
[[Category:1182 births]] [[Category:1204 deaths]] [[Category:Kamakura shoguns]] [[Category:Minamoto clan|Yoriie]] [[Category:People of Heian period Japan]] [[Category:People of Kamakura period Japan]] [[ca:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[de:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[es:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[eu:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[fr:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[ko:미나모토 요리이에]] [[it:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[nl:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[ja:源頼家]] [[pl:Yori'ie Minamoto]] [[ru:Минамото-но Ёрииэ]] [[sv:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[zh:源賴家]]

[[Image:Minamoto no Yoriies grave 2007-02-25.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Minamoto no Yoriie's grave in Shuzenji, [[Izu, Shizuoka|Izu]]]]{{nihongo|'''Minamoto no Yoriie'''|源頼家}} ([[September 11]], [[1182]] – [[August 14]], [[1204]]) was the second [[shogun]] (1202–1203) of [[Japan]]'s [[Kamakura shogunate]], and the first son of first shogun [[Minamoto no Yoritomo|Yoritomo]]. His short rule was purely nominal, and he died in Shuzenji, a small town in today's [[Izu province]], assassinated by his uncle [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. The years in which Yoriie was shogun are encompassed within only one [[Japanese era names|era name]] or ''[[nengō]]'': ''[[Kennin]]'' (1201–1204). == Life == [[File:Minamoto no Yoriie.jpg|thumb|left||Minamoto no Yoriie]]Born from Tokimasa's daughter [[Hōjō Masako]] at [[Hiki Yoshikazu]]'s residence in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], Yoriie had as wet nurses the wives of powerful men like Hiki himself, [[Kajiwara Kagetoki]], and Yoshikazu's younger sister.Yasuda (1990:592-593) Before he was born, his father Yoritomo had [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] and his men carry stones to build the [[Tsurugaoka_Hachiman-gū#Wakamiya_.C5.8Cji|Dankazura]] on [[Wakamiya Ōji]] to pray for the child's safe delivery. When Yoriie later himself had a heir, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]], the child was also born at the Hiki mansionThe mansion no longer exists and its location ({{coord|35|19|1.31|N|139|33|20.95|E|region:JP_type:landmark_scale:1500|display=inline}}) is occupied by Myōhon-ji, a temple dedicated to the memory of the Hiki clan, exterminated by the [[Hōjō clan]]. from Hiki's daughter Wakasa no Tsubone, a fact which further consolidating an already strong emotional bondKamiya Vol. 1 (2008:44-45) From this relationship Hiki gained considerable influence when Yoriie became shogun, raising the hostility of [[Hōjō Tokimasa]], who was instead close to Yoriie's younger brother Senman (future third shogun [[Minamoto no Sanetomo|Sanetomo]]), and was in his turn trying to leverage that relationship for political advantage.Papinot (2972:385_386) After his father's death in 1199, the 17-year-old became head of the [[Minamoto]] clan and was appointed [[Shogun|''seii taishogun'']] in 1202. He was however criticized for his abandonment of his father's policies, and his mother forbade him to do any political activity. On June 30, 1333 (([[Shōji]] 1, 12th day of the 4th month) his remaining powers were formally taken from him and assumed by a council of 13 elders headed by his grandfather [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. Yoriie in turn plotted with the Hiki to subjugate the Hōjō clan but failed, was put under house arrest, forced to abdicate and eventually assassinated on July 17, 1204 in Izu. Yoriie was succeeded by his younger brother Sanetomo, the last of the [[Seiwa Genji]] line to rule, at least nominally, over Kamakura. == Hiki Yoshikazu's rebellion== {{main|Hiki Yoshikazu}} Seriously ill, Yoriie proposed to name both his younger brother [[Minamoto no Sanetomo|Sanetomo]], and his young son (Hiki's grandson) [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] to succeed him; the two would split power, governing separate parts of the country. It seemed natural to them that Hiki would then be the regent, even if unofficially, of young Ichiman. Hiki suggested to Yoriie, who would be assassinated shortly afterwards by a separate faction (the [[Hōjō clan]], that they arrange to have Sanetomo killed. [[Hōjō Masako]], Yoriie's mother and wife of the first shogun Yoritomo, allegedly overheard the conversation. On a pretext, [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] invited Hiki Yoshikazu to his home and assassinated him. A battle between the clans ensued, the Hiki were defeated by a coalition of the Hōjō, [[Wada clan|Wada]], [[Miura clan|Miura]] and [[Hatakeyama clan]]s and exterminated.Kamakura Citizen's NetAccording to Japanese Wikipedia's "源一幡" (Minamoto no Ichiman) article, the [[Gukanshō]] and the [[Azuma Kagami]] give different versions of the events. This is the Azuma Kagami's version. According to the Gukanshō, when in 1203 Yoriie became seriously ill, the Hōjō clan supported his younger brother Senman (future third shogun Minamoto no Sanetomo) as a successor, while the the Hiki supported son Ichiman. To avoid power falling into the hands of the Hiki, the Hōjō decided to get rid of the Hiki and their protege. This is the version of events described in the article [[Minamoto no Ichiman]].
According to the same Wikipedia article, the Azuma Kagami claims Ichiman was killed by the Hiki, and not by the Hōjō. == Three sons of Yoriie== Yoriie had three sons, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]], [[Kugyō (Minamoto no Yoshinari)|Kugyō]], and Senju-maru, all of whom died of a violent death, victims of the power struggle that followed Yoritomo's sudden death. Ichiman（1198-1203) was the eldest. His mother Wakasa no Tsubone was Hiki Yoshikazu's daughter, and the child was brought up by the Hiki clan and he died in the fire that destroyed the Hiki residence. Second son Yoshinari, the only one of the three to reach adulthood, was forced to become a bonze and in 1219 murdered his uncle Sanetomo on the stone stairs at Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū in the shogunal capital of Kamakura, an act for which he was himself slain on the same day.Kusumoto (2002: 70-73) Third son {{nihongo|Senju-maru|千寿丸|(1201-1214)}} was 12 when Izumi Chikahira rebelled against the Hōjō to make the child shogun. After Chikahira's defeat, the child was forced to become a bonze like his older brother Yoshinari. A year later [[Wada Yoshimori]] also rebelled but, like Chikahira, was defeated and Senju-maru died with the others of the Wada clan. ==Notes== ==References== {{Reflist}} ==Bibliography== * [http://www.kcn-net.org/e_kama_history/omachi/omachi_1.htm Ōmachi], by the Kamakura Citizen's Net, accessed on September 30, 2008 * {{cite book | last = Kusumoto | first = Katsuji | coauthors = | title = Kamakura Naruhodo Jiten | publisher = Jitsugyō no Nihonsha | date = July 2002 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | id = ISBN 978-4-408-00779-3 }} * Papinot, E. (1910). "Historical and Geographical Dictionary of Japan." 1972 Printing. Charles E. Tuttle Company, Tokyo, ISBN 0-8048-0996-8. * {{cite book | last = Yasuda | first = Motohisa (editor) | coauthors = | title = Kamakura, Muromachi Jinmei Jiten | publisher = Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha | date = 1990 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-404-01757-4 | author = | oclc = 24654085 }} * {{cite book | last = Kamiya | first = Michinori | coauthors = | title = Fukaku Aruku - Kamakura Shiseki Sansaku Vol. 1 & 2 | publisher = Kamakura Shunshūsha | date = 2008 | location = Kamakura | language = Japanese | isbn = 4774003409 | oclc = 169992721 }} ===Further reading=== * {{fr icon}} [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh]], Isaac, ed. (1834). [Siyun-sai Rin-siyo/[[Hayashi Gahō]], 1652], ''[[Nipon o daï itsi ran]]; ou, [http://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran Annales des empereurs du Japon.]'' Paris: [[Royal Asiatic Society|Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland]]. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/300555357?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 300555357]

[[Category:1182 births]] [[Category:1204 deaths]] [[Category:Kamakura shoguns]] [[Category:Minamoto clan|Yoriie]] [[Category:People of Heian period Japan]] [[Category:People of Kamakura period Japan]] [[ca:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[de:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[es:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[eu:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[fr:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[ko:미나모토 요리이에]] [[it:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[nl:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[ja:源頼家]] [[pl:Yori'ie Minamoto]] [[ru:Минамото-но Ёрииэ]] [[sv:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[zh:源賴家]]

[[Image:Minamoto no Yoriies grave 2007-02-25.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Minamoto no Yoriie's grave in Shuzenji, [[Izu, Shizuoka|Izu]]]]{{nihongo|'''Minamoto no Yoriie'''|源頼家}} ([[September 11]], [[1182]] – [[August 14]], [[1204]]) was the second [[shogun]] (1202–1203) of [[Japan]]'s [[Kamakura shogunate]], and the first son of first shogun [[Minamoto no Yoritomo|Yoritomo]]. His short rule was purely nominal, and he died in Shuzenji, a small town in today's [[Izu province]], assassinated by his uncle [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. The years in which Yoriie was shogun are encompassed within only one [[Japanese era names|era name]] or ''[[nengō]]'': ''[[Kennin]]'' (1201–1204). == Life == [[File:Minamoto no Yoriie.jpg|thumb|left||Minamoto no Yoriie]]Born from Tokimasa's daughter [[Hōjō Masako]] at [[Hiki Yoshikazu]]'s residence in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], Yoriie had as wet nurses the wives of powerful men like Hiki himself and [[Kajiwara Kagetoki]], and Hiki's younger sister.Yasuda (1990:592-593) Before he was born, his father Yoritomo had [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] and his men carry stones to build the [[Tsurugaoka_Hachiman-gū#Wakamiya_.C5.8Cji|Dankazura]] on [[Wakamiya Ōji]] to pray for the child's safe delivery. When Yoriie later himself had a heir, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]], the child was also born at the Hiki mansionThe mansion no longer exists and its location ({{coord|35|19|1.31|N|139|33|20.95|E|region:JP_type:landmark_scale:1500|display=inline}}) is occupied by Myōhon-ji, a temple dedicated to the memory of the Hiki clan, exterminated by the [[Hōjō clan]]. from Hiki's daughter Wakasa no Tsubone, a fact which further consolidating an already strong emotional bondKamiya Vol. 1 (2008:44-45) From this relationship Hiki gained considerable influence when Yoriie became shogun, raising the hostility of [[Hōjō Tokimasa]], who was instead close to Yoriie's younger brother Senman (future third shogun [[Minamoto no Sanetomo|Sanetomo]]), and was in his turn trying to leverage that relationship for political advantage.Papinot (2972:385_386) After his father's death in 1199, the 17-year-old became head of the [[Minamoto]] clan and was appointed [[Shogun|''seii taishogun'']] in 1202. He was however criticized for his abandonment of his father's policies, and his mother forbade him to do any political activity. On June 30, 1333 (([[Shōji]] 1, 12th day of the 4th month) his remaining powers were formally taken from him and assumed by a council of 13 elders headed by his grandfather [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. Yoriie in turn plotted with the Hiki to subjugate the Hōjō clan but failed, was put under house arrest, forced to abdicate and eventually assassinated on July 17, 1204 in Izu. Yoriie was succeeded by his younger brother Sanetomo, the last of the [[Seiwa Genji]] line to rule, at least nominally, over Kamakura. == Hiki Yoshikazu's rebellion== {{main|Hiki Yoshikazu}} Seriously ill, Yoriie proposed to name both his younger brother [[Minamoto no Sanetomo|Sanetomo]], and his young son (Hiki's grandson) [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] to succeed him; the two would split power, governing separate parts of the country. It seemed natural to them that Hiki would then be the regent, even if unofficially, of young Ichiman. Hiki suggested to Yoriie, who would be assassinated shortly afterwards by a separate faction (the [[Hōjō clan]], that they arrange to have Sanetomo killed. [[Hōjō Masako]], Yoriie's mother and wife of the first shogun Yoritomo, allegedly overheard the conversation. On a pretext, [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] invited Hiki Yoshikazu to his home and assassinated him. A battle between the clans ensued, the Hiki were defeated by a coalition of the Hōjō, [[Wada clan|Wada]], [[Miura clan|Miura]] and [[Hatakeyama clan]]s and exterminated.Kamakura Citizen's NetAccording to Japanese Wikipedia's "源一幡" (Minamoto no Ichiman) article, the [[Gukanshō]] and the [[Azuma Kagami]] give different versions of the events. This is the Azuma Kagami's version. According to the Gukanshō, when in 1203 Yoriie became seriously ill, the Hōjō clan supported his younger brother Senman (future third shogun Minamoto no Sanetomo) as a successor, while the the Hiki supported son Ichiman. To avoid power falling into the hands of the Hiki, the Hōjō decided to get rid of the Hiki and their protege. This is the version of events described in the article [[Minamoto no Ichiman]].
According to the same Wikipedia article, the Azuma Kagami claims Ichiman was killed by the Hiki, and not by the Hōjō. == Three sons of Yoriie== Yoriie had three sons, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]], [[Kugyō (Minamoto no Yoshinari)|Kugyō]], and Senju-maru, all of whom died of a violent death, victims of the power struggle that followed Yoritomo's sudden death. Ichiman（1198-1203) was the eldest. His mother Wakasa no Tsubone was Hiki Yoshikazu's daughter, and the child was brought up by the Hiki clan and he died in the fire that destroyed the Hiki residence. Second son Yoshinari, the only one of the three to reach adulthood, was forced to become a bonze and in 1219 murdered his uncle Sanetomo on the stone stairs at Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū in the shogunal capital of Kamakura, an act for which he was himself slain on the same day.Kusumoto (2002: 70-73) Third son {{nihongo|Senju-maru|千寿丸|(1201-1214)}} was 12 when Izumi Chikahira rebelled against the Hōjō to make the child shogun. After Chikahira's defeat, the child was forced to become a bonze like his older brother Yoshinari. A year later [[Wada Yoshimori]] also rebelled but, like Chikahira, was defeated and Senju-maru died with the others of the Wada clan. ==Notes== ==References== {{Reflist}} ==Bibliography== * [http://www.kcn-net.org/e_kama_history/omachi/omachi_1.htm Ōmachi], by the Kamakura Citizen's Net, accessed on September 30, 2008 * {{cite book | last = Kusumoto | first = Katsuji | coauthors = | title = Kamakura Naruhodo Jiten | publisher = Jitsugyō no Nihonsha | date = July 2002 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | id = ISBN 978-4-408-00779-3 }} * Papinot, E. (1910). "Historical and Geographical Dictionary of Japan." 1972 Printing. Charles E. Tuttle Company, Tokyo, ISBN 0-8048-0996-8. * {{cite book | last = Yasuda | first = Motohisa (editor) | coauthors = | title = Kamakura, Muromachi Jinmei Jiten | publisher = Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha | date = 1990 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-404-01757-4 | author = | oclc = 24654085 }} * {{cite book | last = Kamiya | first = Michinori | coauthors = | title = Fukaku Aruku - Kamakura Shiseki Sansaku Vol. 1 & 2 | publisher = Kamakura Shunshūsha | date = 2008 | location = Kamakura | language = Japanese | isbn = 4774003409 | oclc = 169992721 }} ===Further reading=== * {{fr icon}} [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh]], Isaac, ed. (1834). [Siyun-sai Rin-siyo/[[Hayashi Gahō]], 1652], ''[[Nipon o daï itsi ran]]; ou, [http://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran Annales des empereurs du Japon.]'' Paris: [[Royal Asiatic Society|Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland]]. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/300555357?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 300555357]
