After his father's death in 1199, the 17-year-old became head of the [[Minamoto]] clan and was appointed [[Shōgun|''sei-i taishōgun'']] in 1202. He was, however, criticized for his abandonment of his father's policies, and his mother forbade him from any involvement political activity. On June 30, 1203 ([[Shōji (era)|Shōji]] 1, 12th day of the 4th month) his remaining powers were formally taken from him and assumed by a council of 13 elders, headed by his grandfather [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. Yoriie in turn plotted with the Hiki to subjugate the Hōjō clan; however, he failed, was put under house arrest and forced to abdicate, and was eventually assassinated on July 17, 1204 in Izu. Yoriie was succeeded by his younger brother Sanetomo, the last of the [[Seiwa Genji]] line to rule, at least nominally, over Kamakura. ==Hiki Yoshikazu's rebellion== {{main|Hiki Yoshikazu}} Seriously ill, Yoriie proposed to name both his younger brother [[Minamoto no Sanetomo|Sanetomo]], and his young son (Hiki's grandson) [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] to succeed him; the two would split power, governing separate parts of the country. It seemed natural to them that Hiki would then be the regent, even if unofficially, of young Ichiman. Hiki suggested to Yoriie, who would be assassinated shortly afterwards by a separate faction (the [[Hōjō clan]]), that they arrange to have Sanetomo killed. [[Hōjō Masako]], Yoriie's mother and wife of the first shogun Yoritomo, allegedly overheard the conversation. On a pretext, [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] invited Hiki Yoshikazu to his home and assassinated him. A battle between the clans ensued, the Hiki were defeated by a coalition of the Hōjō, [[Wada clan|Wada]], [[Miura clan|Miura]] and [[Hatakeyama clan]]s and were exterminated. Yoriie died in Shuzenji, a small town in what was later called [[Izu Province]], assassinated by his uncle [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. ==Three sons of Yoriie== Yoriie had three sons, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]], [[Kugyō (Minamoto no Yoshinari)|Kugyō]], Senju-maru and he also had one daughter, Minamoto no Yoshiko married to fourth shogun of [[Kamakura]], [[Kujō Yoritsune]]. But all of his son died violent deaths, victims of the power struggle that followed Yoritomo's sudden death. Ichiman (1198–1203) was the eldest. His mother Wakasa no Tsubone was Hiki Yoshikazu's daughter, and the child was brought up by the Hiki clan. There are contrasting versions of his death, but in any case he died in the fire that destroyed the Hiki residence. His second son Yoshinari, the only one of the three to reach adulthood, was forced to become a Buddhist monk (''bonze'') and in 1219 murdered his uncle Sanetomo on the stone stairs at Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū in the shogunal capital of Kamakura, an act for which he was himself slain on the same day.Kusumoto (2002: 70–73) Third son Senju-maru ({{nihongo2|千寿丸}}, 1201–1214) was 12 when Izumi Chikahira rebelled against the Hōjō to make the child ''shōgun''. After Chikahira's defeat, the child was forced to become a Buddhist monk like his older brother [[Minamoto no Yoshinari|Yoshinari]]. A year later [[Wada Yoshimori]] also rebelled but, like Chikahira, was defeated and Senju-maru died with the others of the Wada clan. ==Family== * Father: [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] * Mother: [[Hōjō Masako]] * Wife: Wakasa no Tsubone (d. 1203) * Concubine: ** Ishina no Tsubone ** Asuke no Tsubone * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] By Wakasa ** Minamoto no Yoshiko or Take no Gosho (1202–1234) married [[Kujō Yoritsune]] by Wakasa ** Eijitsu (1201–1215) by Ishina no Tsubone ** Zengyo (d. 1220) by Ishina no Tsubone ** [[Kugyō]] by Asuke no Tsubone ==Eras of Yoriie's ''bakufu''== The years in which Yoriie was shogun are all within only one [[Japanese era names|era name]] or ''[[nengō]]'': ''[[Kennin]]'' (1201–1204). ==Notes== [[Image:Minamoto no Yoriies grave 2007-02-25.jpg|thumb|right|Minamoto no Yoriie's grave in Shuzenji, [[Izu, Shizuoka|Izu]]]] {{Reflist}} ==References== * {{cite book | last = Kamiya | first = Michinori | coauthors = | title = Fukaku Aruku: Kamakura Shiseki Sansaku Vol. 1 & 2 | publisher = Kamakura Shunshūsha | year = 2008 | location = Kamakura | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-7740-0340-9 | oclc = 169992721 }} * {{cite book | last = Kusumoto | first = Katsuji | coauthors = | title = Kamakura Naruhodo Jiten | publisher = Jitsugyō no Nihonsha | date = July 2002 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-408-00779-3 }} * [[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]] and Käthe Roth. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&client=firefox-a ''Japan encyclopedia''.] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/58053128?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 58053128] * [[Edmond Papinot|Papinot, Edmond]]. (1906) ''Dictionnaire d'histoire et de géographie du japon''. Tokyo: Librarie Sansaisha.[http://www.unterstein.net/Toyoashihara-no-Chiaki-Nagaioaki-no-Mitsuho-no-Kuni/NobiliaireJapon.pdf ..Click link for digitized 1906 ''Nobiliaire du japon'' (2003)] * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). ''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''.] Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. [http://www.worldcat.org/title/nipon-o-dai-itsi-ran-ou-annales-des-empereurs-du-japon/oclc/5850691 OCLC 5850691]. * {{cite book | last = Yasuda | first = Motohisa (editor) | coauthors = | title = Kamakura, Muromachi Jinmei Jiten | publisher = Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha | year = 1990 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-404-01757-4 | author = | oclc = 24654085 }} ==External links== * [http://www.kcn-net.org/e_kama_history/omachi/omachi_1.htm Ōmachi], by the Kamakura Citizen's Net, accessed on September 30, 2008 {{s-start}} {{succession box | title=''[[Shōgun]]'':

{{Japanese name|Minamoto}} [[File:Minamoto no Yoriie.jpg|thumb|right|Minamoto no Yoriie]] {{nihongo|'''Minamoto no Yoriie'''|源 頼家||September 11, 1182 – August 14, 1204|lead=yes}} was the second ''[[shōgun]]'' (1202–1203) of [[Japan]]'s [[Kamakura shogunate]], and the first son of first shōgun [[Minamoto no Yoritomo|Yoritomo]].[[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). "Minamoto no Yoriie" in {{Google books|p2QnPijAEmEC|''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 635|page=635}}. His buddhist name was Hokke-in-dono Kingo Da'i Zengo (法華院殿金吾大禅閤). == Life == Born to Tokimasa's daughter [[Hōjō Masako]] at [[Hiki Yoshikazu]]'s residence in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], Yoriie had as wet nurses the wives of powerful men such as Hiki himself and [[Kajiwara Kagetoki]], and also Hiki's younger sister.Yasuda (1990:592-593) Before he was born, his father Yoritomo had [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] and his men carry stones to build the [[Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū#Wakamiya .C5.8Cji|Dankazura]] on [[Wakamiya Ōji]] to pray for the child's safe delivery. When Yoriie later himself had an heir, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]], the child was also born at the Hiki mansionThe mansion no longer exists and its location ({{coord|35|19|1.31|N|139|33|20.95|E|region:JP_type:landmark_scale:1500|display=inline}}) is occupied by [[Myōhon-ji]] a temple dedicated to the memory of the Hiki clan, exterminated by the [[Hōjō clan]]. to Hiki's daughter Wakasa no Tsubone, a fact which further consolidated an already strong emotional bond.Kamiya Vol. 1 (2008:44-45) From this relationship Hiki gained considerable influence when Yoriie became shōgun, incurring the hostility of [[Hōjō Tokimasa]], who was instead close to Yoriie's younger brother Senman (future third shōgun [[Minamoto no Sanetomo|Sanetomo]]), and who was in his turn trying to leverage that relationship for political advantage. His childhood name was Manju (万寿). Yoriie showed when still very young great interest in military arts like fencing, and horse-riding. After his father's death in 1199, the 17-year-old became head of the [[Minamoto]] clan and was appointed [[Shōgun|''sei-i taishōgun'']] in 1202. He was, however, criticized for his abandonment of his father's policies, and his mother forbade him from any involvement political activity. On June 30, 1203 ([[Shōji (era)|Shōji]] 1, 12th day of the 4th month) his remaining powers were formally taken from him and assumed by a council of 13 elders, headed by his grandfather [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. Yoriie in turn plotted with the Hiki to subjugate the Hōjō clan; however, he failed, was put under house arrest and forced to abdicate, and was eventually assassinated on July 17, 1204 in Izu. Yoriie was succeeded by his younger brother Sanetomo, the last of the [[Seiwa Genji]] line to rule, at least nominally, over Kamakura. ==Hiki Yoshikazu's rebellion== {{main|Hiki Yoshikazu}} Seriously ill, Yoriie proposed to name both his younger brother [[Minamoto no Sanetomo|Sanetomo]], and his young son (Hiki's grandson) [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] to succeed him; the two would split power, governing separate parts of the country. It seemed natural to them that Hiki would then be the regent, even if unofficially, of young Ichiman. Hiki suggested to Yoriie, who would be assassinated shortly afterwards by a separate faction (the [[Hōjō clan]]), that they arrange to have Sanetomo killed. [[Hōjō Masako]], Yoriie's mother and wife of the first shōgun Yoritomo, allegedly overheard the conversation. On a pretext, [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] invited Hiki Yoshikazu to his home and assassinated him. A battle between the clans ensued, the Hiki were defeated by a coalition of the Hōjō, [[Wada clan|Wada]], [[Miura clan|Miura]] and [[Hatakeyama clan]]s and were exterminated. Yoriie died in Shuzenji, a small town in what was later called [[Izu Province]], assassinated by his uncle [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. ==Three sons of Yoriie== Yoriie had three sons, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]], [[Kugyō (Minamoto no Yoshinari)|Kugyō]], Senju-maru and he also had one daughter, Minamoto no Yoshiko married to fourth shōgun of [[Kamakura]], [[Kujō Yoritsune]]. But all of his son died violent deaths, victims of the power struggle that followed Yoritomo's sudden death. Ichiman (1198–1203) was the eldest. His mother Wakasa no Tsubone was Hiki Yoshikazu's daughter, and the child was brought up by the Hiki clan. There are contrasting versions of his death, but in any case he died in the fire that destroyed the Hiki residence. His second son Yoshinari, the only one of the three to reach adulthood, was forced to become a Buddhist monk (''bonze'') and in 1219 murdered his uncle Sanetomo on the stone stairs at Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū in the shogunal capital of Kamakura, an act for which he was himself slain on the same day.Kusumoto (2002: 70–73) Third son Senju-maru ({{nihongo2|千寿丸}}, 1201–1214) was 12 when Izumi Chikahira rebelled against the Hōjō to make the child ''shōgun''. After Chikahira's defeat, the child was forced to become a Buddhist monk like his older brother [[Minamoto no Yoshinari|Yoshinari]]. A year later [[Wada Yoshimori]] also rebelled but, like Chikahira, was defeated and Senju-maru died with the others of the Wada clan. ==Family== * Father: [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] * Mother: [[Hōjō Masako]] * Wife: Wakasa no Tsubone (d. 1203) * Concubine: ** Ishina no Tsubone ** Asuke no Tsubone * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] By Wakasa ** Minamoto no Yoshiko or Take no Gosho (1202–1234) married [[Kujō Yoritsune]] by Wakasa ** Eijitsu (1201–1215) by Ishina no Tsubone ** Zengyo (d. 1220) by Ishina no Tsubone ** [[Kugyō]] by Asuke no Tsubone ==Eras of Yoriie's ''bakufu''== The years in which Yoriie was shōgun are all within only one [[Japanese era names|era name]] or ''[[nengō]]'': ''[[Kennin]]'' (1201–1204). ==Notes== [[Image:Minamoto no Yoriies grave 2007-02-25.jpg|thumb|right|Minamoto no Yoriie's grave in Shuzenji, [[Izu, Shizuoka|Izu]]]] {{Reflist}} ==References== * {{cite book | last = Kamiya | first = Michinori | coauthors = | title = Fukaku Aruku: Kamakura Shiseki Sansaku Vol. 1 & 2 | publisher = Kamakura Shunshūsha | year = 2008 | location = Kamakura | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-7740-0340-9 | oclc = 169992721 }} * {{cite book | last = Kusumoto | first = Katsuji | coauthors = | title = Kamakura Naruhodo Jiten | publisher = Jitsugyō no Nihonsha | date = July 2002 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-408-00779-3 }} * [[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]] and Käthe Roth. (2005). "Minamoto no Yoriie" in {{Google books|p2QnPijAEmEC|''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 635|page=635}}. His buddhist name was Hokke-in-dono Kingo Da'i Zengo (法華院殿金吾大禅閤). == Life == Born to Tokimasa's daughter [[Hōjō Masako]] at [[Hiki Yoshikazu]]'s residence in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], Yoriie had as wet nurses the wives of powerful men such as Hiki himself and [[Kajiwara Kagetoki]], and also Hiki's younger sister.Yasuda (1990:592-593) Before he was born, his father Yoritomo had [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] and his men carry stones to build the [[Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū#Wakamiya .C5.8Cji|Dankazura]] on [[Wakamiya Ōji]] to pray for the child's safe delivery. When Yoriie later himself had an heir, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]], the child was also born at the Hiki mansionThe mansion no longer exists and its location ({{coord|35|19|1.31|N|139|33|20.95|E|region:JP_type:landmark_scale:1500|display=inline}}) is occupied by [[Myōhon-ji]] a temple dedicated to the memory of the Hiki clan, exterminated by the [[Hōjō clan]]. to Hiki's daughter Wakasa no Tsubone, a fact which further consolidated an already strong emotional bond.Kamiya Vol. 1 (2008:44-45) From this relationship Hiki gained considerable influence when Yoriie became shōgun, incurring the hostility of [[Hōjō Tokimasa]], who was instead close to Yoriie's younger brother Senman (future third shōgun [[Minamoto no Sanetomo|Sanetomo]]), and who was in his turn trying to leverage that relationship for political advantage. His childhood name was Manju (万寿). Yoriie showed when still very young great interest in military arts like fencing, and horse-riding. After his father's death in 1199, the 17-year-old became head of the [[Minamoto]] clan and was appointed [[Shōgun|''sei-i taishōgun'']] in 1202. He was, however, criticized for his abandonment of his father's policies, and his mother forbade him from any involvement political activity. On June 30, 1203 ([[Shōji (era)|Shōji]] 1, 12th day of the 4th month) his remaining powers were formally taken from him and assumed by a council of 13 elders, headed by his grandfather [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. Yoriie in turn plotted with the Hiki to subjugate the Hōjō clan; however, he failed, was put under house arrest and forced to abdicate, and was eventually assassinated on July 17, 1204 in Izu. Yoriie was succeeded by his younger brother Sanetomo, the last of the [[Seiwa Genji]] line to rule, at least nominally, over Kamakura. ==Hiki Yoshikazu's rebellion== {{main|Hiki Yoshikazu}} Seriously ill, Yoriie proposed to name both his younger brother [[Minamoto no Sanetomo|Sanetomo]], and his young son (Hiki's grandson) [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] to succeed him; the two would split power, governing separate parts of the country. It seemed natural to them that Hiki would then be the regent, even if unofficially, of young Ichiman. Hiki suggested to Yoriie, who would be assassinated shortly afterwards by a separate faction (the [[Hōjō clan]]), that they arrange to have Sanetomo killed. [[Hōjō Masako]], Yoriie's mother and wife of the first shōgun Yoritomo, allegedly overheard the conversation. On a pretext, [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] invited Hiki Yoshikazu to his home and assassinated him. A battle between the clans ensued, the Hiki were defeated by a coalition of the Hōjō, [[Wada clan|Wada]], [[Miura clan|Miura]] and [[Hatakeyama clan]]s and were exterminated. Yoriie died in Shuzenji, a small town in what was later called [[Izu Province]], assassinated by his uncle [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. ==Three sons of Yoriie== Yoriie had three sons, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]], [[Kugyō (Minamoto no Yoshinari)|Kugyō]], Senju-maru and he also had one daughter, Minamoto no Yoshiko married to fourth shōgun of [[Kamakura]], [[Kujō Yoritsune]]. But all of his son died violent deaths, victims of the power struggle that followed Yoritomo's sudden death. Ichiman (1198–1203) was the eldest. His mother Wakasa no Tsubone was Hiki Yoshikazu's daughter, and the child was brought up by the Hiki clan. There are contrasting versions of his death, but in any case he died in the fire that destroyed the Hiki residence. His second son Yoshinari, the only one of the three to reach adulthood, was forced to become a Buddhist monk (''bonze'') and in 1219 murdered his uncle Sanetomo on the stone stairs at Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū in the shogunal capital of Kamakura, an act for which he was himself slain on the same day.Kusumoto (2002: 70–73) Third son Senju-maru ({{nihongo2|千寿丸}}, 1201–1214) was 12 when Izumi Chikahira rebelled against the Hōjō to make the child ''shōgun''. After Chikahira's defeat, the child was forced to become a Buddhist monk like his older brother [[Minamoto no Yoshinari|Yoshinari]]. A year later [[Wada Yoshimori]] also rebelled but, like Chikahira, was defeated and Senju-maru died with the others of the Wada clan. ==Family== * Father: [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] * Mother: [[Hōjō Masako]] * Wife: Wakasa no Tsubone (d. 1203) * Concubine: ** Ishina no Tsubone ** Asuke no Tsubone * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] By Wakasa ** Minamoto no Yoshiko or Take no Gosho (1202–1234) married [[Kujō Yoritsune]] by Wakasa ** Eijitsu (1201–1215) by Ishina no Tsubone ** Zengyo (d. 1220) by Ishina no Tsubone ** [[Kugyō (priest)|Kugyō]] by Asuke no Tsubone ==Eras of Yoriie's ''bakufu''== The years in which Yoriie was shōgun are all within only one [[Japanese era names|era name]] or ''[[nengō]]'': ''[[Kennin]]'' (1201–1204). ==Notes== [[Image:Minamoto no Yoriies grave 2007-02-25.jpg|thumb|right|Minamoto no Yoriie's grave in Shuzenji, [[Izu, Shizuoka|Izu]]]] {{Reflist}} ==References== * {{cite book | last = Kamiya | first = Michinori | coauthors = | title = Fukaku Aruku: Kamakura Shiseki Sansaku Vol. 1 & 2 | publisher = Kamakura Shunshūsha | year = 2008 | location = Kamakura | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-7740-0340-9 | oclc = 169992721 }} * {{cite book | last = Kusumoto | first = Katsuji | coauthors = | title = Kamakura Naruhodo Jiten | publisher = Jitsugyō no Nihonsha | date = July 2002 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-408-00779-3 }} * [[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]] and Käthe Roth. {{Japanese name|Minamoto}} [[File:Minamoto no Yoriie.jpg|thumb|right|Minamoto no Yoriie]] {{nihongo|'''Minamoto no Yoriie'''|源 頼家||September 11, 1182 – August 14, 1204|lead=yes}} was the second ''[[shōgun]]'' (1202–1203) of [[Japan]]'s [[Kamakura shogunate]], and the first son of first shōgun [[Minamoto no Yoritomo|Yoritomo]].[[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). "Minamoto no Yoriie" in {{Google books|p2QnPijAEmEC|''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 635|page=635}}. His buddhist name was Hokke-in-dono Kingo Da'i Zengo (法華院殿金吾大禅閤). == Life == Born to Tokimasa's daughter [[Hōjō Masako]] at [[Hiki Yoshikazu]]'s residence in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], Yoriie had as wet nurses the wives of powerful men such as Hiki himself and [[Kajiwara Kagetoki]], and also Hiki's younger sister.Yasuda (1990:592-593) Before he was born, his father Yoritomo had [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] and his men carry stones to build the [[Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū#Wakamiya .C5.8Cji|Dankazura]] on [[Wakamiya Ōji]] to pray for the child's safe delivery. When Yoriie later himself had an heir, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]], the child was also born at the Hiki mansionThe mansion no longer exists and its location ({{coord|35|19|1.31|N|139|33|20.95|E|region:JP_type:landmark_scale:1500|display=inline}}) is occupied by [[Myōhon-ji]] a temple dedicated to the memory of the Hiki clan, exterminated by the [[Hōjō clan]]. to Hiki's daughter Wakasa no Tsubone, a fact which further consolidated an already strong emotional bond.Kamiya Vol. 1 (2008:44-45) From this relationship Hiki gained considerable influence when Yoriie became shōgun, incurring the hostility of [[Hōjō Tokimasa]], who was instead close to Yoriie's younger brother Senman (future third shōgun [[Minamoto no Sanetomo|Sanetomo]]), and who was in his turn trying to leverage that relationship for political advantage. His childhood name was Manju (万寿). Yoriie showed when still very young great interest in military arts like fencing, and horse-riding. After his father's death in 1199, the 17-year-old became head of the [[Minamoto]] clan and was appointed [[Shōgun|''sei-i taishōgun'']] in 1202. He was, however, criticized for his abandonment of his father's policies, and his mother forbade him from any involvement political activity. On June 30, 1203 ([[Shōji (era)|Shōji]] 1, 12th day of the 4th month) his remaining powers were formally taken from him and assumed by a council of 13 elders, headed by his grandfather [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. He ordained as a Buddhist monk. Yoriie, in turn, plotted with the Hiki to subjugate the Hōjō clan; however, he failed, was put under house arrest and forced to abdicate, and was eventually assassinated on July 17, 1204 in Izu. Yoriie was succeeded by his younger brother Sanetomo, the last of the [[Seiwa Genji]] line to rule, at least nominally, over Kamakura. ==Hiki Yoshikazu's rebellion== {{main|Hiki Yoshikazu}} Seriously ill, Yoriie proposed to name both his younger brother [[Minamoto no Sanetomo|Sanetomo]], and his young son (Hiki's grandson) [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] to succeed him; the two would split power, governing separate parts of the country. It seemed natural to them that Hiki would then be the regent, even if unofficially, of young Ichiman. Hiki suggested to Yoriie, who would be assassinated shortly afterwards by a separate faction (the [[Hōjō clan]]), that they arrange to have Sanetomo killed. [[Hōjō Masako]], Yoriie's mother and wife of the first shōgun Yoritomo, allegedly overheard the conversation. On a pretext, [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] invited Hiki Yoshikazu to his home and assassinated him. A battle between the clans ensued, the Hiki were defeated by a coalition of the Hōjō, [[Wada clan|Wada]], [[Miura clan|Miura]] and [[Hatakeyama clan]]s and were exterminated. Yoriie died in Shuzenji, a small town in what was later called [[Izu Province]], assassinated by his uncle [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. ==Three sons of Yoriie== Yoriie had three sons, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]], [[Kugyō (Minamoto no Yoshinari)|Kugyō]], Senju-maru and he also had one daughter, Minamoto no Yoshiko married to fourth shōgun of [[Kamakura]], [[Kujō Yoritsune]]. But all of his son died violent deaths, victims of the power struggle that followed Yoritomo's sudden death. Ichiman (1198–1203) was the eldest. His mother Wakasa no Tsubone was Hiki Yoshikazu's daughter, and the child was brought up by the Hiki clan. There are contrasting versions of his death, but in any case, he died in the fire that destroyed the Hiki residence. His second son Yoshinari, the only one of the three to reach adulthood, was forced to become a Buddhist monk (''bonze'') and in 1219 murdered his uncle Sanetomo on the stone stairs at Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū in the shogunal capital of Kamakura, an act for which he was himself slain on the same day.Kusumoto (2002: 70–73) Third son Senju-maru ({{nihongo2|千寿丸}}, 1201–1214) was 12 when Izumi Chikahira rebelled against the Hōjō to make the child ''shōgun''. After Chikahira's defeat, the child was forced to become a Buddhist monk like his older brother [[Minamoto no Yoshinari|Yoshinari]]. A year later [[Wada Yoshimori]] also rebelled but, like Chikahira, was defeated and Senju-maru died with the others of the Wada clan. ==Family== * Father: [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] * Mother: [[Hōjō Masako]] * Wife: Wakasa no Tsubone (d. 1203) * Concubine: ** Ishina no Tsubone ** Asuke no Tsubone * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] By Wakasa ** Minamoto no Yoshiko or Take no Gosho (1202–1234) married [[Kujō Yoritsune]] by Wakasa ** Eijitsu (1201–1215) by Ishina no Tsubone ** Zengyo (d. 1220) by Ishina no Tsubone ** [[Kugyō (priest)|Kugyō]] by Asuke no Tsubone ==Eras of Yoriie's ''bakufu''== The years in which Yoriie was shōgun are all within only one [[Japanese era names|era name]] or ''[[nengō]]'': ''[[Kennin]]'' (1201–1204). ==Notes== [[Image:Minamoto no Yoriies grave 2007-02-25.jpg|thumb|right|Minamoto no Yoriie's grave in Shuzenji, [[Izu, Shizuoka|Izu]]]] {{Reflist}} ==References== * {{cite book | last = Kamiya | first = Michinori | coauthors = | title = Fukaku Aruku: Kamakura Shiseki Sansaku Vol. 1 & 2 | publisher = Kamakura Shunshūsha | year = 2008 | location = Kamakura | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-7740-0340-9 | oclc = 169992721 }} * {{cite book | last = Kusumoto | first = Katsuji | coauthors = | title = Kamakura Naruhodo Jiten | publisher = Jitsugyō no Nihonsha | date = July 2002 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-408-00779-3 }} * [[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]] and Käthe Roth. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&client=firefox-a ''Japan encyclopedia''.] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/58053128?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 58053128] * [[Edmond Papinot|Papinot, Edmond]]. (1906) ''Dictionnaire d'histoire et de géographie du japon''. Tokyo: Librarie Sansaisha.[http://www.unterstein.net/Toyoashihara-no-Chiaki-Nagaioaki-no-Mitsuho-no-Kuni/NobiliaireJapon.pdf ..Click link for digitized 1906 ''Nobiliaire du japon'' (2003)] * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). ''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''.] Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. [http://www.worldcat.org/title/nipon-o-dai-itsi-ran-ou-annales-des-empereurs-du-japon/oclc/5850691 OCLC 5850691]. * {{cite book | last = Yasuda | first = Motohisa (editor) | coauthors = | title = Kamakura, Muromachi Jinmei Jiten | publisher = Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha | year = 1990 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-404-01757-4 | author = | oclc = 24654085 }} ==External links== * [http://www.kcn-net.org/e_kama_history/omachi/omachi_1.htm Ōmachi], by the Kamakura Citizen's Net, accessed on September 30, 2008 {{s-start}} {{succession box | title=''[[Shōgun]]'':
Minamoto no Yoriie | before=[[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] | after=[[Minamoto no Sanetomo]] | years=1202–1203}} {{s-end}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Minamoto no, Yoriie}} [[Category:1182 births]] [[Category:1204 deaths]] [[Category:12th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:13th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:13th-century shōguns]] [[Category:Kamakura shōguns]] [[Category:Minamoto clan]] [[Category:People of Heian-period Japan]] [[Category:People of Kamakura-period Japan]] [[Category:Kamakura period Buddhist monks]] [[Category:Assassinated Japanese people]]

(2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&client=firefox-a ''Japan encyclopedia''.] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/58053128?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 58053128] * [[Edmond Papinot|Papinot, Edmond]]. (1906) ''Dictionnaire d'histoire et de géographie du japon''. Tokyo: Librarie Sansaisha.[http://www.unterstein.net/Toyoashihara-no-Chiaki-Nagaioaki-no-Mitsuho-no-Kuni/NobiliaireJapon.pdf ..Click link for digitized 1906 ''Nobiliaire du japon'' (2003)] * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). ''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''.] Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. [http://www.worldcat.org/title/nipon-o-dai-itsi-ran-ou-annales-des-empereurs-du-japon/oclc/5850691 OCLC 5850691]. * {{cite book | last = Yasuda | first = Motohisa (editor) | title = Kamakura, Muromachi Jinmei Jiten | publisher = Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha | year = 1990 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-404-01757-4 | oclc = 24654085 }} ==External links== * [http://www.kcn-net.org/e_kama_history/omachi/omachi_1.htm Ōmachi], by the Kamakura Citizen's Net, accessed on September 30, 2008 {{s-start}} {{succession box | title=''[[Shōgun]]'':

{{family name hatnote|Minamoto|lang=Japanese}} [[File:Minamoto no Yoriie.jpg|thumb|right|Minamoto no Yoriie]] {{nihongo|'''Minamoto no Yoriie'''|源 頼家||September 11, 1182 – August 14, 1204|lead=yes}} was the second ''[[shōgun]]'' (1202–1203) of [[Japan]]'s [[Kamakura shogunate]], and the first son of first shōgun [[Minamoto no Yoritomo|Yoritomo]].[[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). "Minamoto no Yoriie" in {{Google books|p2QnPijAEmEC|''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 635|page=635}}. His buddhist name was Hokke-in-dono Kingo Da'i Zengo (法華院殿金吾大禅閤). == Life == Born to Tokimasa's daughter [[Hōjō Masako]] at [[Hiki Yoshikazu]]'s residence in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], Yoriie had as wet nurses the wives of powerful men such as Hiki himself and [[Kajiwara Kagetoki]], and also Hiki's younger sister.Yasuda (1990:592-593) Before he was born, his father Yoritomo had [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] and his men carry stones to build the [[Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū#Wakamiya .C5.8Cji|Dankazura]] on [[Wakamiya Ōji]] to pray for the child's safe delivery. When Yoriie later himself had an heir, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]], the child was also born at the Hiki mansionThe mansion no longer exists and its location ({{coord|35|19|1.31|N|139|33|20.95|E|region:JP_type:landmark_scale:1500|display=inline}}) is occupied by [[Myōhon-ji]] a temple dedicated to the memory of the Hiki clan, exterminated by the [[Hōjō clan]]. to Hiki's daughter Wakasa no Tsubone, a fact which further consolidated an already strong emotional bond.Kamiya Vol. 1 (2008:44-45) From this relationship Hiki gained considerable influence when Yoriie became shōgun, incurring the hostility of [[Hōjō Tokimasa]], who was instead close to Yoriie's younger brother Senman (future third shōgun [[Minamoto no Sanetomo|Sanetomo]]), and who was in his turn trying to leverage that relationship for political advantage. His childhood name was Manju (万寿). Yoriie showed when still very young great interest in military arts like fencing, and horse-riding. After his father's death in 1199, the 17-year-old became head of the [[Minamoto]] clan and was appointed [[Shōgun|''sei-i taishōgun'']] in 1202. He was, however, criticized for his abandonment of his father's policies, and his mother forbade him from any involvement political activity. On June 30, 1203 ([[Shōji (era)|Shōji]] 1, 12th day of the 4th month) his remaining powers were formally taken from him and assumed by a council of 13 elders, headed by his grandfather [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. He ordained as a Buddhist monk. Yoriie, in turn, plotted with the Hiki to subjugate the Hōjō clan; however, he failed, was put under house arrest and forced to abdicate, and was eventually assassinated on July 17, 1204 in Izu. Yoriie was succeeded by his younger brother Sanetomo, the last of the [[Seiwa Genji]] line to rule, at least nominally, over Kamakura. ==Hiki Yoshikazu's rebellion== {{main|Hiki Yoshikazu}} Seriously ill, Yoriie proposed to name both his younger brother [[Minamoto no Sanetomo|Sanetomo]], and his young son (Hiki's grandson) [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] to succeed him; the two would split power, governing separate parts of the country. It seemed natural to them that Hiki would then be the regent, even if unofficially, of young Ichiman. Hiki suggested to Yoriie, who would be assassinated shortly afterwards by a separate faction (the [[Hōjō clan]]), that they arrange to have Sanetomo killed. [[Hōjō Masako]], Yoriie's mother and wife of the first shōgun Yoritomo, allegedly overheard the conversation. On a pretext, [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] invited Hiki Yoshikazu to his home and assassinated him. A battle between the clans ensued, the Hiki were defeated by a coalition of the Hōjō, [[Wada clan|Wada]], [[Miura clan|Miura]] and [[Hatakeyama clan]]s and were exterminated. Yoriie died in Shuzenji, a small town in what was later called [[Izu Province]], assassinated by his uncle [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. ==Three sons of Yoriie== Yoriie had three sons, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]], [[Kugyō (Minamoto no Yoshinari)|Kugyō]], Senju-maru and he also had one daughter, Minamoto no Yoshiko married to fourth shōgun of [[Kamakura]], [[Kujō Yoritsune]]. But all of his son died violent deaths, victims of the power struggle that followed Yoritomo's sudden death. Ichiman (1198–1203) was the eldest. His mother Wakasa no Tsubone was Hiki Yoshikazu's daughter, and the child was brought up by the Hiki clan. There are contrasting versions of his death, but in any case, he died in the fire that destroyed the Hiki residence. His second son Yoshinari, the only one of the three to reach adulthood, was forced to become a Buddhist monk (''bonze'') and in 1219 murdered his uncle Sanetomo on the stone stairs at Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū in the shogunal capital of Kamakura, an act for which he was himself slain on the same day.Kusumoto (2002: 70–73) Third son Senju-maru ({{nihongo2|千寿丸}}, 1201–1214) was 12 when Izumi Chikahira rebelled against the Hōjō to make the child ''shōgun''. After Chikahira's defeat, the child was forced to become a Buddhist monk like his older brother [[Minamoto no Yoshinari|Yoshinari]]. A year later [[Wada Yoshimori]] also rebelled but, like Chikahira, was defeated and Senju-maru died with the others of the Wada clan. ==Family== * Father: [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] * Mother: [[Hōjō Masako]] * Wife: Wakasa no Tsubone (d. 1203) * Concubine: ** Ishina no Tsubone ** Asuke no Tsubone * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] By Wakasa ** Minamoto no Yoshiko or Take no Gosho (1202–1234) married [[Kujō Yoritsune]] by Wakasa ** [[Kugyō (priest)|Kugyō]] by Asuke no Tsubone ==Eras of Yoriie's ''bakufu''== The years in which Yoriie was shōgun are all within only one [[Japanese era names|era name]] or ''[[nengō]]'': ''[[Kennin]]'' (1201–1204). ==Notes== [[Image:Minamoto no Yoriies grave 2007-02-25.jpg|thumb|right|Minamoto no Yoriie's grave in Shuzenji, [[Izu, Shizuoka|Izu]]]] {{Reflist}} ==References== * {{cite book | last = Kamiya | first = Michinori | title = Fukaku Aruku: Kamakura Shiseki Sansaku Vol. 1 & 2 | publisher = Kamakura Shunshūsha | year = 2008 | location = Kamakura | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-7740-0340-5 | oclc = 169992721 }} * {{cite book | last = Kusumoto | first = Katsuji | title = Kamakura Naruhodo Jiten | publisher = Jitsugyō no Nihonsha | date = July 2002 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-408-00779-3 }} * [[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]] and Käthe Roth. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&client=firefox-a ''Japan encyclopedia''.] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/58053128?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 58053128] * [[Edmond Papinot|Papinot, Edmond]]. (1906) ''Dictionnaire d'histoire et de géographie du japon''. Tokyo: Librarie Sansaisha.[http://www.unterstein.net/Toyoashihara-no-Chiaki-Nagaioaki-no-Mitsuho-no-Kuni/NobiliaireJapon.pdf ..Click link for digitized 1906 ''Nobiliaire du japon'' (2003)] * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). ''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''.] Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. [http://www.worldcat.org/title/nipon-o-dai-itsi-ran-ou-annales-des-empereurs-du-japon/oclc/5850691 OCLC 5850691]. * {{cite book | last = Yasuda | first = Motohisa (editor) | title = Kamakura, Muromachi Jinmei Jiten | publisher = Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha | year = 1990 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-404-01757-4 | oclc = 24654085 }} ==External links== * [http://www.kcn-net.org/e_kama_history/omachi/omachi_1.htm Ōmachi], by the Kamakura Citizen's Net, accessed on September 30, 2008 {{s-start}} {{succession box | title=''[[Shōgun]]'':
Minamoto no Yoriie | before=[[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] | after=[[Minamoto no Sanetomo]] | years=1202–1203}} {{s-end}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Minamoto no, Yoriie}} [[Category:1182 births]] [[Category:1204 deaths]] [[Category:12th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:13th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:13th-century shōguns]] [[Category:Kamakura shōguns]] [[Category:Minamoto clan]] [[Category:People of Heian-period Japan]] [[Category:People of Kamakura-period Japan]] [[Category:Kamakura period Buddhist monks]] [[Category:Assassinated Japanese people]]

His second son Yoshinari, the only one of the three to reach adulthood, was forced to become a Buddhist monk (''bonze'') and in 1219 murdered his uncle Sanetomo on the stone stairs at Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū in the shogunal capital of Kamakura, an act for which he was himself slain on the same day.Kusumoto (2002: 70–73) Third son Senju-maru ({{nihongo2|千寿丸}}, 1201–1214) was 12 when Izumi Chikahira rebelled against the Hōjō to make the child ''shōgun''. After Chikahira's defeat, the child was forced to become a Buddhist monk like his older brother [[Minamoto no Yoshinari|Yoshinari]]. A year later [[Wada Yoshimori]] also rebelled but, like Chikahira, was defeated and Senju-maru died with the others of the Wada clan. ==Family== * Father: [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] * Mother: [[Hōjō Masako]] * Wife: Wakasa no Tsubone (d. 1203) * Concubine: ** Ishina no Tsubone ** Asuke no Tsubone * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] By Wakasa ** Minamoto no Yoshiko or Take no Gosho (1202–1234) married [[Kujō Yoritsune]] by Wakasa ** Eijitsu (1201–1215) by Ishina no Tsubone ** Zengyo (d. 1220) by Ishina no Tsubone ** [[Kugyō (priest)|Kugyō]] by Asuke no Tsubone ==Eras of Yoriie's ''bakufu''== The years in which Yoriie was shōgun are all within only one [[Japanese era names|era name]] or ''[[nengō]]'': ''[[Kennin]]'' (1201–1204). ==Notes== [[Image:Minamoto no Yoriies grave 2007-02-25.jpg|thumb|right|Minamoto no Yoriie's grave in Shuzenji, [[Izu, Shizuoka|Izu]]]] {{Reflist}} ==References== * {{cite book | last = Kamiya | first = Michinori | title = Fukaku Aruku: Kamakura Shiseki Sansaku Vol. 1 & 2 | publisher = Kamakura Shunshūsha | year = 2008 | location = Kamakura | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-7740-0340-5 | oclc = 169992721 }} * {{cite book | last = Kusumoto | first = Katsuji | title = Kamakura Naruhodo Jiten | publisher = Jitsugyō no Nihonsha | date = July 2002 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-408-00779-3 }} * [[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]] and Käthe Roth. {{family name hatnote|Minamoto|lang=Japanese}} [[File:Minamoto no Yoriie.jpg|thumb|right|Minamoto no Yoriie]] {{nihongo|'''Minamoto no Yoriie'''|源 頼家||September 11, 1182 – August 14, 1204|lead=yes}} was the second ''[[shōgun]]'' (1202–1203) of [[Japan]]'s [[Kamakura shogunate]], and the first son of first shōgun [[Minamoto no Yoritomo|Yoritomo]].[[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). "Minamoto no Yoriie" in {{Google books|p2QnPijAEmEC|''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 635|page=635}}. His buddhist name was Hokke-in-dono Kingo Da'i Zengo (法華院殿金吾大禅閤). == Life == Born to Tokimasa's daughter [[Hōjō Masako]] at [[Hiki Yoshikazu]]'s residence in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], Yoriie had as wet nurses the wives of powerful men such as Hiki himself and [[Kajiwara Kagetoki]], and also Hiki's younger sister.Yasuda (1990:592-593) Before he was born, his father Yoritomo had [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] and his men carry stones to build the [[Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū#Wakamiya .C5.8Cji|Dankazura]] on [[Wakamiya Ōji]] to pray for the child's safe delivery. When Yoriie later himself had an heir, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]], the child was also born at the Hiki mansionThe mansion no longer exists and its location ({{coord|35|19|1.31|N|139|33|20.95|E|region:JP_type:landmark_scale:1500|display=inline}}) is occupied by [[Myōhon-ji]] a temple dedicated to the memory of the Hiki clan, exterminated by the [[Hōjō clan]]. to Hiki's daughter Wakasa no Tsubone, a fact which further consolidated an already strong emotional bond.Kamiya Vol. 1 (2008:44-45) From this relationship Hiki gained considerable influence when Yoriie became shōgun, incurring the hostility of [[Hōjō Tokimasa]], who was instead close to Yoriie's younger brother Senman (future third shōgun [[Minamoto no Sanetomo|Sanetomo]]), and who was in his turn trying to leverage that relationship for political advantage. His childhood name was Manju (万寿). Yoriie showed when still very young great interest in military arts like fencing, and horse-riding. After his father's death in 1199, the 17-year-old became head of the [[Minamoto]] clan and was appointed [[Shōgun|''sei-i taishōgun'']] in 1202. He was, however, criticized for his abandonment of his father's policies, and his mother forbade him from any involvement political activity. On June 30, 1203 ([[Shōji (era)|Shōji]] 1, 12th day of the 4th month) his remaining powers were formally taken from him and assumed by a council of 13 elders, headed by his grandfather [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. He ordained as a Buddhist monk. Yoriie, in turn, plotted with the Hiki to subjugate the Hōjō clan; however, he failed, was put under house arrest and forced to abdicate, and was eventually assassinated on July 17, 1204 in Izu. Yoriie was succeeded by his younger brother Sanetomo, the last of the [[Seiwa Genji]] line to rule, at least nominally, over Kamakura. ==Hiki Yoshikazu's rebellion== {{main|Hiki Yoshikazu}} Seriously ill, Yoriie proposed to name both his younger brother [[Minamoto no Sanetomo|Sanetomo]], and his young son (Hiki's grandson) [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] to succeed him; the two would split power, governing separate parts of the country. It seemed natural to them that Hiki would then be the regent, even if unofficially, of young Ichiman. Hiki suggested to Yoriie, who would be assassinated shortly afterwards by a separate faction (the [[Hōjō clan]]), that they arrange to have Sanetomo killed. [[Hōjō Masako]], Yoriie's mother and wife of the first shōgun Yoritomo, allegedly overheard the conversation. On a pretext, [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] invited Hiki Yoshikazu to his home and assassinated him. A battle between the clans ensued, the Hiki were defeated by a coalition of the Hōjō, [[Wada clan|Wada]], [[Miura clan|Miura]] and [[Hatakeyama clan]]s and were exterminated. Yoriie died in Shuzenji, a small town in what was later called [[Izu Province]], assassinated by his uncle [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. ==Three sons of Yoriie== Yoriie had three sons, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]], [[Kugyō (Minamoto no Yoshinari)|Kugyō]], Senju-maru and he also had one daughter, Minamoto no Yoshiko married to fourth shōgun of [[Kamakura]], [[Kujō Yoritsune]]. But all of his son died violent deaths, victims of the power struggle that followed Yoritomo's sudden death. Ichiman (1198–1203) was the eldest. His mother Wakasa no Tsubone was Hiki Yoshikazu's daughter, and the child was brought up by the Hiki clan. There are contrasting versions of his death, but in any case, he died in the fire that destroyed the Hiki residence. His second son Yoshinari, the only one of the three to reach adulthood, was forced to become a Buddhist monk (''bonze'') and in 1219 murdered his uncle Sanetomo on the stone stairs at Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū in the shogunal capital of Kamakura, an act for which he was himself slain on the same day.Kusumoto (2002: 70–73) Third son Senju-maru ({{nihongo2|千寿丸}}, 1201–1214) was 12 when Izumi Chikahira rebelled against the Hōjō to make the child ''shōgun''. After Chikahira's defeat, the child was forced to become a Buddhist monk like his older brother [[Minamoto no Yoshinari|Yoshinari]]. A year later [[Wada Yoshimori]] also rebelled but, like Chikahira, was defeated and Senju-maru died with the others of the Wada clan. ==Family== * Father: [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] * Mother: [[Hōjō Masako]] * Wife: Wakasa no Tsubone (d. 1203) * Concubine: ** Ishina no Tsubone ** Asuke no Tsubone * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] By Wakasa ==Eras of Yoriie's ''bakufu''== The years in which Yoriie was shōgun are all within only one [[Japanese era names|era name]] or ''[[nengō]]'': ''[[Kennin]]'' (1201–1204). ==Notes== [[Image:Minamoto no Yoriies grave 2007-02-25.jpg|thumb|right|Minamoto no Yoriie's grave in Shuzenji, [[Izu, Shizuoka|Izu]]]] {{Reflist}} ==References== * {{cite book | last = Kamiya | first = Michinori | title = Fukaku Aruku: Kamakura Shiseki Sansaku Vol. 1 & 2 | publisher = Kamakura Shunshūsha | year = 2008 | location = Kamakura | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-7740-0340-5 | oclc = 169992721 }} * {{cite book | last = Kusumoto | first = Katsuji | title = Kamakura Naruhodo Jiten | publisher = Jitsugyō no Nihonsha | date = July 2002 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-408-00779-3 }} * [[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]] and Käthe Roth. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&client=firefox-a ''Japan encyclopedia''.] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/58053128?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 58053128] * [[Edmond Papinot|Papinot, Edmond]]. (1906) ''Dictionnaire d'histoire et de géographie du japon''. Tokyo: Librarie Sansaisha.[http://www.unterstein.net/Toyoashihara-no-Chiaki-Nagaioaki-no-Mitsuho-no-Kuni/NobiliaireJapon.pdf ..Click link for digitized 1906 ''Nobiliaire du japon'' (2003)] * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). ''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''.] Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. [http://www.worldcat.org/title/nipon-o-dai-itsi-ran-ou-annales-des-empereurs-du-japon/oclc/5850691 OCLC 5850691]. * {{cite book | last = Yasuda | first = Motohisa (editor) | title = Kamakura, Muromachi Jinmei Jiten | publisher = Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha | year = 1990 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-404-01757-4 | oclc = 24654085 }} ==External links== * [http://www.kcn-net.org/e_kama_history/omachi/omachi_1.htm Ōmachi], by the Kamakura Citizen's Net, accessed on September 30, 2008 {{s-start}} {{succession box | title=''[[Shōgun]]'':

His mother Wakasa no Tsubone was Hiki Yoshikazu's daughter, and the child was brought up by the Hiki clan. There are contrasting versions of his death, but in any case, he died in the fire that destroyed the Hiki residence. His second son Yoshinari, the only one of the three to reach adulthood, was forced to become a Buddhist monk (''bonze'') and in 1219 murdered his uncle Sanetomo on the stone stairs at Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū in the shogunal capital of Kamakura, an act for which he was himself slain on the same day.Kusumoto (2002: 70–73) Third son Senju-maru ({{nihongo2|千寿丸}}, 1201–1214) was 12 when Izumi Chikahira rebelled against the Hōjō to make the child ''shōgun''. After Chikahira's defeat, the child was forced to become a Buddhist monk like his older brother [[Minamoto no Yoshinari|Yoshinari]]. A year later [[Wada Yoshimori]] also rebelled but, like Chikahira, was defeated and Senju-maru died with the others of the Wada clan. ==Family== * Father: [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] * Mother: [[Hōjō Masako]] * Wife: Wakasa no Tsubone (d. 1203) * Concubine: ** Ishina no Tsubone ** Asuke no Tsubone * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] ** [[Kugyo|priest]] ==Eras of Yoriie's ''bakufu''== The years in which Yoriie was shōgun are all within only one [[Japanese era names|era name]] or ''[[nengō]]'': ''[[Kennin]]'' (1201–1204). ==Notes== [[Image:Minamoto no Yoriies grave 2007-02-25.jpg|thumb|right|Minamoto no Yoriie's grave in Shuzenji, [[Izu, Shizuoka|Izu]]]] {{Reflist}} ==References== * {{cite book | last = Kamiya | first = Michinori | title = Fukaku Aruku: Kamakura Shiseki Sansaku Vol. 1 & 2 | publisher = Kamakura Shunshūsha | year = 2008 | location = Kamakura | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-7740-0340-5 | oclc = 169992721 }} * {{cite book | last = Kusumoto | first = Katsuji | title = Kamakura Naruhodo Jiten | publisher = Jitsugyō no Nihonsha | date = July 2002 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-408-00779-3 }} * [[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]] and Käthe Roth. {{family name hatnote|Minamoto|lang=Japanese}} [[File:Minamoto no Yoriie.jpg|thumb|right|Minamoto no Yoriie]] {{nihongo|'''Minamoto no Yoriie'''|源 頼家||September 11, 1182 – August 14, 1204|lead=yes}} was the second ''[[shōgun]]'' (1202–1203) of [[Japan]]'s [[Kamakura shogunate]], and the first son of first shōgun [[Minamoto no Yoritomo|Yoritomo]].[[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). "Minamoto no Yoriie" in {{Google books|p2QnPijAEmEC|''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 635|page=635}}. His buddhist name was Hokke-in-dono Kingo Da'i Zengo (法華院殿金吾大禅閤). == Life == Born to Tokimasa's daughter [[Hōjō Masako]] at [[Hiki Yoshikazu]]'s residence in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], Yoriie had as wet nurses the wives of powerful men such as Hiki himself and [[Kajiwara Kagetoki]], and also Hiki's younger sister.Yasuda (1990:592-593) Before he was born, his father Yoritomo had [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] and his men carry stones to build the [[Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū#Wakamiya .C5.8Cji|Dankazura]] on [[Wakamiya Ōji]] to pray for the child's safe delivery. When Yoriie later himself had an heir, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]], the child was also born at the Hiki mansionThe mansion no longer exists and its location ({{coord|35|19|1.31|N|139|33|20.95|E|region:JP_type:landmark_scale:1500|display=inline}}) is occupied by [[Myōhon-ji]] a temple dedicated to the memory of the Hiki clan, exterminated by the [[Hōjō clan]]. to Hiki's daughter Wakasa no Tsubone, a fact which further consolidated an already strong emotional bond.Kamiya Vol. 1 (2008:44-45) From this relationship Hiki gained considerable influence when Yoriie became shōgun, incurring the hostility of [[Hōjō Tokimasa]], who was instead close to Yoriie's younger brother Senman (future third shōgun [[Minamoto no Sanetomo|Sanetomo]]), and who was in his turn trying to leverage that relationship for political advantage. His childhood name was Manju (万寿). Yoriie showed when still very young great interest in military arts like fencing, and horse-riding. After his father's death in 1199, the 17-year-old became head of the [[Minamoto]] clan and was appointed [[Shōgun|''sei-i taishōgun'']] in 1202. He was, however, criticized for his abandonment of his father's policies, and his mother forbade him from any involvement political activity. On June 30, 1203 ([[Shōji (era)|Shōji]] 1, 12th day of the 4th month) his remaining powers were formally taken from him and assumed by a council of 13 elders, headed by his grandfather [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. He ordained as a Buddhist monk. Yoriie, in turn, plotted with the Hiki to subjugate the Hōjō clan; however, he failed, was put under house arrest and forced to abdicate, and was eventually assassinated on July 17, 1204 in Izu. Yoriie was succeeded by his younger brother Sanetomo, the last of the [[Seiwa Genji]] line to rule, at least nominally, over Kamakura. ==Hiki Yoshikazu's rebellion== {{main|Hiki Yoshikazu}} Seriously ill, Yoriie proposed to name both his younger brother [[Minamoto no Sanetomo|Sanetomo]], and his young son (Hiki's grandson) [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] to succeed him; the two would split power, governing separate parts of the country. It seemed natural to them that Hiki would then be the regent, even if unofficially, of young Ichiman. Hiki suggested to Yoriie, who would be assassinated shortly afterwards by a separate faction (the [[Hōjō clan]]), that they arrange to have Sanetomo killed. [[Hōjō Masako]], Yoriie's mother and wife of the first shōgun Yoritomo, allegedly overheard the conversation. On a pretext, [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] invited Hiki Yoshikazu to his home and assassinated him. A battle between the clans ensued, the Hiki were defeated by a coalition of the Hōjō, [[Wada clan|Wada]], [[Miura clan|Miura]] and [[Hatakeyama clan]]s and were exterminated. Yoriie died in Shuzenji, a small town in what was later called [[Izu Province]], assassinated by his uncle [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. ==Three sons of Yoriie== Yoriie had three sons, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]], [[Kugyō (Minamoto no Yoshinari)|Kugyō]], Senju-maru and he also had one daughter, Minamoto no Yoshiko married to fourth shōgun of [[Kamakura]], [[Kujō Yoritsune]]. But all of his son died violent deaths, victims of the power struggle that followed Yoritomo's sudden death. Ichiman (1198–1203) was the eldest. His mother Wakasa no Tsubone was Hiki Yoshikazu's daughter, and the child was brought up by the Hiki clan. There are contrasting versions of his death, but in any case, he died in the fire that destroyed the Hiki residence. His second son Yoshinari, the only one of the three to reach adulthood, was forced to become a Buddhist monk (''bonze'') and in 1219 murdered his uncle Sanetomo on the stone stairs at Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū in the shogunal capital of Kamakura, an act for which he was himself slain on the same day.Kusumoto (2002: 70–73) Third son Senju-maru ({{nihongo2|千寿丸}}, 1201–1214) was 12 when Izumi Chikahira rebelled against the Hōjō to make the child ''shōgun''. After Chikahira's defeat, the child was forced to become a Buddhist monk like his older brother [[Minamoto no Yoshinari|Yoshinari]]. A year later [[Wada Yoshimori]] also rebelled but, like Chikahira, was defeated and Senju-maru died with the others of the Wada clan. ==Family== * Father: [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] * Mother: [[Hōjō Masako]] * Wife: Wakasa no Tsubone (d. 1203) * Concubine: ** Ishina no Tsubone ** Asuke no Tsubone * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] ** [[Kugyō (priest)|Kugyō]] ==Eras of Yoriie's ''bakufu''== The years in which Yoriie was shōgun are all within only one [[Japanese era names|era name]] or ''[[nengō]]'': ''[[Kennin]]'' (1201–1204). ==Notes== [[Image:Minamoto no Yoriies grave 2007-02-25.jpg|thumb|right|Minamoto no Yoriie's grave in Shuzenji, [[Izu, Shizuoka|Izu]]]] {{Reflist}} ==References== * {{cite book | last = Kamiya | first = Michinori | title = Fukaku Aruku: Kamakura Shiseki Sansaku Vol. 1 & 2 | publisher = Kamakura Shunshūsha | year = 2008 | location = Kamakura | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-7740-0340-5 | oclc = 169992721 }} * {{cite book | last = Kusumoto | first = Katsuji | title = Kamakura Naruhodo Jiten | publisher = Jitsugyō no Nihonsha | date = July 2002 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-408-00779-3 }} * [[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]] and Käthe Roth. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&client=firefox-a ''Japan encyclopedia''.] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/58053128?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 58053128] * [[Edmond Papinot|Papinot, Edmond]]. (1906) ''Dictionnaire d'histoire et de géographie du japon''. Tokyo: Librarie Sansaisha.[http://www.unterstein.net/Toyoashihara-no-Chiaki-Nagaioaki-no-Mitsuho-no-Kuni/NobiliaireJapon.pdf ..Click link for digitized 1906 ''Nobiliaire du japon'' (2003)] * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). ''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''.] Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. [[Hōjō Masako]], Yoriie's mother and wife of the first shōgun Yoritomo, allegedly overheard the conversation. On a pretext, [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] invited Hiki Yoshikazu to his home and assassinated him. Yoriie died in Shuzenji, a small town in what was later called [[Izu Province]], assassinated by his uncle [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. ==Three sons of Yoriie== Yoriie had three sons, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]], [[Kugyō (Minamoto no Yoshinari)|Kugyō]], Senju-maru and he also had one daughter, Minamoto no Yoshiko married to fourth shōgun of [[Kamakura]], [[Kujō Yoritsune]]. But all of his son died violent deaths, victims of the power struggle that followed Yoritomo's sudden death. Ichiman (1198–1203) was the eldest. His mother Wakasa no Tsubone was Hiki Yoshikazu's daughter, and the child was brought up by the Hiki clan. There are contrasting versions of his death, but in any case, he died in the fire that destroyed the Hiki residence. His second son Yoshinari, the only one of the three to reach adulthood, was forced to become a Buddhist monk (''bonze'') and in 1219 murdered his uncle Sanetomo on the stone stairs at Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū in the shogunal capital of Kamakura, an act for which he was himself slain on the same day.Kusumoto (2002: 70–73) Third son Senju-maru ({{nihongo2|千寿丸}}, 1201–1214) was 12 when Izumi Chikahira rebelled against the Hōjō to make the child ''shōgun''. After Chikahira's defeat, the child was forced to become a Buddhist monk like his older brother [[Minamoto no Yoshinari|Yoshinari]]. A year later [[Wada Yoshimori]] also rebelled but, like Chikahira, was defeated and Senju-maru died with the others of the Wada clan. ==Family== * Father: [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] * Mother: [[Hōjō Masako]] * Wife: Wakasa no Tsubone (d. 1203) * Concubine: ** Ishina no Tsubone ** Asuke no Tsubone * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] ** [[Kugyō (priest)|Kugyō]] ==Eras of Yoriie's ''bakufu''== The years in which Yoriie was shōgun are all within only one [[Japanese era names|era name]] or ''[[nengō]]'': ''[[Kennin]]'' (1201–1204). ==Notes== [[Image:Minamoto no Yoriies grave 2007-02-25.jpg|thumb|right|Minamoto no Yoriie's grave in Shuzenji, [[Izu, Shizuoka|Izu]]]] {{Reflist}} ==References== * {{cite book | last = Kamiya | first = Michinori | title = Fukaku Aruku: Kamakura Shiseki Sansaku Vol. 1 & 2 | publisher = Kamakura Shunshūsha | year = 2008 | location = Kamakura | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-7740-0340-5 | oclc = 169992721 }} * {{cite book | last = Kusumoto | first = Katsuji | title = Kamakura Naruhodo Jiten | publisher = Jitsugyō no Nihonsha | date = July 2002 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-408-00779-3 }} * [[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]] and Käthe Roth. {{family name hatnote|Minamoto|lang=Japanese}} [[File:Minamoto no Yoriie.jpg|thumb|right|Minamoto no Yoriie]] {{nihongo|'''Minamoto no Yoriie'''|源 頼家||September 11, 1182 – August 14, 1204|lead=yes}} was the second ''[[shōgun]]'' (1202–1203) of [[Japan]]'s [[Kamakura shogunate]], and the first son of first shōgun [[Minamoto no Yoritomo|Yoritomo]].[[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). "Minamoto no Yoriie" in {{Google books|p2QnPijAEmEC|''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 635|page=635}}. His buddhist name was Hokke-in-dono Kingo Da'i Zengo (法華院殿金吾大禅閤). == Life == Born to Tokimasa's daughter [[Hōjō Masako]] at [[Hiki Yoshikazu]]'s residence in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], Yoriie had as wet nurses the wives of powerful men such as Hiki himself and [[Kajiwara Kagetoki]], and also Hiki's younger sister.Yasuda (1990:592-593) Before he was born, his father Yoritomo had [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] and his men carry stones to build the [[Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū#Wakamiya .C5.8Cji|Dankazura]] on [[Wakamiya Ōji]] to pray for the child's safe delivery. When Yoriie later himself had an heir, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]], the child was also born at the Hiki mansionThe mansion no longer exists and its location ({{coord|35|19|1.31|N|139|33|20.95|E|region:JP_type:landmark_scale:1500|display=inline}}) is occupied by [[Myōhon-ji]] a temple dedicated to the memory of the Hiki clan, exterminated by the [[Hōjō clan]]. to Hiki's daughter Wakasa no Tsubone, a fact which further consolidated an already strong emotional bond.Kamiya Vol. 1 (2008:44-45) From this relationship Hiki gained considerable influence when Yoriie became shōgun, incurring the hostility of [[Hōjō Tokimasa]], who was instead close to Yoriie's younger brother Senman (future third shōgun [[Minamoto no Sanetomo|Sanetomo]]), and who was in his turn trying to leverage that relationship for political advantage. His childhood name was Manju (万寿). Yoriie showed when still very young great interest in military arts like fencing, and horse-riding. After his father's death in 1199, the 17-year-old became head of the [[Minamoto]] clan and was appointed [[Shōgun|''sei-i taishōgun'']] in 1202. He was, however, criticized for his abandonment of his father's policies, and his mother forbade him from any involvement political activity. On June 30, 1203 ([[Shōji (era)|Shōji]] 1, 12th day of the 4th month) his remaining powers were formally taken from him and assumed by a council of 13 elders, headed by his grandfather [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. He ordained as a Buddhist monk. Yoriie, in turn, plotted with the Hiki to subjugate the Hōjō clan; however, he failed, was put under house arrest and forced to abdicate, and was eventually assassinated on July 17, 1204 in Izu. Yoriie was succeeded by his younger brother Sanetomo, the last of the [[Seiwa Genji]] line to rule, at least nominally, over Kamakura. ==Hiki Yoshikazu's rebellion== {{main|Hiki Yoshikazu}} Seriously ill, Yoriie proposed to name both his younger brother [[Minamoto no Sanetomo|Sanetomo]], and his young son (Hiki's grandson) [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] to succeed him; the two would split power, governing separate parts of the country. It seemed natural to them that Hiki would then be the regent, even if unofficially, of young Ichiman. Hiki suggested to Yoriie, who would be assassinated shortly afterwards by a separate faction (the [[Hōjō clan]]), that they arrange to have Sanetomo killed. [[Hōjō Masako]], Yoriie's mother and wife of the first shōgun Yoritomo, allegedly overheard the conversation. On a pretext, [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] invited Hiki Yoshikazu to his home and assassinated him. A battle between the clans ensued, the Hiki were defeated by a coalition of the Hōjō, [[Wada clan|Wada]], [[Miura clan|Miura]] and [[Hatakeyama clan]]s and were exterminated. Yoriie died in Shuzenji, a small town in what was later called [[Izu Province]], assassinated by his uncle [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. ==Three sons of Yoriie== Yoriie had three sons, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]], [[Kugyō (Minamoto no Yoshinari)|Kugyō]], Senju-maru and he also had one daughter, Minamoto no Yoshiko married to fourth shōgun of [[Kamakura]], [[Kujō Yoritsune]]. But all of his son died violent deaths, victims of the power struggle that followed Yoritomo's sudden death. Ichiman (1198–1203) was the eldest. His mother Wakasa no Tsubone was Hiki Yoshikazu's daughter, and the child was brought up by the Hiki clan. There are contrasting versions of his death, but in any case, he died in the fire that destroyed the Hiki residence. His second son Yoshinari, the only one of the three to reach adulthood, was forced to become a Buddhist monk (''bonze'') and in 1219 murdered his uncle Sanetomo on the stone stairs at Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū in the shogunal capital of Kamakura, an act for which he was himself slain on the same day.Kusumoto (2002: 70–73) Third son Senju-maru ({{nihongo2|千寿丸}}, 1201–1214) was 12 when Izumi Chikahira rebelled against the Hōjō to make the child ''shōgun''. After Chikahira's defeat, the child was forced to become a Buddhist monk like his older brother [[Minamoto no Yoshinari|Yoshinari]]. A year later [[Wada Yoshimori]] also rebelled but, like Chikahira, was defeated and Senju-maru died with the others of the Wada clan. ==Family== * Father: [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] * Mother: [[Hōjō Masako]] * Wife: Wakasa no Tsubone (d. 1203) * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] ** [[Kugyō (priest)|Kugyō]] ==Eras of Yoriie's ''bakufu''== The years in which Yoriie was shōgun are all within only one [[Japanese era names|era name]] or ''[[nengō]]'': ''[[Kennin]]'' (1201–1204). ==Notes== [[Image:Minamoto no Yoriies grave 2007-02-25.jpg|thumb|right|Minamoto no Yoriie's grave in Shuzenji, [[Izu, Shizuoka|Izu]]]] {{Reflist}} ==References== * {{cite book | last = Kamiya | first = Michinori | title = Fukaku Aruku: Kamakura Shiseki Sansaku Vol. 1 & 2 | publisher = Kamakura Shunshūsha | year = 2008 | location = Kamakura | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-7740-0340-5 | oclc = 169992721 }} * {{cite book | last = Kusumoto | first = Katsuji | title = Kamakura Naruhodo Jiten | publisher = Jitsugyō no Nihonsha | date = July 2002 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-408-00779-3 }} * [[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]] and Käthe Roth. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&client=firefox-a ''Japan encyclopedia''.] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/58053128?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 58053128] * [[Edmond Papinot|Papinot, Edmond]]. Tokyo: Librarie Sansaisha.[http://www.unterstein.net/Toyoashihara-no-Chiaki-Nagaioaki-no-Mitsuho-no-Kuni/NobiliaireJapon.pdf ..Click link for digitized 1906 ''Nobiliaire du japon'' (2003)] * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). ''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''.] Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. His mother Wakasa no Tsubone was Hiki Yoshikazu's daughter, and the child was brought up by the Hiki clan. There are contrasting versions of his death, but in any case, he died in the fire that destroyed the Hiki residence. And his second son Yoshinari, who was the only one to reached adulthood, was forced to become a Buddhist monk (''bonze'') and in 1219 murdered his uncle Sanetomo on the stone stairs at Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū in the shogunal capital of Kamakura, an act for which he was himself slain on the same day.Kusumoto (2002: 70–73) ==Family== * Father: [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] * Mother: [[Hōjō Masako]] * Wife: Wakasa no Tsubone (d. 1203) * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] ** [[Kugyō (priest)|Kugyō]] ==Eras of Yoriie's ''bakufu''== The years in which Yoriie was shōgun are all within only one [[Japanese era names|era name]] or ''[[nengō]]'': ''[[Kennin]]'' (1201–1204). ==Notes== [[Image:Minamoto no Yoriies grave 2007-02-25.jpg|thumb|right|Minamoto no Yoriie's grave in Shuzenji, [[Izu, Shizuoka|Izu]]]] {{Reflist}} ==References== * {{cite book | last = Kamiya | first = Michinori | title = Fukaku Aruku: Kamakura Shiseki Sansaku Vol. 1 & 2 | publisher = Kamakura Shunshūsha | year = 2008 | location = Kamakura | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-7740-0340-5 | oclc = 169992721 }} * {{cite book | last = Kusumoto | first = Katsuji | title = Kamakura Naruhodo Jiten | publisher = Jitsugyō no Nihonsha | date = July 2002 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-408-00779-3 }} * [[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]] and Käthe Roth. {{family name hatnote|Minamoto|lang=Japanese}} [[File:Minamoto no Yoriie.jpg|thumb|right|Minamoto no Yoriie]] {{nihongo|'''Minamoto no Yoriie'''|源 頼家||September 11, 1182 – August 14, 1204|lead=yes}} was the second ''[[shōgun]]'' (1202–1203) of [[Japan]]'s [[Kamakura shogunate]], and the first son of first shōgun [[Minamoto no Yoritomo|Yoritomo]].[[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). "Minamoto no Yoriie" in {{Google books|p2QnPijAEmEC|''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 635|page=635}}. His buddhist name was Hokke-in-dono Kingo Da'i Zengo (法華院殿金吾大禅閤). == Life == Minamoto no Yoriie was born to [[Hōjō Masako]] at [[Hiki Yoshikazu]]'s residence in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]].Yasuda (1990:592-593) Before he was born, his father Yoritomo had [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] and his men carry stones to build the [[Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū#Wakamiya .C5.8Cji|Dankazura]] on [[Wakamiya Ōji]] to pray for the child's safe delivery. When Yoriie later himself had an heir, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]], the child was also born at the Hiki mansionThe mansion no longer exists and its location ({{coord|35|19|1.31|N|139|33|20.95|E|region:JP_type:landmark_scale:1500|display=inline}}) is occupied by [[Myōhon-ji]] a temple dedicated to the memory of the Hiki clan, exterminated by the [[Hōjō clan]]. to Hiki's daughter Wakasa no Tsubone, a fact which further consolidated an already strong emotional bond.Kamiya Vol. 1 (2008:44-45) From this relationship Hiki gained considerable influence when Yoriie became shōgun, incurring the hostility of [[Hōjō Tokimasa]], who was instead close to Yoriie's younger brother Senman (future third shōgun [[Minamoto no Sanetomo|Sanetomo]]), and who was in his turn trying to leverage that relationship for political advantage. His childhood name was Manju (万寿). When he was young, he was interested in military arts like fencing, and horse-riding. After his father's death in 1199, the 17-year-old became head of the [[Minamoto]] clan and was appointed [[Shōgun|''sei-i taishōgun'']] in 1202. He was, however, criticized for his abandonment of his father's policies, and his mother forbade him from any political activity. On June 30, 1203, his remaining powers were formally taken from him and assumed by a council of 13 elders, headed by his grandfather [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. He ordained as a Buddhist monk. Yoriie, in turn, plotted with the Hiki to subjugate the Hōjō clan. However, he failed, and was put under house arrest, where he was forced to abdicate. Then in July 17, 1204, he was assassinated in [[Izu]]. Yoriie was succeeded by his younger brother Sanetomo, the last of the [[Minamoto]] to rule over [[Kamakura]]. ==Hiki Yoshikazu's rebellion== {{main|Hiki Yoshikazu}} Seriously ill, Yoriie proposed to name both his younger brother [[Minamoto no Sanetomo|Sanetomo]], and his young son (Hiki's grandson) [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] to succeed him; the two would split power, governing separate parts of the country. It seemed natural to them that Hiki would then be the regent, even if unofficially, of young Ichiman. Hiki suggested to Yoriie, who would be assassinated shortly afterwards by a separate faction (the [[Hōjō clan]]), that they arrange to have Sanetomo killed. [[Hōjō Masako]], Yoriie's mother and wife of the first shōgun Yoritomo, allegedly overheard the conversation. On a pretext, [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] invited Hiki Yoshikazu to his home and assassinated him. A battle between the clans ensued, the Hiki were defeated by a coalition of the Hōjō, [[Wada clan|Wada]], [[Miura clan|Miura]] and [[Hatakeyama clan]]s and were exterminated. Yoriie died in Shuzenji, a small town in what was later called [[Izu Province]], assassinated by his uncle [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. ==Two sons of Yoriie== Yoriie had two sons, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]], [[Kugyō (Minamoto no Yoshinari)|Kugyō]], Senju-maru and he also had one daughter, Minamoto no Yoshiko married to fourth shōgun of [[Kamakura]], [[Kujō Yoritsune]]. But all of his son died violent deaths, victims of the power struggle that followed Yoritomo's sudden death. Ichiman (1198–1203) was the eldest. His mother Wakasa no Tsubone was Hiki Yoshikazu's daughter, and the child was brought up by the Hiki clan. There are contrasting versions of his death, but in any case, he died in the fire that destroyed the Hiki residence. And his second son Yoshinari, who was the only one to reached adulthood, was forced to become a Buddhist monk (''bonze'') and in 1219 murdered his uncle Sanetomo on the stone stairs at Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū in the shogunal capital of Kamakura, an act for which he was himself slain on the same day.Kusumoto (2002: 70–73) ==Family== * Father: [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] * Mother: [[Hōjō Masako]] * Wife: Wakasa no Tsubone (d. 1203) * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] ** [[Kugyō (priest)|Kugyō]] ==Eras of Yoriie's ''bakufu''== The years in which Yoriie was shōgun are all within only one [[Japanese era names|era name]] or ''[[nengō]]'': ''[[Kennin]]'' (1201–1204). ==Notes== [[Image:Minamoto no Yoriies grave 2007-02-25.jpg|thumb|right|Minamoto no Yoriie's grave in Shuzenji, [[Izu, Shizuoka|Izu]]]] {{Reflist}} ==References== * {{cite book | last = Kamiya | first = Michinori | title = Fukaku Aruku: Kamakura Shiseki Sansaku Vol. 1 & 2 | publisher = Kamakura Shunshūsha | year = 2008 | location = Kamakura | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-7740-0340-5 | oclc = 169992721 }} * {{cite book | last = Kusumoto | first = Katsuji | title = Kamakura Naruhodo Jiten | publisher = Jitsugyō no Nihonsha | date = July 2002 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-408-00779-3 }} * [[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]] and Käthe Roth. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&client=firefox-a ''Japan encyclopedia''.] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/58053128?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 58053128] * [[Edmond Papinot|Papinot, Edmond]]. (1906) ''Dictionnaire d'histoire et de géographie du japon''. Tokyo: Librarie Sansaisha.[http://www.unterstein.net/Toyoashihara-no-Chiaki-Nagaioaki-no-Mitsuho-no-Kuni/NobiliaireJapon.pdf ..Click link for digitized 1906 ''Nobiliaire du japon'' (2003)] * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). ''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''.] Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. [http://www.worldcat.org/title/nipon-o-dai-itsi-ran-ou-annales-des-empereurs-du-japon/oclc/5850691 OCLC 5850691]. * {{cite book | last = Yasuda | first = Motohisa (editor) | title = Kamakura, Muromachi Jinmei Jiten | publisher = Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha | year = 1990 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-404-01757-4 | oclc = 24654085 }} ==External links== * [http://www.kcn-net.org/e_kama_history/omachi/omachi_1.htm Ōmachi], by the Kamakura Citizen's Net, accessed on September 30, 2008 {{s-start}} {{succession box | title=''[[Shōgun]]'':

[https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&client=firefox-a ''Japan encyclopedia''.] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/58053128?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 58053128] * [[Edmond Papinot|Papinot, Edmond]]. (1906) ''Dictionnaire d'histoire et de géographie du japon''. Tokyo: Librarie Sansaisha.[http://www.unterstein.net/Toyoashihara-no-Chiaki-Nagaioaki-no-Mitsuho-no-Kuni/NobiliaireJapon.pdf ..Click link for digitized 1906 ''Nobiliaire du japon'' (2003)] * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). ''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''.] Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. [http://www.worldcat.org/title/nipon-o-dai-itsi-ran-ou-annales-des-empereurs-du-japon/oclc/5850691 OCLC 5850691]. * {{cite book | last = Yasuda | first = Motohisa (editor) | title = Kamakura, Muromachi Jinmei Jiten | publisher = Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha | year = 1990 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-404-01757-4 | oclc = 24654085 }} ==External links== * [http://www.kcn-net.org/e_kama_history/omachi/omachi_1.htm Ōmachi], by the Kamakura Citizen's Net, accessed on September 30, 2008 {{s-start}} {{succession box | title=''[[Shōgun]]'':
