{{Short description|Shogun of the Kamakura shogunate (r. 1202–03)}} {{family name hatnote|Minamoto|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox officeholder | name = Minamoto no Yoriie
{{lang|ja|源頼家}} | image = Minamoto no Yoriie.jpg | caption = | office = ''[[Shōgun]]'' | monarch = [[Emperor Tsuchimikado|Tsuchimikado]] | predecessor = [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] | successor = [[Minamoto no Sanetomo]] | birth_date = {{birth date|1182|9|11}} | birth_place = [[Kamakura, Kanagawa]], [[Japan]] | death_date = {{death date and age|1204|8|14|1182|9|11}} | death_place = | spouse = Wakasa no Tsubone | children = {{Plainlist| * [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] * [[Kugyō (priest)|Kugyō]] * Eijitsu * Zengyō * Take no gosho}} | term_start =1202 | term_end = 1203 | father = [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] | mother = [[Hōjō Masako]] | signature = }} {{nihongo|'''Minamoto no Yoriie'''|源 頼家||September 11, 1182 – August 14, 1204|lead=yes}} was the second ''[[shōgun]]'' (1202–1203) of [[Japan]]'s [[Kamakura shogunate]], and the first son of first shōgun [[Minamoto no Yoritomo|Yoritomo]].[[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). "Minamoto no Yoriie" in {{Google books|p2QnPijAEmEC|''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 635|page=635}}. His [[Dharma name]] was Hokke-in-dono Kingo Da'i Zengo (法華院殿金吾大禅閤). == Life == Minamoto no Yoriie was born to [[Hōjō Masako]] at [[Hiki Yoshikazu]]'s residence in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]].Yasuda (1990:592-593) Before he was born, his father Yoritomo had [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] and his men carry stones to build the [[Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū#Wakamiya .C5.8Cji|Dankazura]] on [[Wakamiya Ōji]] to pray for the child's safe delivery. When Yoriie later himself had an heir, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]], the child was also born at the Hiki mansionThe mansion no longer exists and its location ({{coord|35|19|1.31|N|139|33|20.95|E|region:JP_type:landmark_scale:1500|display=inline}}) is occupied by [[Myōhon-ji]] a temple dedicated to the memory of the Hiki clan, exterminated by the [[Hōjō clan]]. to Hiki's daughter Wakasa no Tsubone, a fact which further consolidated an already strong emotional bond.Kamiya Vol. 1 (2008:44-45) From this relationship [[Hiki clan|Hiki]] gained considerable influence when Yoriie became shōgun, incurring the hostility of [[Hōjō Tokimasa]], who was instead close to Yoriie's younger brother Senman (future third shōgun [[Minamoto no Sanetomo|Sanetomo]]), and who was in his turn trying to leverage that relationship for political advantage. His childhood name was Manju (万寿). When he was young, he was interested in military arts like fencing, and horse-riding. After his father's death in 1199, the 17-year-old became head of the [[Minamoto]] clan and was appointed [[Shōgun|''sei-i taishōgun'']] in 1202. He was, however, criticized for his abandonment of his father's policies, and his mother forbade him from any political activity. On June 30, 1203, his remaining powers were formally taken from him and assumed by a council of 13 elders, headed by his grandfather [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. He ordained as a Buddhist monk. Yoriie, in turn, plotted with the Hiki to subjugate the [[Hōjō clan|Hōjō]] clan. However, he failed, and was put under house arrest, where he was forced to abdicate. Then on July 17, 1204, he was assassinated in his mansion in Izu. Yoriie was succeeded by his younger brother Sanetomo, the last of the [[Minamoto]] shogun to rule over [[Kamakura]]. ==Hiki Yoshikazu's rebellion== {{main|Hiki Yoshikazu}} Seriously ill, Yoriie proposed to name both his younger brother, Minamoto no Sanetomo, and his young son [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] to succeed him; the two would split power, governing separate parts of the country. It seemed natural to them that Hiki would then be the regent, even if unofficially, of young Ichiman. Hiki suggested to Yoriie that they arrange to have Sanetomo killed. [[Hōjō Masako]], Yoriie's mother and wife of the first shōgun Yoritomo, allegedly overheard the conversation. On a pretext, [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] invited [[Hiki Yoshikazu]] to his home and assassinated him. A battle between the clans ensued, the Hiki were defeated by a coalition of the Hōjō, [[Wada clan|Wada]], [[Miura clan|Miura]] and [[Hatakeyama]] clans and were exterminated. Yoriie died in Shuzenji, a small town in what was later called [[Izu Province]], assassinated by his grandfather [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. ==The sons of Yoriie== Yoriie had two sons, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]] and [[Kugyō (Minamoto no Yoshinari)|Kugyō]]. Both of his sons would end up dead, like their father they would fall victim to the power struggle that followed Yoritomo's death. Ichiman (1198–1203) was the eldest. His mother, Wakasa no Tsubone, was [[Hiki Yoshikazu]]'s daughter, and the child was brought up by the [[Hiki clan]]. It is said that he died in a fire that destroyed the Hiki residence. Yoriie's second son Yoshinari, his only son to reach adulthood, was forced to become a Buddhist monk. In 1219, Yoshinari murdered his uncle Sanetomo on the stairs leading to the shrine of [[Tsurugaoka Hachimangū]] in the shogunal capital of [[Kamakura]], an act for which he was himself slain on the same day.Kusumoto (2002: 70–73) ==Family== * Father: [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] * Mother: [[Hōjō Masako]] * Wife: Wakasa no Tsubone (d. 1203) * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] ** [[Kugyō (priest)|Kugyō]] ==Eras of Yoriie's ''bakufu''== The years in which Yoriie was shōgun are all within only one [[Japanese era names|era name]] or ''[[nengō]]'': ''[[Kennin]]'' (1201–1204). ==Notes== [[Image:Minamoto no Yoriies grave 2007-02-25.jpg|thumb|right|Minamoto no Yoriie's grave in Shuzenji, [[Izu, Shizuoka|Izu]]]] {{Reflist}} ==References== * {{cite book | last = Kamiya | first = Michinori | title = Fukaku Aruku: Kamakura Shiseki Sansaku Vol. 1 & 2 | publisher = Kamakura Shunshūsha | year = 2008 | location = Kamakura | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-7740-0340-5 | oclc = 169992721 }} * {{cite book | last = Kusumoto | first = Katsuji | title = Kamakura Naruhodo Jiten | publisher = Jitsugyō no Nihonsha | date = July 2002 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-408-00779-3 }} * [[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]] and Käthe Roth. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC ''Japan encyclopedia''.] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/58053128?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 58053128] * [[Edmond Papinot|Papinot, Edmond]]. (1906) ''Dictionnaire d'histoire et de géographie du japon''. Tokyo: Librarie Sansaisha.[http://www.unterstein.net/Toyoashihara-no-Chiaki-Nagaioaki-no-Mitsuho-no-Kuni/NobiliaireJapon.pdf ..Click link for digitized 1906 ''Nobiliaire du japon'' (2003)] * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). ''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''.] Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/5850691 OCLC 5850691]. * {{cite book | editor-last = Yasuda | editor-first = Motohisa | title = Kamakura, Muromachi Jinmei Jiten | publisher = Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha | year = 1990 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-404-01757-4 | oclc = 24654085 }} ==External links== * [http://www.kcn-net.org/e_kama_history/omachi/omachi_1.htm Ōmachi], by the Kamakura Citizen's Net, accessed on September 30, 2008 {{s-start}} {{succession box | title=''[[Shōgun]]'':

{{Short description|Shogun of the Kamakura shogunate (r. 1202–03)}} {{family name hatnote|Minamoto|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox officeholder | name = Minamoto no Yoriie
{{lang|ja|源頼家}} | image = Minamoto no Yoriie.jpg | caption = | office = ''[[Shōgun]]'' | monarch = [[Emperor Tsuchimikado|Tsuchimikado]] | predecessor = [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] | successor = [[Minamoto no Sanetomo]] | birth_date = {{birth date|1182|9|11}} | birth_place = [[Kamakura, Kanagawa]], [[Japan]] | death_date = {{death date and age|1204|8|14|1182|9|11}} | death_place = | spouse = Wakasa no Tsubone | children = {{Plainlist| * [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] * [[Kugyō (priest)|Kugyō]] * Eijitsu * Zengyō * Take no gosho}} | term_start =1202 | term_end = 1203 | father = [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] | mother = [[Hōjō Masako]] | signature = }} {{nihongo|'''Minamoto no Yoriie'''|源 頼家||September 11, 1182 – August 14, 1204|lead=yes}} was the second ''[[shōgun]]'' (1202–1203) of [[Japan]]'s [[Kamakura shogunate]], and the first son of first shōgun [[Minamoto no Yoritomo|Yoritomo]].[[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). "Minamoto no Yoriie" in {{Google books|p2QnPijAEmEC|''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 635|page=635}}. His [[Dharma name]] was Hokke-in-dono Kingo Da'i Zengo (法華院殿金吾大禅閤). == Life == Minamoto no Yoriie was born to [[Hōjō Masako]] at [[Hiki Yoshikazu]]'s residence in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]].Yasuda (1990:592-593) Before he was born, his father Yoritomo had [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] and his men carry stones to build the [[Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū#Wakamiya .C5.8Cji|Dankazura]] on [[Wakamiya Ōji]] to pray for the child's safe delivery. When Yoriie later himself had an heir, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]], the child was also born at the Hiki mansionThe mansion no longer exists and its location ({{coord|35|19|1.31|N|139|33|20.95|E|region:JP_type:landmark_scale:1500|display=inline}}) is occupied by [[Myōhon-ji]] a temple dedicated to the memory of the Hiki clan, exterminated by the [[Hōjō clan]]. to Hiki's daughter Wakasa no Tsubone, a fact which further consolidated an already strong emotional bond.Kamiya Vol. 1 (2008:44-45) From this relationship [[Hiki clan|Hiki]] gained considerable influence when Yoriie became shōgun, incurring the hostility of [[Hōjō Tokimasa]], who was instead close to Yoriie's younger brother Senman (future third shōgun [[Minamoto no Sanetomo|Sanetomo]]), and who was in his turn trying to leverage that relationship for political advantage. His childhood name was Manju (万寿). When he was young, he was interested in military arts like fencing, and horse-riding. After his father's death in 1199, the 17-year-old became head of the [[Minamoto]] clan and was appointed [[Shōgun|''sei-i taishōgun'']] in 1202. He was, however, criticized for his abandonment of his father's policies, and his mother forbade him from any political activity. On June 30, 1203, his remaining powers were formally taken from him and assumed by a council of 13 elders, headed by his grandfather [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. He ordained as a Buddhist monk. Yoriie, in turn, plotted with the Hiki to subjugate the [[Hōjō clan|Hōjō]] clan. However, he failed, and was put under house arrest, where he was forced to abdicate. Then on July 17, 1204, he was assassinated in his mansion in Izu. Yoriie was succeeded by his younger brother Sanetomo, the last of the [[Minamoto]] shogun to rule over [[Kamakura]]. ==Hiki Yoshikazu's rebellion== {{main|Hiki Yoshikazu}} Seriously ill, Yoriie proposed to name both his younger brother, Minamoto no Sanetomo, and his young son [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] to succeed him; the two would split power, governing separate parts of the country. It seemed natural to them that Hiki would then be the regent, even if unofficially, of young Ichiman. Hiki suggested to Yoriie that they arrange to have Sanetomo killed. [[Hōjō Masako]], Yoriie's mother and wife of the first shōgun Yoritomo, allegedly overheard the conversation. On a pretext, [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] invited [[Hiki Yoshikazu]] to his home and assassinated him. A battle between the clans ensued, the Hiki were defeated by a coalition of the Hōjō, [[Wada clan|Wada]], [[Miura clan|Miura]] and [[Hatakeyama]] clans and were exterminated. Yoriie died in Shuzenji, a small town in what was later called [[Izu Province]], assassinated by his grandfather [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. ==The sons of Yoriie== Yoriie had two sons, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]] and [[Kugyō (Minamoto no Yoshinari)|Kugyō]]. Both of his sons would end up dead, like their father they would fall victim to the power struggle that followed Yoritomo's death. Ichiman (1198–1203) was the eldest. His mother, Wakasa no Tsubone, was [[Hiki Yoshikazu]]'s daughter, and the child was brought up by the [[Hiki clan]]. It is said that he died in a fire that destroyed the Hiki residence. Yoriie's second son Yoshinari, his only son to reach adulthood, was forced to become a Buddhist monk. In 1219, Yoshinari murdered his uncle Sanetomo on the stairs leading to the shrine of [[Tsurugaoka Hachimangū]] in the shogunal capital of [[Kamakura]], an act for which he was himself slain on the same day.Kusumoto (2002: 70–73) ==Family== * Father: [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] * Mother: [[Hōjō Masako]] * Wife: Wakasa no Tsubone (d. 1203) * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] ** [[Kugyō (priest)|Kugyō]] ==Eras of Yoriie's ''bakufu''== The years in which Yoriie was shōgun are all within only one [[Japanese era names|era name]] or ''[[nengō]]'': ''[[Kennin]]'' (1201–1204). ==Notes== [[Image:Minamoto no Yoriies grave 2007-02-25.jpg|thumb|right|Minamoto no Yoriie's grave in Shuzenji, [[Izu, Shizuoka|Izu]]]] {{Reflist}} ==References== * {{cite book | last = Kamiya | first = Michinori | title = Fukaku Aruku: Kamakura Shiseki Sansaku Vol. 1 & 2 | publisher = Kamakura Shunshūsha | year = 2008 | location = Kamakura | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-7740-0340-5 | oclc = 169992721 }} * {{cite book | last = Kusumoto | first = Katsuji | title = Kamakura Naruhodo Jiten | publisher = Jitsugyō no Nihonsha | date = July 2002 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-408-00779-3 }} * [[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]] and Käthe Roth. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC ''Japan encyclopedia''.] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/58053128?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 58053128] * [[Edmond Papinot|Papinot, Edmond]]. (1906) ''Dictionnaire d'histoire et de géographie du japon''. Tokyo: Librarie Sansaisha.[http://www.unterstein.net/Toyoashihara-no-Chiaki-Nagaioaki-no-Mitsuho-no-Kuni/NobiliaireJapon.pdf ..Click link for digitized 1906 ''Nobiliaire du japon'' (2003)] * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). ''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''.] Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/5850691 OCLC 5850691]. * {{cite book | editor-last = Yasuda | editor-first = Motohisa | title = Kamakura, Muromachi Jinmei Jiten | publisher = Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha | year = 1990 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-404-01757-4 | oclc = 24654085 }} ==External links== * [http://www.kcn-net.org/e_kama_history/omachi/omachi_1.htm Ōmachi], by the Kamakura Citizen's Net, accessed on September 30, 2008 {{s-start}} {{succession box | title=''[[Shōgun]]'':

{{Short description|Shogun of the Kamakura shogunate (r. 1202–03)}} {{family name hatnote|Minamoto|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox officeholder | name = Minamoto no Yoriie
{{lang|ja|源頼家}} | image = Minamoto no Yoriie.jpg | caption = | office = ''[[Shōgun]]'' | monarch = [[Emperor Tsuchimikado|Tsuchimikado]] | predecessor = [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] | successor = [[Minamoto no Sanetomo]] | birth_date = {{birth date|1182|9|11}} | birth_place = [[Kamakura, Kanagawa]], [[Japan]] | death_date = {{death date and age|1204|8|14|1182|9|11}} | death_place = | spouse = Wakasa no Tsubone | children = {{Plainlist| * [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] * [[Kugyō (priest)|Kugyō]] * Eijitsu * Zengyō * Take no gosho}} | term_start =1202 | term_end = 1203 | father = [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] | mother = [[Hōjō Masako]] | signature = }} {{nihongo|'''Minamoto no Yoriie'''|源 頼家||September 11, 1182 – August 14, 1204|lead=yes}} was the second ''[[shōgun]]'' (1202–1203) of [[Japan]]'s [[Kamakura shogunate]], and the first son of first shōgun [[Minamoto no Yoritomo|Yoritomo]].[[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). "Minamoto no Yoriie" in {{Google books|p2QnPijAEmEC|''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 635|page=635}}. His [[Dharma name]] was Hokke-in-dono Kingo Da'i Zengo (法華院殿金吾大禅閤). == Life == Minamoto no Yoriie was born to [[Hōjō Masako]] at [[Hiki Yoshikazu]]'s residence in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]].Yasuda (1990:592-593) Before he was born, his father Yoritomo had [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] and his men carry stones to build the [[Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū#Wakamiya .C5.8Cji|Dankazura]] on [[Wakamiya Ōji]] to pray for the child's safe delivery. When Yoriie later himself had an heir, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]], the child was also born at the Hiki mansionThe mansion no longer exists and its location ({{coord|35|19|1.31|N|139|33|20.95|E|region:JP_type:landmark_scale:1500|display=inline}}) is occupied by [[Myōhon-ji]] a temple dedicated to the memory of the Hiki clan, exterminated by the [[Hōjō clan]]. to Hiki's daughter Wakasa no Tsubone, a fact which further consolidated an already strong emotional bond.Kamiya Vol. 1 (2008:44-45) From this relationship [[Hiki clan|Hiki]] gained considerable influence when Yoriie became shōgun, incurring the hostility of [[Hōjō Tokimasa]], who was instead close to Yoriie's younger brother Senman (future third shōgun [[Minamoto no Sanetomo|Sanetomo]]), and who was in his turn trying to leverage that relationship for political advantage. His childhood name was Manju (万寿). When he was young, he was interested in military arts like fencing, and horse-riding. After his father's death in 1199, the 17-year-old became head of the [[Minamoto]] clan and was appointed [[Shōgun|''sei-i taishōgun'']] in 1202. He was, however, criticized for his abandonment of his father's policies, and his mother forbade him from any political activity. On June 30, 1203, his remaining powers were formally taken from him and assumed by a council of 13 elders, headed by his grandfather [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. He ordained as a Buddhist monk. Yoriie, in turn, plotted with the Hiki to subjugate the [[Hōjō clan|Hōjō]] clan. However, he failed, and was put under house arrest, where he was forced to abdicate. Then on July 17, 1204, he was assassinated in his mansion in Izu. Yoriie was succeeded by his younger brother Sanetomo, the last of the [[Minamoto]] shogun to rule over [[Kamakura]]. ==Hiki Yoshikazu's rebellion== {{main|Hiki Yoshikazu}} Seriously ill, Yoriie proposed to name both his younger brother, Minamoto no Sanetomo, and his young son [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] to succeed him; the two would split power, governing separate parts of the country. It seemed natural to them that Hiki would then be the regent, even if unofficially, of young Ichiman. Hiki suggested to Yoriie that they arrange to have Sanetomo killed. [[Hōjō Masako]], Yoriie's mother and wife of the first shōgun Yoritomo, allegedly overheard the conversation. On a pretext, [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] invited [[Hiki Yoshikazu]] to his home and assassinated him. A battle between the clans ensued, the Hiki were defeated by a coalition of the Hōjō, [[Wada clan|Wada]], [[Miura clan|Miura]] and [[Hatakeyama]] clans and were exterminated. Yoriie died in Shuzenji, a small town in what was later called [[Izu Province]], assassinated by his grandfather [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. ==The sons of Yoriie== Yoriie had two sons, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]] and [[Kugyō (Minamoto no Yoshinari)|Kugyō]]. Both of his sons would end up dead, like their father they would fall victim to the power struggle that followed Yoritomo's death. Ichiman (1198–1203) was the eldest. His mother, Wakasa no Tsubone, was [[Hiki Yoshikazu]]'s daughter, and the child was brought up by the [[Hiki clan]]. It is said that he died in a fire that destroyed the Hiki residence. Yoriie's second son Yoshinari, his only son to reach adulthood, was forced to become a Buddhist monk. In 1219, Yoshinari murdered his uncle Sanetomo on the stairs leading to the shrine of [[Tsurugaoka Hachimangū]] in the shogunal capital of [[Kamakura]], an act for which he was himself slain on the same day.Kusumoto (2002: 70–73) ==Family== * Father: [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] * Mother: [[Hōjō Masako]] * Wife: Wakasa no Tsubone (d. 1203) * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] ** [[Kugyō (priest)|Kugyō]] ==Eras of Yoriie's ''bakufu''== The years in which Yoriie was shōgun are all within only one [[Japanese era names|era name]] or ''[[nengō]]'': ''[[Kennin]]'' (1201–1204). ==Notes== [[Image:Minamoto no Yoriies grave 2007-02-25.jpg|thumb|right|Minamoto no Yoriie's grave in Shuzenji, [[Izu, Shizuoka|Izu]]]] {{Reflist}} ==References== * {{cite book | last = Kamiya | first = Michinori | title = Fukaku Aruku: Kamakura Shiseki Sansaku Vol. 1 & 2 | publisher = Kamakura Shunshūsha | year = 2008 | location = Kamakura | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-7740-0340-5 | oclc = 169992721 }} * {{cite book | last = Kusumoto | first = Katsuji | title = Kamakura Naruhodo Jiten | publisher = Jitsugyō no Nihonsha | date = July 2002 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-408-00779-3 }} * [[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]] and Käthe Roth. {{Short description|Shogun of the Kamakura shogunate (r. 1202–03)}} {{family name hatnote|Minamoto|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox officeholder | name = Minamoto no Yoriie
{{lang|ja|源頼家}} | image = Minamoto no Yoriie.jpg | caption = | office = ''[[Shōgun]]'' | monarch = [[Emperor Tsuchimikado|Tsuchimikado]] | predecessor = [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] | successor = [[Minamoto no Sanetomo]] | birth_date = {{birth date|1182|9|11}} | birth_place = [[Kamakura, Kanagawa]], [[Japan]] | death_date = {{death date and age|1204|8|14|1182|9|11}} | death_place = | spouse = Wakasa no Tsubone | children = {{Plainlist| * [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] * [[Kugyō (priest)|Kugyō]] * Eijitsu * Zengyō * Take no gosho}} | term_start =1202 | term_end = 1203 | father = [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] | mother = [[Hōjō Masako]] | signature = }} {{nihongo|'''Minamoto no Yoriie'''|源 頼家||September 11, 1182 – August 14, 1204|lead=yes}} was the second ''[[shōgun]]'' (1202–1203) of [[Japan]]'s [[Kamakura shogunate]], and the first son of first shōgun [[Minamoto no Yoritomo|Yoritomo]].[[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). "Minamoto no Yoriie" in {{Google books|p2QnPijAEmEC|''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 635|page=635}}. His [[Dharma name]] was Hokke-in-dono Kingo Da'i Zengo (法華院殿金吾大禅閤). == Life == Minamoto no Yoriie was born to [[Hōjō Masako]] at [[Hiki Yoshikazu]]'s residence in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]].Yasuda (1990:592-593) Before he was born, his father Yoritomo had [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] and his men carry stones to build the [[Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū#Wakamiya .C5.8Cji|Dankazura]] on [[Wakamiya Ōji]] to pray for the child's safe delivery. When Yoriie later himself had an heir, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]], the child was also born at the Hiki mansionThe mansion no longer exists and its location ({{coord|35|19|1.31|N|139|33|20.95|E|region:JP_type:landmark_scale:1500|display=inline}}) is occupied by [[Myōhon-ji]] a temple dedicated to the memory of the Hiki clan, exterminated by the [[Hōjō clan]]. to Hiki's daughter Wakasa no Tsubone, a fact which further consolidated an already strong emotional bond.Kamiya Vol. 1 (2008:44-45) From this relationship [[Hiki clan|Hiki]] gained considerable influence when Yoriie became shōgun, incurring the hostility of [[Hōjō Tokimasa]], who was instead close to Yoriie's younger brother Senman (future third shōgun [[Minamoto no Sanetomo|Sanetomo]]), and who was in his turn trying to leverage that relationship for political advantage. His childhood name was Manju (万寿). When he was young, he was interested in military arts like fencing, and horse-riding. After his father's death in 1199, the 17-year-old became head of the [[Minamoto]] clan and was appointed [[Shōgun|''sei-i taishōgun'']] in 1202. He was, however, criticized for his abandonment of his father's policies, and his mother forbade him from any political activity. On June 30, 1203, his remaining powers were formally taken from him and assumed by a council of 13 elders, headed by his grandfather [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. He ordained as a Buddhist monk. Yoriie, in turn, plotted with the Hiki to subjugate the [[Hōjō clan|Hōjō]] clan. However, he failed, and was put under house arrest, where he was forced to abdicate. Then on July 17, 1204, he was assassinated in his mansion in Izu. Yoriie was succeeded by his younger brother Sanetomo, the last of the [[Minamoto]] shogun to rule over [[Kamakura]]. ==Hiki Yoshikazu's rebellion== {{main|Hiki Yoshikazu}} Seriously ill, Yoriie proposed to name both his younger brother, Minamoto no Sanetomo, and his young son [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] to succeed him; the two would split power, governing separate parts of the country. It seemed natural to them that Hiki would then be the regent, even if unofficially, of young Ichiman. Hiki suggested to Yoriie that they arrange to have Sanetomo killed. [[Hōjō Masako]], Yoriie's mother and wife of the first shōgun Yoritomo, allegedly overheard the conversation. On a pretext, [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] invited [[Hiki Yoshikazu]] to his home and assassinated him. A battle between the clans ensued, the Hiki were defeated by a coalition of the Hōjō, [[Wada clan|Wada]], [[Miura clan|Miura]] and [[Hatakeyama]] clans and were exterminated. Yoriie died in Shuzenji, a small town in what was later called [[Izu Province]], assassinated by his grandfather [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. ==The sons of Yoriie== Yoriie had two sons, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]] and [[Kugyō (Minamoto no Yoshinari)|Kugyō]]. Both of his sons would end up dead, like their father they would fall victim to the power struggle that followed Yoritomo's death. Ichiman (1198–1203) was the eldest. His mother, Wakasa no Tsubone, was [[Hiki Yoshikazu]]'s daughter, and the child was brought up by the [[Hiki clan]]. It is said that he died in a fire that destroyed the Hiki residence. Yoriie's second son Yoshinari, his only son to reach adulthood, was forced to become a Buddhist monk. In 1219, Yoshinari murdered his uncle Sanetomo on the stairs leading to the shrine of [[Tsurugaoka Hachimangū]] in the shogunal capital of [[Kamakura]], an act for which he was himself slain on the same day.Kusumoto (2002: 70–73) ==Family== * Father: [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] * Mother: [[Hōjō Masako]] * Wife: Wakasa no Tsubone (d. 1203) * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] ** [[Kugyō (priest)|Kugyō]] ==Eras of Yoriie's ''bakufu''== The years in which Yoriie was shōgun are all within only one [[Japanese era names|era name]] or ''[[nengō]]'': ''[[Kennin]]'' (1201–1204). ==Notes== [[Image:Minamoto no Yoriies grave 2007-02-25.jpg|thumb|right|Minamoto no Yoriie's grave in Shuzenji, [[Izu, Shizuoka|Izu]]]] {{Reflist}} ==References== * {{cite book | last = Kamiya | first = Michinori | title = Fukaku Aruku: Kamakura Shiseki Sansaku Vol. 1 & 2 | publisher = Kamakura Shunshūsha | year = 2008 | location = Kamakura | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-7740-0340-5 | oclc = 169992721 }} * {{cite book | last = Kusumoto | first = Katsuji | title = Kamakura Naruhodo Jiten | publisher = Jitsugyō no Nihonsha | date = July 2002 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-408-00779-3 }} * [[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]] and Käthe Roth. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC ''Japan encyclopedia''.] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/58053128?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 58053128] * [[Edmond Papinot|Papinot, Edmond]]. (1906) ''Dictionnaire d'histoire et de géographie du japon''. Tokyo: Librarie Sansaisha.[http://www.unterstein.net/Toyoashihara-no-Chiaki-Nagaioaki-no-Mitsuho-no-Kuni/NobiliaireJapon.pdf ..Click link for digitized 1906 ''Nobiliaire du japon'' (2003)] * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). ''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''.] Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/5850691 OCLC 5850691]. * {{cite book | editor-last = Yasuda | editor-first = Motohisa | title = Kamakura, Muromachi Jinmei Jiten | publisher = Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha | year = 1990 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-404-01757-4 | oclc = 24654085 }} ==External links== * [http://www.kcn-net.org/e_kama_history/omachi/omachi_1.htm Ōmachi], by the Kamakura Citizen's Net, accessed on September 30, 2008 {{s-start}} {{succession box | title=''[[Shōgun]]'':

On a pretext, [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] invited [[Hiki Yoshikazu]] to his home and assassinated him. Yoriie died in Shuzenji, a small town in what was later called [[Izu Province]], assassinated by his grandfather [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. ==The sons of Yoriie== Yoriie had two sons, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]] and [[Kugyō (Minamoto no Yoshinari)|Kugyō]]. Both of his sons would end up dead, like their father they would fall victim to the power struggle that followed Yoritomo's death. Ichiman (1198–1203) was the eldest. His mother, Wakasa no Tsubone, was [[Hiki Yoshikazu]]'s daughter, and the child was brought up by the [[Hiki clan]]. It is said that he died in a fire that destroyed the Hiki residence. Yoriie's second son Yoshinari, his only son to reach adulthood, was forced to become a Buddhist monk. In 1219, Yoshinari murdered his uncle Sanetomo on the stairs leading to the shrine of [[Tsurugaoka Hachimangū]] in the shogunal capital of [[Kamakura]], an act for which he was himself slain on the same day.Kusumoto (2002: 70–73) ==Family== * Father: [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] * Mother: [[Hōjō Masako]] * Wife: Wakasa no Tsubone (d. 1203) * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] ** [[Kugyō (priest)|Kugyō]] ==Eras of Yoriie's ''bakufu''== The years in which Yoriie was shōgun are all within only one [[Japanese era names|era name]] or ''[[nengō]]'': ''[[Kennin]]'' (1201–1204). ==Notes== [[Image:Minamoto no Yoriies grave 2007-02-25.jpg|thumb|right|Minamoto no Yoriie's grave in Shuzenji, [[Izu, Shizuoka|Izu]]]] {{Reflist}} ==References== * {{cite book | last = Kamiya | first = Michinori | title = Fukaku Aruku: Kamakura Shiseki Sansaku Vol. 1 & 2 | publisher = Kamakura Shunshūsha | year = 2008 | location = Kamakura | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-7740-0340-5 | oclc = 169992721 }} * {{cite book | last = Kusumoto | first = Katsuji | title = Kamakura Naruhodo Jiten | publisher = Jitsugyō no Nihonsha | date = July 2002 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-408-00779-3 }} * [[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]] and Käthe Roth. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC ''Japan encyclopedia''.] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/58053128?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 58053128] * [[Edmond Papinot|Papinot, Edmond]]. (1906) ''Dictionnaire d'histoire et de géographie du japon''. Tokyo: Librarie Sansaisha.[http://www.unterstein.net/Toyoashihara-no-Chiaki-Nagaioaki-no-Mitsuho-no-Kuni/NobiliaireJapon.pdf ..Click link for digitized 1906 ''Nobiliaire du japon'' (2003)] * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). ''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''.] Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/5850691 OCLC 5850691]. * {{cite book | editor-last = Yasuda | editor-first = Motohisa | title = Kamakura, Muromachi Jinmei Jiten | publisher = Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha | year = 1990 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-404-01757-4 | oclc = 24654085 }} ==External links== * [http://www.kcn-net.org/e_kama_history/omachi/omachi_1.htm Ōmachi], by the Kamakura Citizen's Net, accessed on September 30, 2008 {{s-start}} {{succession box | title=''[[Shōgun]]'':

{{Short description|Shogun of the Kamakura shogunate (r. 1202–03)}} {{family name hatnote|Minamoto|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox officeholder | name = Minamoto no Yoriie
{{lang|ja|源頼家}} | image = Minamoto no Yoriie.jpg | caption = | office = ''[[Shōgun]]'' | monarch = [[Emperor Tsuchimikado|Tsuchimikado]] | predecessor = [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] | successor = [[Minamoto no Sanetomo]] | birth_date = {{birth date|1182|9|11}} | birth_place = [[Kamakura, Kanagawa]], [[Japan]] | death_date = {{death date and age|1204|8|14|1182|9|11}} | death_place = | spouse = Wakasa no Tsubone | children = {{Plainlist| * [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] * [[Kugyō (priest)|Kugyō]] * Eijitsu * Zengyō * Take no gosho}} | term_start =1202 | term_end = 1203 | father = [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] | mother = [[Hōjō Masako]] | signature = }} {{nihongo|'''Minamoto no Yoriie'''|源 頼家||September 11, 1182 – August 14, 1204|lead=yes}} was the second ''[[shōgun]]'' (1202–1203) of [[Japan]]'s [[Kamakura shogunate]], and the first son of first shōgun [[Minamoto no Yoritomo|Yoritomo]].[[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). "Minamoto no Yoriie" in {{Google books|p2QnPijAEmEC|''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 635|page=635}}. His [[Dharma name]] was Hokke-in-dono Kingo Da'i Zengo (法華院殿金吾大禅閤). == Life == Minamoto no Yoriie was born to [[Hōjō Masako]] at [[Hiki Yoshikazu]]'s residence in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]].Yasuda (1990:592-593) Before he was born, his father Yoritomo had [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] and his men carry stones to build the [[Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū#Wakamiya .C5.8Cji|Dankazura]] on [[Wakamiya Ōji]] to pray for the child's safe delivery. When Yoriie later himself had an heir, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]], the child was also born at the Hiki mansionThe mansion no longer exists and its location ({{coord|35|19|1.31|N|139|33|20.95|E|region:JP_type:landmark_scale:1500|display=inline}}) is occupied by [[Myōhon-ji]] a temple dedicated to the memory of the Hiki clan, exterminated by the [[Hōjō clan]]. to Hiki's daughter Wakasa no Tsubone, a fact which further consolidated an already strong emotional bond.Kamiya Vol. 1 (2008:44-45) From this relationship [[Hiki clan|Hiki]] gained considerable influence when Yoriie became shōgun, incurring the hostility of [[Hōjō Tokimasa]], who was instead close to Yoriie's younger brother Senman (future third shōgun [[Minamoto no Sanetomo|Sanetomo]]), and who was in his turn trying to leverage that relationship for political advantage. His childhood name was Manju (万寿). When he was young, he was interested in military arts like fencing, and horse-riding. After his father's death in 1199, the 17-year-old became head of the [[Minamoto]] clan and was appointed [[Shōgun|''sei-i taishōgun'']] in 1202. He was, however, criticized for his abandonment of his father's policies, and his mother forbade him from any political activity. On June 30, 1203, his remaining powers were formally taken from him and assumed by a council of 13 elders, headed by his grandfather [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. He ordained as a Buddhist monk. Yoriie, in turn, plotted with the Hiki to subjugate the [[Hōjō clan|Hōjō]] clan. However, he failed, and was put under house arrest, where he was forced to abdicate. Then on July 17, 1204, he was assassinated in his mansion in Izu. Yoriie was succeeded by his younger brother Sanetomo, the last of the [[Minamoto]] shogun to rule over [[Kamakura]]. ==Hiki Yoshikazu's rebellion== {{main|Hiki Yoshikazu}} Seriously ill, Yoriie proposed to name both his younger brother, Minamoto no Sanetomo, and his young son [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] to succeed him; the two would split power, governing separate parts of the country. It seemed natural to them that Hiki would then be the regent, even if unofficially, of young Ichiman. Hiki suggested to Yoriie that they arrange to have Sanetomo killed. [[Hōjō Masako]], Yoriie's mother and wife of the first shōgun Yoritomo, allegedly overheard the conversation. On a pretext, [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] invited [[Hiki Yoshikazu]] to his home and assassinated him. A battle between the clans ensued, the Hiki were defeated by a coalition of the Hōjō, [[Wada clan|Wada]], [[Miura clan|Miura]] and [[Hatakeyama]] clans and were exterminated. Yoriie died in Shuzenji, a small town in what was later called [[Izu Province]], assassinated by his grandfather [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. ==The sons of Yoriie== Yoriie had two sons, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]] and [[Kugyō (Minamoto no Yoshinari)|Kugyō]]. Both of his sons would end up dead, like their father they would fall victim to the power struggle that followed Yoritomo's death. Ichiman (1198–1203) was the eldest. His mother, Wakasa no Tsubone, was [[Hiki Yoshikazu]]'s daughter, and the child was brought up by the [[Hiki clan]]. It is said that he died in a fire that destroyed the Hiki residence. Yoriie's second son Yoshinari, his only son to reach adulthood, was forced to become a Buddhist monk. In 1219, Yoshinari murdered his uncle Sanetomo on the stairs leading to the shrine of [[Tsurugaoka Hachimangū]] in the shogunal capital of [[Kamakura]], an act for which he was himself slain on the same day.Kusumoto (2002: 70–73) ==Family== * Father: [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] * Mother: [[Hōjō Masako]] * Wife: Wakasa no Tsubone (d. 1203) * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] ** [[Kugyō (priest)|Kugyō]] ==Eras of Yoriie's ''bakufu''== The years in which Yoriie was shōgun are all within only one [[Japanese era names|era name]] or ''[[nengō]]'': ''[[Kennin]]'' (1201–1204). ==Notes== [[Image:Minamoto no Yoriies grave 2007-02-25.jpg|thumb|right|Minamoto no Yoriie's grave in Shuzenji, [[Izu, Shizuoka|Izu]]]] {{Reflist}} ==References== * {{cite book | last = Kamiya | first = Michinori | title = Fukaku Aruku: Kamakura Shiseki Sansaku Vol. 1 & 2 | publisher = Kamakura Shunshūsha | year = 2008 | location = Kamakura | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-7740-0340-5 | oclc = 169992721 }} * {{cite book | last = Kusumoto | first = Katsuji | title = Kamakura Naruhodo Jiten | publisher = Jitsugyō no Nihonsha | date = July 2002 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-408-00779-3 }} * [[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]] and Käthe Roth. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC ''Japan encyclopedia''.] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/58053128?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 58053128] * [[Edmond Papinot|Papinot, Edmond]]. (1906) ''Dictionnaire d'histoire et de géographie du japon''. Tokyo: Librarie Sansaisha.[http://www.unterstein.net/Toyoashihara-no-Chiaki-Nagaioaki-no-Mitsuho-no-Kuni/NobiliaireJapon.pdf ..Click link for digitized 1906 ''Nobiliaire du japon'' (2003)] * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). ''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''.] Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/5850691 OCLC 5850691]. * {{cite book | editor-last = Yasuda | editor-first = Motohisa | title = Kamakura, Muromachi Jinmei Jiten | publisher = Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha | year = 1990 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-404-01757-4 | oclc = 24654085 }} ==External links== * [http://www.kcn-net.org/e_kama_history/omachi/omachi_1.htm Ōmachi], by the Kamakura Citizen's Net, accessed on September 30, 2008 {{s-start}} {{succession box | title=''[[Shōgun]]'':

He was, however, criticized for his abandonment of his father's policies, and his mother forbade him from any political activity. On June 30, 1203, his remaining powers were formally taken from him and assumed by a council of 13 elders, headed by his grandfather [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. He ordained as a Buddhist monk. Yoriie, in turn, plotted with the Hiki to subjugate the [[Hōjō clan|Hōjō]] clan. However, he failed, and was put under house arrest, where he was forced to abdicate. Then on July 17, 1204, he was assassinated in his mansion in Izu. Yoriie was succeeded by his younger brother Sanetomo, the last of the [[Minamoto]] shogun to rule over [[Kamakura]]. ==Hiki Yoshikazu's rebellion== {{main|Hiki Yoshikazu}} Seriously ill, Yoriie proposed to name both his younger brother, Minamoto no Sanetomo, and his young son [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] to succeed him; the two would split power, governing separate parts of the country. It seemed natural to them that Hiki would then be the regent, even if unofficially, of young Ichiman. Hiki suggested to Yoriie that they arrange to have Sanetomo killed. [[Hōjō Masako]], Yoriie's mother and wife of the first shōgun Yoritomo, allegedly overheard the conversation. On a pretext, [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] invited [[Hiki Yoshikazu]] to his home and assassinated him. A battle between the clans ensued, the Hiki were defeated by a coalition of the Hōjō, [[Wada clan|Wada]], [[Miura clan|Miura]] and [[Hatakeyama]] clans and were exterminated. Yoriie died in Shuzenji, a small town in what was later called [[Izu Province]], assassinated by his grandfather [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. ==The sons of Yoriie== Yoriie had two sons, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]] and [[Kugyō (Minamoto no Yoshinari)|Kugyō]]. Both of his sons would end up dead, like their father they would fall victim to the power struggle that followed Yoritomo's death. Ichiman (1198–1203) was the eldest. His mother, Wakasa no Tsubone, was [[Hiki Yoshikazu]]'s daughter, and the child was brought up by the [[Hiki clan]]. It is said that he died in a fire that destroyed the Hiki residence. Yoriie's second son Yoshinari, his only son to reach adulthood, was forced to become a Buddhist monk. In 1219, Yoshinari murdered his uncle Sanetomo on the stairs leading to the shrine of [[Tsurugaoka Hachimangū]] in the shogunal capital of [[Kamakura]], an act for which he was himself slain on the same day.Kusumoto (2002: 70–73) ==Family== * Father: [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] * Mother: [[Hōjō Masako]] * Wife: Wakasa no Tsubone (d. 1203) * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] ** [[Kugyō (priest)|Kugyō]] ==Eras of Yoriie's ''bakufu''== The years in which Yoriie was shōgun are all within only one [[Japanese era names|era name]] or ''[[nengō]]'': ''[[Kennin]]'' (1201–1204). ==Notes== [[Image:Minamoto no Yoriies grave 2007-02-25.jpg|thumb|right|Minamoto no Yoriie's grave in Shuzenji, [[Izu, Shizuoka|Izu]]]] {{Reflist}} ==References== * {{cite book | last = Kamiya | first = Michinori | title = Fukaku Aruku: Kamakura Shiseki Sansaku Vol. 1 & 2 | publisher = Kamakura Shunshūsha | year = 2008 | location = Kamakura | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-7740-0340-5 | oclc = 169992721 }} * {{cite book | last = Kusumoto | first = Katsuji | title = Kamakura Naruhodo Jiten | publisher = Jitsugyō no Nihonsha | date = July 2002 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-408-00779-3 }} * [[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]] and Käthe Roth. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC ''Japan encyclopedia''.] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/58053128?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 58053128] * [[Edmond Papinot|Papinot, Edmond]]. (1906) ''Dictionnaire d'histoire et de géographie du japon''. Tokyo: Librarie Sansaisha.[http://www.unterstein.net/Toyoashihara-no-Chiaki-Nagaioaki-no-Mitsuho-no-Kuni/NobiliaireJapon.pdf ..Click link for digitized 1906 ''Nobiliaire du japon'' (2003)] * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). ''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''.] Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/5850691 OCLC 5850691]. * {{cite book | editor-last = Yasuda | editor-first = Motohisa | title = Kamakura, Muromachi Jinmei Jiten | publisher = Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha | year = 1990 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-404-01757-4 | oclc = 24654085 }} ==External links== * [http://www.kcn-net.org/e_kama_history/omachi/omachi_1.htm Ōmachi], by the Kamakura Citizen's Net, accessed on September 30, 2008 {{s-start}} {{succession box | title=''[[Shōgun]]'':

{{Short description|Shogun of the Kamakura shogunate (r. 1202–03)}} {{family name hatnote|Minamoto|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox officeholder | name = Minamoto no Yoriie
{{lang|ja|源頼家}} | image = Minamoto no Yoriie.jpg | caption = | office = ''[[Shōgun]]'' | monarch = [[Emperor Tsuchimikado|Tsuchimikado]] | predecessor = [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] | successor = [[Minamoto no Sanetomo]] | birth_date = {{birth date|1182|9|11}} | birth_place = [[Kamakura, Kanagawa]], [[Japan]] | death_date = {{death date and age|1204|8|14|1182|9|11}} | death_place = | spouse = Wakasa no Tsubone | children = {{Plainlist| * [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] * [[Kugyō (priest)|Kugyō]] * Eijitsu * Zengyō * Take no gosho}} | term_start =1202 | term_end = 1203 | father = [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] | mother = [[Hōjō Masako]] | signature = }} {{nihongo|'''Minamoto no Yoriie'''|源 頼家||September 11, 1182 – August 14, 1204|lead=yes}} was the second ''[[shōgun]]'' (1202–1203) of [[Japan]]'s [[Kamakura shogunate]], and the first son of first shōgun [[Minamoto no Yoritomo|Yoritomo]].[[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). "Minamoto no Yoriie" in {{Google books|p2QnPijAEmEC|''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 635|page=635}}. His [[Dharma name]] was Hokke-in-dono Kingo Da'i Zengo (法華院殿金吾大禅閤). == Life == Minamoto no Yoriie was born to [[Hōjō Masako]] at [[Hiki Yoshikazu]]'s residence in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]].Yasuda (1990:592-593) Before he was born, his father Yoritomo had [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] and his men carry stones to build the [[Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū#Wakamiya .C5.8Cji|Dankazura]] on [[Wakamiya Ōji]] to pray for the child's safe delivery. When Yoriie later himself had an heir, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]], the child was also born at the Hiki mansionThe mansion no longer exists and its location ({{coord|35|19|1.31|N|139|33|20.95|E|region:JP_type:landmark_scale:1500|display=inline}}) is occupied by [[Myōhon-ji]] a temple dedicated to the memory of the Hiki clan, exterminated by the [[Hōjō clan]]. to Hiki's daughter Wakasa no Tsubone, a fact which further consolidated an already strong emotional bond.Kamiya Vol. 1 (2008:44-45) From this relationship [[Hiki clan|Hiki]] gained considerable influence when Yoriie became shōgun, incurring the hostility of [[Hōjō Tokimasa]], who was instead close to Yoriie's younger brother Senman (future third shōgun [[Minamoto no Sanetomo|Sanetomo]]), and who was in his turn trying to leverage that relationship for political advantage. His childhood name was Manju (万寿). When he was young, he was interested in military arts like fencing, and horse-riding. After his father's death in 1199, the 17-year-old became head of the [[Minamoto]] clan and was appointed [[Shōgun|''sei-i taishōgun'']] in 1202. He was, however, criticized for his abandonment of his father's policies, and his mother forbade him from any political activity. On June 30, 1203, his remaining powers were formally taken from him and assumed by a council of 13 elders, headed by his grandfather [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. He ordained as a Buddhist monk. Yoriie, in turn, plotted with the Hiki to subjugate the [[Hōjō clan|Hōjō]] clan. However, he failed, and was put under house arrest, where he was forced to abdicate. Then on July 17, 1204, he was assassinated in his mansion in Izu. Yoriie was succeeded by his younger brother Sanetomo, the last of the [[Minamoto]] shogun to rule over [[Kamakura]]. ==Hiki Yoshikazu's rebellion== {{main|Hiki Yoshikazu}} Seriously ill, Yoriie proposed to name both his younger brother, Minamoto no Sanetomo, and his young son [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] to succeed him; the two would split power, governing separate parts of the country. It seemed natural to them that Hiki would then be the regent, even if unofficially, of young Ichiman. Hiki suggested to Yoriie that they arrange to have Sanetomo killed. [[Hōjō Masako]], Yoriie's mother and wife of the first shōgun Yoritomo, allegedly overheard the conversation. On a pretext, [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] invited [[Hiki Yoshikazu]] to his home and assassinated him. A battle between the clans ensued, the Hiki were defeated by a coalition of the Hōjō, [[Wada clan|Wada]], [[Miura clan|Miura]] and [[Hatakeyama]] clans and were exterminated. Yoriie died in Shuzenji, a small town in what was later called [[Izu Province]], assassinated by his grandfather [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. ==The sons of Yoriie== Yoriie had two sons, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]] and [[Kugyō (Minamoto no Yoshinari)|Kugyō]]. Both of his sons would end up dead, like their father they would fall victim to the power struggle that followed Yoritomo's death. Ichiman (1198–1203) was the eldest. His mother, Wakasa no Tsubone, was [[Hiki Yoshikazu]]'s daughter, and the child was brought up by the [[Hiki clan]]. It is said that he died in a fire that destroyed the Hiki residence. Yoriie's second son Yoshinari, his only son to reach adulthood, was forced to become a Buddhist monk. In 1219, Yoshinari murdered his uncle Sanetomo on the stairs leading to the shrine of [[Tsurugaoka Hachimangū]] in the shogunal capital of [[Kamakura]], an act for which he was himself slain on the same day.Kusumoto (2002: 70–73) ==Family== * Father: [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] * Mother: [[Hōjō Masako]] * Wife: Wakasa no Tsubone (d. 1203) * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] ** [[Kugyō (priest)|Kugyō]] ==Eras of Yoriie's ''bakufu''== The years in which Yoriie was shōgun are all within only one [[Japanese era names|era name]] or ''[[nengō]]'': ''[[Kennin]]'' (1201–1204). ==Notes== [[Image:Minamoto no Yoriies grave 2007-02-25.jpg|thumb|right|Minamoto no Yoriie's grave in Shuzenji, [[Izu, Shizuoka|Izu]]]] {{Reflist}} ==References== * {{cite book | last = Kamiya | first = Michinori | title = Fukaku Aruku: Kamakura Shiseki Sansaku Vol. 1 & 2 | publisher = Kamakura Shunshūsha | year = 2008 | location = Kamakura | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-7740-0340-5 | oclc = 169992721 }} * {{cite book | last = Kusumoto | first = Katsuji | title = Kamakura Naruhodo Jiten | publisher = Jitsugyō no Nihonsha | date = July 2002 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-408-00779-3 }} * [[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]] and Käthe Roth. [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC ''Japan encyclopedia''.] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/58053128?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 58053128] * [[Edmond Papinot|Papinot, Edmond]]. ''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''.] Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. (2005). "Minamoto no Yoriie" in {{Google books|p2QnPijAEmEC|''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 635|page=635}}. His [[Dharma name]] was Hokke-in-dono Kingo Da'i Zengo (法華院殿金吾大禅閤). == Life == Minamoto no Yoriie was born to [[Hōjō Masako]] at [[Hiki Yoshikazu]]'s residence in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]].Yasuda (1990:592-593) Before he was born, his father Yoritomo had [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] and his men carry stones to build the [[Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū#Wakamiya .C5.8Cji|Dankazura]] on [[Wakamiya Ōji]] to pray for the child's safe delivery. When Yoriie later himself had an heir, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]], the child was also born at the Hiki mansionThe mansion no longer exists and its location ({{coord|35|19|1.31|N|139|33|20.95|E|region:JP_type:landmark_scale:1500|display=inline}}) is occupied by [[Myōhon-ji]] a temple dedicated to the memory of the Hiki clan, exterminated by the [[Hōjō clan]]. to Hiki's daughter Wakasa no Tsubone, a fact which further consolidated an already strong emotional bond.Kamiya Vol. 1 (2008:44-45) From this relationship [[Hiki clan|Hiki]] gained considerable influence when Yoriie became shōgun, incurring the hostility of [[Hōjō Tokimasa]], who was instead close to Yoriie's younger brother Senman (future third shōgun [[Minamoto no Sanetomo|Sanetomo]]), and who was in his turn trying to leverage that relationship for political advantage. His childhood name was Manju (万寿). When he was young, he was interested in military arts like fencing, and horse-riding. After his father's death in 1199, the 17-year-old became head of the [[Minamoto]] clan and was appointed [[Shōgun|''sei-i taishōgun'']] in 1202. He was, however, criticized for his abandonment of his father's policies, and his mother forbade him from any political activity. On June 30, 1203, his remaining powers were formally taken from him and assumed by a council of 13 elders, headed by his grandfather [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. He ordained as a Buddhist monk. Yoriie, in turn, plotted with the Hiki to subjugate the [[Hōjō clan|Hōjō]] clan. However, he failed, and was put under house arrest, where he was forced to abdicate. Then on July 17, 1204, he was assassinated in his mansion in Izu. Yoriie was succeeded by his younger brother Sanetomo, the last of the [[Minamoto]] shogun to rule over [[Kamakura]]. ==Hiki Yoshikazu's rebellion== {{main|Hiki Yoshikazu}} Seriously ill, Yoriie proposed to name both his younger brother, Minamoto no Sanetomo, and his young son [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] to succeed him; the two would split power, governing separate parts of the country. It seemed natural to them that Hiki would then be the regent, even if unofficially, of young Ichiman. Hiki suggested to Yoriie that they arrange to have Sanetomo killed. [[Hōjō Masako]], Yoriie's mother and wife of the first shōgun Yoritomo, allegedly overheard the conversation. On a pretext, [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] invited [[Hiki Yoshikazu]] to his home and assassinated him. A battle between the clans ensued, the Hiki were defeated by a coalition of the Hōjō, [[Wada clan|Wada]], [[Miura clan|Miura]] and [[Hatakeyama]] clans and were exterminated. Yoriie died in Shuzenji, a small town in what was later called [[Izu Province]], assassinated by his grandfather [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. ==The sons of Yoriie== Yoriie had two sons, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]] and [[Kugyō (Minamoto no Yoshinari)|Kugyō]]. Both of his sons would end up dead, like their father they would fall victim to the power struggle that followed Yoritomo's death. Ichiman (1198–1203) was the eldest. His mother, Wakasa no Tsubone, was [[Hiki Yoshikazu]]'s daughter, and the child was brought up by the [[Hiki clan]]. It is said that he died in a fire that destroyed the Hiki residence. Yoriie's second son Yoshinari, his only son to reach adulthood, was forced to become a Buddhist monk. In 1219, Yoshinari murdered his uncle Sanetomo on the stairs leading to the shrine of [[Tsurugaoka Hachimangū]] in the shogunal capital of [[Kamakura]], an act for which he was himself slain on the same day.Kusumoto (2002: 70–73) ==Family== * Father: [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] * Mother: [[Hōjō Masako]] * Wife: Wakasa no Tsubone (d. 1203) * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] ** [[Kugyō (priest)|Kugyō]] ==Eras of Yoriie's ''bakufu''== The years in which Yoriie was shōgun are all within only one [[Japanese era names|era name]] or ''[[nengō]]'': ''[[Kennin]]'' (1201–1204). ==Notes== [[Image:Minamoto no Yoriies grave 2007-02-25.jpg|thumb|right|Minamoto no Yoriie's grave in Shuzenji, [[Izu, Shizuoka|Izu]]]] {{Reflist}} ==References== * {{cite book | last = Kamiya | first = Michinori | title = Fukaku Aruku: Kamakura Shiseki Sansaku Vol. 1 & 2 | publisher = Kamakura Shunshūsha | year = 2008 | location = Kamakura | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-7740-0340-5 | oclc = 169992721 }} * {{cite book | last = Kusumoto | first = Katsuji | title = Kamakura Naruhodo Jiten | publisher = Jitsugyō no Nihonsha | date = July 2002 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-408-00779-3 }} * [[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]] and Käthe Roth. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC ''Japan encyclopedia''.] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/58053128?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 58053128] * [[Edmond Papinot|Papinot, Edmond]]. (1906) ''Dictionnaire d'histoire et de géographie du japon''. Tokyo: Librarie Sansaisha.[http://www.unterstein.net/Toyoashihara-no-Chiaki-Nagaioaki-no-Mitsuho-no-Kuni/NobiliaireJapon.pdf ..Click link for digitized 1906 ''Nobiliaire du japon'' (2003)] * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). ''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''.] Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/5850691 OCLC 5850691]. * {{cite book | editor-last = Yasuda | editor-first = Motohisa | title = Kamakura, Muromachi Jinmei Jiten | publisher = Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha | year = 1990 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-404-01757-4 | oclc = 24654085 }} ==External links== * [http://www.kcn-net.org/e_kama_history/omachi/omachi_1.htm Ōmachi], by the Kamakura Citizen's Net, accessed on September 30, 2008 {{s-start}} {{succession box | title=''[[Shōgun]]'':

{{Short description|Shogun of the Kamakura shogunate (r. 1202–03)}} {{family name hatnote|Minamoto|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox officeholder | name = Minamoto no Yoriie
{{lang|ja|源頼家}} | image = Minamoto no Yoriie.jpg | caption = | office = ''[[Shōgun]]'' | monarch = [[Emperor Tsuchimikado|Tsuchimikado]] | predecessor = [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] | successor = [[Minamoto no Sanetomo]] | birth_date = {{birth date|1182|9|11}} | birth_place = [[Kamakura, Kanagawa]], [[Japan]] | death_date = {{death date and age|1204|8|14|1182|9|11}} | death_place = | spouse = Wakasa no Tsubone | children = {{Plainlist| * [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] * [[Kugyō (priest)|Kugyō]] * Eijitsu * Zengyō * Take no gosho}} | term_start =1202 | term_end = 1203 | father = [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] | mother = [[Hōjō Masako]] | signature = }} {{nihongo|'''Minamoto no Yoriie'''|源 頼家||September 11, 1182 – August 14, 1204|lead=yes}} was the second ''[[shōgun]]'' (1202–1203) of [[Japan]]'s [[Kamakura shogunate]], and the first son of first shōgun [[Minamoto no Yoritomo|Yoritomo]].[[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). "Minamoto no Yoriie" in {{Google books|p2QnPijAEmEC|''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 635|page=635}}. His [[Dharma name]] was Hokke-in-dono Kingo Da'i Zengo (法華院殿金吾大禅閤). == Life == Minamoto no Yoriie was born to [[Hōjō Masako]] at [[Hiki Yoshikazu]]'s residence in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]].Yasuda (1990:592-593) Before he was born, his father Yoritomo had [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] and his men carry stones to build the [[Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū#Wakamiya .C5.8Cji|Dankazura]] on [[Wakamiya Ōji]] to pray for the child's safe delivery. When Yoriie later himself had an heir, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]], the child was also born at the Hiki mansionThe mansion no longer exists and its location ({{coord|35|19|1.31|N|139|33|20.95|E|region:JP_type:landmark_scale:1500|display=inline}}) is occupied by [[Myōhon-ji]] a temple dedicated to the memory of the Hiki clan, exterminated by the [[Hōjō clan]]. to Hiki's daughter Wakasa no Tsubone, a fact which further consolidated an already strong emotional bond.Kamiya Vol. 1 (2008:44-45) From this relationship [[Hiki clan|Hiki]] gained considerable influence when Yoriie became shōgun, incurring the hostility of [[Hōjō Tokimasa]], who was instead close to Yoriie's younger brother Senman (future third shōgun [[Minamoto no Sanetomo|Sanetomo]]), and who was in his turn trying to leverage that relationship for political advantage. His childhood name was Manju (万寿). When he was young, he was interested in military arts like fencing, and horse-riding. After his father's death in 1199, the 17-year-old became head of the [[Minamoto]] clan and was appointed [[Shōgun|''sei-i taishōgun'']] in 1202. He was, however, criticized for his abandonment of his father's policies, and his mother forbade him from any political activity. On June 30, 1203, his remaining powers were formally taken from him and assumed by a council of 13 elders, headed by his grandfather [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. He ordained as a Buddhist monk. Yoriie, in turn, plotted with the Hiki to subjugate the [[Hōjō clan|Hōjō]] clan. However, he failed, and was put under house arrest, where he was forced to abdicate. Then on July 17, 1204, he was assassinated in his mansion in Izu. Yoriie was succeeded by his younger brother Sanetomo, the last of the [[Minamoto]] shogun to rule over [[Kamakura]]. ==Hiki Yoshikazu's rebellion== {{main|Hiki Yoshikazu}} Seriously ill, Yoriie proposed to name both his younger brother, Minamoto no Sanetomo, and his young son [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] to succeed him; the two would split power, governing separate parts of the country. It seemed natural to them that Hiki would then be the regent, even if unofficially, of young Ichiman. Hiki suggested to Yoriie that they arrange to have Sanetomo killed. [[Hōjō Masako]], Yoriie's mother and wife of the first shōgun Yoritomo, allegedly overheard the conversation. On a pretext, [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] invited [[Hiki Yoshikazu]] to his home and assassinated him. A battle between the clans ensued, the Hiki were defeated by a coalition of the Hōjō, [[Wada clan|Wada]], [[Miura clan|Miura]] and [[Hatakeyama]] clans and were exterminated. Yoriie died in Shuzenji, a small town in what was later called [[Izu Province]], assassinated by his grandfather [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. ==The sons of Yoriie== Yoriie had two sons, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]] and [[Kugyō (Minamoto no Yoshinari)|Kugyō]]. Both of his sons would end up dead, like their father they would fall victim to the power struggle that followed Yoritomo's death. Ichiman (1198–1203) was the eldest. His mother, Wakasa no Tsubone, was [[Hiki Yoshikazu]]'s daughter, and the child was brought up by the [[Hiki clan]]. It is said that he died in a fire that destroyed the Hiki residence. Yoriie's second son Yoshinari, his only son to reach adulthood, was forced to become a Buddhist monk. In 1219, Yoshinari murdered his uncle Sanetomo on the stairs leading to the shrine of [[Tsurugaoka Hachimangū]] in the shogunal capital of [[Kamakura]], an act for which he was himself slain on the same day.Kusumoto (2002: 70–73) ==Family== * Father: [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] * Mother: [[Hōjō Masako]] * Wife: Wakasa no Tsubone (d. 1203) * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] ** [[Kugyō (priest)|Kugyō]] ==Eras of Yoriie's ''bakufu''== The years in which Yoriie was shōgun are all within only one [[Japanese era names|era name]] or ''[[nengō]]'': ''[[Kennin]]'' (1201–1204). ==Notes== [[Image:Minamoto no Yoriies grave 2007-02-25.jpg|thumb|right|Minamoto no Yoriie's grave in Shuzenji, [[Izu, Shizuoka|Izu]]]] {{Reflist}} ==References== * {{cite book | last = Kamiya | first = Michinori | title = Fukaku Aruku: Kamakura Shiseki Sansaku Vol. 1 & 2 | publisher = Kamakura Shunshūsha | year = 2008 | location = Kamakura | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-7740-0340-5 | oclc = 169992721 }} * {{cite book | last = Kusumoto | first = Katsuji | title = Kamakura Naruhodo Jiten | publisher = Jitsugyō no Nihonsha | date = July 2002 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-408-00779-3 }} * [[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]] and Käthe Roth. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC ''Japan encyclopedia''.] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/58053128?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 58053128] * [[Edmond Papinot|Papinot, Edmond]]. (1906) ''Dictionnaire d'histoire et de géographie du japon''. Tokyo: Librarie Sansaisha.[http://www.unterstein.net/Toyoashihara-no-Chiaki-Nagaioaki-no-Mitsuho-no-Kuni/NobiliaireJapon.pdf ..Click link for digitized 1906 ''Nobiliaire du japon'' (2003)] * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). ''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''.] Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/5850691 OCLC 5850691]. * {{cite book | editor-last = Yasuda | editor-first = Motohisa | title = Kamakura, Muromachi Jinmei Jiten | publisher = Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha | year = 1990 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-404-01757-4 | oclc = 24654085 }} ==External links== * [http://www.kcn-net.org/e_kama_history/omachi/omachi_1.htm Ōmachi], by the Kamakura Citizen's Net, accessed on September 30, 2008 {{s-start}} {{succession box | title=''[[Shōgun]]'':
Minamoto no Yoriie | before=[[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] | after=[[Minamoto no Sanetomo]] | years=1202–1203}} {{s-end}} {{Shoguns|state=collapsed}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:11 2]] [[Category:1182 births]] [[Category:1204 deaths]] [[Category:12th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:13th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:13th-century shōguns]] [[Category:Kamakura shōguns]] [[Category:Minamoto clan]] [[Category:People of Heian-period Japan]] [[Category:People of Kamakura-period Japan]] [[Category:Kamakura period Buddhist clergy]] [[Category:Assassinated Japanese people]] }} [[sn:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[en:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[en:Minamoto no Yoriie[[en:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

Minamoto no Yoriie | before=[[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] | after=[[Minamoto no Sanetomo]] | years=1202–1203}} {{s-end}} {{Shoguns|state=collapsed}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:11 2]] [[Category:1182 births]] [[Category:1204 deaths]] [[Category:12th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:13th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:13th-century shōguns]] [[Category:Kamakura shōguns]] [[Category:Minamoto clan]] [[Category:People of Heian-period Japan]] [[Category:People of Kamakura-period Japan]] [[Category:Kamakura period Buddhist clergy]] [[Category:Assassinated Japanese people]] }} [[sn:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[en:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[en:Minamoto no Yoriie[[en:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[be:Мінамота-но Ёрыіэ]] [[ca:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[de:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[es:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[eu:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[fa:میناموتو نو یوری‌ایه]] [[fr:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[it:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[ja:源頼家]] [[ko:미나모토노 요리이에]] [[nl:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[pl:Yoriie Minamoto]] [[pt:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[ru:Минамото-но Ёрииэ]] [[sv:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[th:มินาโมโตะ โนะ โยริอิเอะ]] [[tr:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[uk:Мінамото но Йоріїе]] [[vi:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[zh:源賴家]] [[zh_min_nan:Minamoto no Yoriie]]

{{Short description|Shogun of the Kamakura shogunate (r. 1202–03)}} {{family name hatnote|Minamoto|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox officeholder | name = Minamoto no Yoriie
{{lang|ja|源頼家}} | image = Minamoto no Yoriie.jpg | caption = | office = ''[[Shōgun]]'' | monarch = [[Emperor Tsuchimikado|Tsuchimikado]] | predecessor = [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] | successor = [[Minamoto no Sanetomo]] | birth_date = {{birth date|1182|9|11}} | birth_place = [[Kamakura, Kanagawa]], [[Japan]] | death_date = {{death date and age|1204|8|14|1182|9|11}} | death_place = | spouse = Wakasa no Tsubone | children = {{Plainlist| * [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] * [[Kugyō (priest)|Kugyō]] * Eijitsu * Zengyō * Take no gosho}} | term_start =1202 | term_end = 1203 | father = [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] | mother = [[Hōjō Masako]] | signature = }} {{nihongo|'''Minamoto no Yoriie'''|源 頼家||September 11, 1182 – August 14, 1204|lead=yes}} was the second ''[[shōgun]]'' (1202–1203) of [[Japan]]'s [[Kamakura shogunate]], and the first son of first shōgun [[Minamoto no Yoritomo|Yoritomo]].[[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). "Minamoto no Yoriie" in {{Google books|p2QnPijAEmEC|''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 635|page=635}}. His [[Dharma name]] was Hokke-in-dono Kingo Da'i Zengo (法華院殿金吾大禅閤). == Life == Minamoto no Yoriie was born to [[Hōjō Masako]] at [[Hiki Yoshikazu]]'s residence in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]].Yasuda (1990:592-593) Before he was born, his father Yoritomo had [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] and his men carry stones to build the [[Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū#Wakamiya .C5.8Cji|Dankazura]] on [[Wakamiya Ōji]] to pray for the child's safe delivery. When Yoriie later himself had an heir, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]], the child was also born at the Hiki mansionThe mansion no longer exists and its location ({{coord|35|19|1.31|N|139|33|20.95|E|region:JP_type:landmark_scale:1500|display=inline}}) is occupied by [[Myōhon-ji]] a temple dedicated to the memory of the Hiki clan, exterminated by the [[Hōjō clan]]. to Hiki's daughter Wakasa no Tsubone, a fact which further consolidated an already strong emotional bond.Kamiya Vol. 1 (2008:44-45) From this relationship [[Hiki clan|Hiki]] gained considerable influence when Yoriie became shōgun, incurring the hostility of [[Hōjō Tokimasa]], who was instead close to Yoriie's younger brother Senman (future third shōgun [[Minamoto no Sanetomo|Sanetomo]]), and who was in his turn trying to leverage that relationship for political advantage. His childhood name was Manju (万寿). When he was young, he was interested in military arts like fencing, and horse-riding. After his father's death in 1199, the 17-year-old became head of the [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]] clan and was appointed [[Shōgun|''sei-i taishōgun'']] in 1202. He was, however, criticized for his abandonment of his father's policies, and his mother forbade him from any political activity. On June 30, 1203, his remaining powers were formally taken from him and assumed by a council of 13 elders, headed by his grandfather [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. He ordained as a Buddhist monk. Yoriie, in turn, plotted with the Hiki to subjugate the [[Hōjō clan|Hōjō]] clan. However, he failed, and was put under house arrest, where he was forced to abdicate. Then on July 17, 1204, he was assassinated in his mansion in Izu. Yoriie was succeeded by his younger brother Sanetomo, the last of the [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]] shogun to rule over [[Kamakura]]. ==Hiki Yoshikazu's rebellion== {{main|Hiki Yoshikazu}} Seriously ill, Yoriie proposed to name both his younger brother, Minamoto no Sanetomo, and his young son [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] to succeed him; the two would split power, governing separate parts of the country. It seemed natural to them that Hiki would then be the regent, even if unofficially, of young Ichiman. Hiki suggested to Yoriie that they arrange to have Sanetomo killed. [[Hōjō Masako]], Yoriie's mother and wife of the first shōgun Yoritomo, allegedly overheard the conversation. On a pretext, [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] invited [[Hiki Yoshikazu]] to his home and assassinated him. A battle between the clans ensued, the Hiki were defeated by a coalition of the Hōjō, [[Wada clan|Wada]], [[Miura clan|Miura]] and [[Hatakeyama]] clans and were exterminated. Yoriie died in Shuzenji, a small town in what was later called [[Izu Province]], assassinated by his grandfather [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. ==The sons of Yoriie== Yoriie had two sons, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]] and [[Kugyō (Minamoto no Yoshinari)|Kugyō]]. Both of his sons would end up dead, like their father they would fall victim to the power struggle that followed Yoritomo's death. Ichiman (1198–1203) was the eldest. His mother, Wakasa no Tsubone, was [[Hiki Yoshikazu]]'s daughter, and the child was brought up by the [[Hiki clan]]. It is said that he died in a fire that destroyed the Hiki residence. Yoriie's second son Yoshinari, his only son to reach adulthood, was forced to become a Buddhist monk. In 1219, Yoshinari murdered his uncle Sanetomo on the stairs leading to the shrine of [[Tsurugaoka Hachimangū]] in the shogunal capital of [[Kamakura]], an act for which he was himself slain on the same day.Kusumoto (2002: 70–73) ==Family== * Father: [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] * Mother: [[Hōjō Masako]] * Wife: Wakasa no Tsubone (d. 1203) * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] ** [[Kugyō (priest)|Kugyō]] ==Eras of Yoriie's ''bakufu''== The years in which Yoriie was shōgun are all within only one [[Japanese era names|era name]] or ''[[nengō]]'': ''[[Kennin]]'' (1201–1204). ==Notes== [[Image:Minamoto no Yoriies grave 2007-02-25.jpg|thumb|right|Minamoto no Yoriie's grave in Shuzenji, [[Izu, Shizuoka|Izu]]]] {{Reflist}} ==References== * {{cite book | last = Kamiya | first = Michinori | title = Fukaku Aruku: Kamakura Shiseki Sansaku Vol. 1 & 2 | publisher = Kamakura Shunshūsha | year = 2008 | location = Kamakura | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-7740-0340-5 | oclc = 169992721 }} * {{cite book | last = Kusumoto | first = Katsuji | title = Kamakura Naruhodo Jiten | publisher = Jitsugyō no Nihonsha | date = July 2002 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-408-00779-3 }} * [[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]] and Käthe Roth. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC ''Japan encyclopedia''.] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/58053128?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 58053128] * [[Edmond Papinot|Papinot, Edmond]]. (1906) ''Dictionnaire d'histoire et de géographie du japon''. Tokyo: Librarie Sansaisha.[http://www.unterstein.net/Toyoashihara-no-Chiaki-Nagaioaki-no-Mitsuho-no-Kuni/NobiliaireJapon.pdf ..Click link for digitized 1906 ''Nobiliaire du japon'' (2003)] * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). ''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''.] Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/5850691 OCLC 5850691]. * {{cite book | editor-last = Yasuda | editor-first = Motohisa | title = Kamakura, Muromachi Jinmei Jiten | publisher = Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha | year = 1990 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-404-01757-4 | oclc = 24654085 }} ==External links== * [http://www.kcn-net.org/e_kama_history/omachi/omachi_1.htm Ōmachi], by the Kamakura Citizen's Net, accessed on September 30, 2008 {{s-start}} {{succession box | title=''[[Shōgun]]'':

[[Hōjō Masako]], Yoriie's mother and wife of the first shōgun Yoritomo, allegedly overheard the conversation. On a pretext, [[Hōjō Tokimasa]] invited [[Hiki Yoshikazu]] to his home and assassinated him. A battle between the clans ensued, the Hiki were defeated by a coalition of the Hōjō, [[Wada clan|Wada]], [[Miura clan|Miura]] and [[Hatakeyama]] clans and were exterminated. Yoriie died in Shuzenji, a small town in what was later called [[Izu Province]], assassinated by his grandfather [[Hōjō Tokimasa]]. ==The sons of Yoriie== Yoriie had two sons, [[Minamoto no Ichiman|Ichiman]] and [[Kugyō (Minamoto no Yoshinari)|Kugyō]]. Both of his sons would end up dead, like their father they would fall victim to the power struggle that followed Yoritomo's death. Ichiman (1198–1203) was the eldest. His mother, Wakasa no Tsubone, was [[Hiki Yoshikazu]]'s daughter, and the child was brought up by the [[Hiki clan]]. It is said that he died in a fire that destroyed the Hiki residence. Yoriie's second son Yoshinari, his only son to reach adulthood, was forced to become a Buddhist monk. In 1219, Yoshinari murdered his uncle Sanetomo on the stairs leading to the shrine of [[Tsurugaoka Hachimangū]] in the shogunal capital of [[Kamakura]], an act for which he was himself slain on the same day.Kusumoto (2002: 70–73) ==Family== * Father: [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] * Mother: [[Hōjō Masako]] * Wife: Wakasa no Tsubone (d. 1203) * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Ichiman]] ** [[Kugyō (priest)|Kugyō]] ==Eras of Yoriie's ''bakufu''== The years in which Yoriie was shōgun are all within only one [[Japanese era names|era name]] or ''[[nengō]]'': ''[[Kennin]]'' (1201–1204). ==Notes== [[Image:Minamoto no Yoriies grave 2007-02-25.jpg|thumb|right|Minamoto no Yoriie's grave in Shuzenji, [[Izu, Shizuoka|Izu]]]] {{Reflist}} ==References== * {{cite book | last = Kamiya | first = Michinori | title = Fukaku Aruku: Kamakura Shiseki Sansaku Vol. 1 & 2 | publisher = Kamakura Shunshūsha | year = 2008 | location = Kamakura | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-7740-0340-5 | oclc = 169992721 }} * {{cite book | last = Kusumoto | first = Katsuji | title = Kamakura Naruhodo Jiten | publisher = Jitsugyō no Nihonsha | date = July 2002 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-408-00779-3 }} * [[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]] and Käthe Roth. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC ''Japan encyclopedia''.] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/58053128?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 58053128] * [[Edmond Papinot|Papinot, Edmond]]. (1906) ''Dictionnaire d'histoire et de géographie du japon''. Tokyo: Librarie Sansaisha.[http://www.unterstein.net/Toyoashihara-no-Chiaki-Nagaioaki-no-Mitsuho-no-Kuni/NobiliaireJapon.pdf ..Click link for digitized 1906 ''Nobiliaire du japon'' (2003)] * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac]]. (1834). ''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''.] Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/5850691 OCLC 5850691]. * {{cite book | editor-last = Yasuda | editor-first = Motohisa | title = Kamakura, Muromachi Jinmei Jiten | publisher = Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha | year = 1990 | location = Tokyo | language = Japanese | isbn = 978-4-404-01757-4 | oclc = 24654085 }} ==External links== * [http://www.kcn-net.org/e_kama_history/omachi/omachi_1.htm Ōmachi], by the Kamakura Citizen's Net, accessed on September 30, 2008 {{s-start}} {{succession box | title=''[[Shōgun]]'':
Minamoto no Yoriie | before=[[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] | after=[[Minamoto no Sanetomo]] | years=1202–1203}} {{s-end}} {{Shoguns|state=collapsed}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:11 2]] [[Category:1182 births]] [[Category:1204 deaths]] [[Category:12th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:13th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:13th-century shōguns]] [[Category:Kamakura shōguns]] [[Category:Minamoto clan]] [[Category:People of Heian-period Japan]] [[Category:People of Kamakura-period Japan]] [[Category:Kamakura period Buddhist clergy]] [[Category:Assassinated Japanese people]] }} [[sn:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[en:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[en:Minamoto no Yoriie]][[en:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[be:Мінамота-но Ёрыіэ]] [[ca:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[de:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[es:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[eu:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[fa:میناموتو نو یوری‌ایه]] [[fr:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[it:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[ja:源頼家]] [[ko:미나모토노 요리이에]] [[nl:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[pl:Yoriie Minamoto]] [[pt:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[ru:Минамото-но Ёрииэ]] [[sv:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[th:มินาโมโตะ โนะ โยริอิเอะ]] [[tr:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[uk:Мінамото но Йоріїе]] [[vi:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[zh:源賴家]] [[zh-min-nan:Minamoto no Yoriie]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]]
